analytic.title: Advanced Techniques for Plant Disease Detection

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: He did his post-graduation and Ph.D. in Agricultural Entomology from A.N.D.U.A. & T., Kumarganj, Ayodhya (U.P.). He has qualified ICAR-NET and received different awards from different professional societies. He has over 12 years of experience in the field of Entomology in various capacities. He has published 28 research papers in the reputed scientific journals, 08 popular articles, 2 book chapters and 1 book.

div.head: Mukesh Kumar Mishra
div.p: Arun Kumar Saurabh Kumar

div.head: NIPA ® GENX ELECTRONIC RESOURCES & SOLUTIONS P. LTD.
div.p: New Delhi-110 034

div.head: Preface
div.p: The book "Recent Trends in Plant Protection" is written with the comprehensive information on modern and highly profitable techniques for pest management including insect-pest and diseases. The content of this book are presented in simple language and easy to understand, which not only arouses students' interest but also simplifies the comprehension process. The chapters of this book provides an overview of the development of modern pest management approaches viz., plant diseases detection techniques, insect trapping, bio inputs and bio pesticides (importance of predatory insects including its conservation techniques, important entomophagous pathogens and their mass multiplication), impact of agrochemicals, new classifications of pesticides and pesticide spraying equipment, plant vectors, regulatory and molecular approaches and non-insect-pest management. A chapter of this book also being discussed to plant and natural enemies' relationship. A part of this topic also covers the chapters on edible and commercial importance insects like honey bee, silk worm and lac insect. It will be great help to students and teachers in the field of agriculture particularly Crop Protection throughout. The purpose of this book is to bridge the gap between research and practice, showcasing innovative solutions and promoting interdisciplinary collaboration. It provides an avenue for sharing experiences, discussing challenges, and exploring new avenues in plant protection research and implementation. We would like to express our gratitude to all the authors who contributed their expertise and valuable insights to this book. Their dedication and commitment to advancing the field of plant protection have made this compilation possible. We hope that this book will serve as a valuable resource and inspire further research and innovations in the field of plant protection. Together, we can work towards sustainable agriculture, enhanced crop protection, and a more food-secure future.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Plant disease detection is a crucial aspect of modern agriculture, as it allows early identification and management of diseases, minimizing crop losses and maximizing yields. In recent years, advancements in technology have led to the development of more sophisticated techniques for plant disease detection. Point-of-care (POC) diagnostic assay for plant disease detection is a rapid, advanced, and on-site testing method designed to identify the presence of pathogens or diseases in plants. This type of diagnostic tool is particularly valuable for farmers, agronomists, and researchers who need immediate results to make timely decisions about disease management and crop protection strategies. POC assays aim to provide accurate and reliable results quickly, without the need for specialized equipment or laboratory facilities. POC diagnostic assay can be performed swiftly and cheaply in the field as it does not require sophisticated lab equipment (Yager et al�, 2006). PCR-based techniques are superior to other technologies in many ways, but they are significantly less suitable for POC applications because they need power for performing the temperature alterations necessary for DNA amplification. Isothermal DNA amplification techniques are a suitable alternative to overcome this constraint (Gill and Ghaemi, 2008;Craw and Balachandran, 2012). For POC detection of pathogen DNA, isothermal amplification in conjunction with lateral flow strips and portable fluorometers has been utilised successfully (Notomi et al�, 2000;Vincent et al�, 2004;Piepenburg et al�, 2006;Chow et al�, 2008;Lutz et al�, 2010;Mahalanabis et al�, 2010;Rohrman and Richards-Kortum, 2012). Fluorometers are quite expensive and are not best suited for use in fields having adverse weather conditions. Nucleic acid-based techniques have revolutionized the field of plant disease detection due to their high specificity and sensitivity. These techniques rely on the detection and analysis of specific nucleic acids, such as DNA and RNA, to identify pathogens responsible for plant diseases. These nucleic acid-based techniques provide accurate and reliable results, enabling early detection of plant diseases, prompt management decisions, and preventing potential crop losses due to pathogen infections. They have become indispensable tools for plant pathologists and plant health management programs worldwide. Some of the most used nucleic acid-based techniques for plant disease detection include: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR): PCR is a well-established technique that amplifies specific DNA sequences. It requires primers designed to target unique regions of the pathogen's genome. PCR can detect pathogens even at low concentrations and is widely used for various plant pathogens. The PCR reaction is set up, which includes the extracted DNA/RNA, the designed primers, a DNA polymerase enzyme, and a mixture of nucleotides (dNTPs).
The PCR machine goes through cycles of heating and cooling to facilitate the amplification of the target DNA or RNA sequence.

• Denaturation: The reaction mixture is heated to a high temperature (around 94-98°C), which causes the double-stranded DNA to separate into single strands. • Annealing: The reaction is then cooled to a lower temperature (around 50-65°C), allowing the primers to bind to their complementary sequences on the target DNA or RNA. • Extension: The reaction is heated to a moderate temperature (around 72°C), and the DNA polymerase synthesizes a new DNA strand that complements the target DNA or RNA, starting from the primers. • Amplification Detection: The amplification of the target sequence occurs exponentially during the PCR cycles. If the target pathogen's DNA or RNA is present in the sample, the amount of the amplified product increases significantly with each cycle. • Gel Electrophoresis: After the PCR is completed, the amplified products are separated and visualized using gel electrophoresis. The PCR products are loaded into a gel, and an electric field is applied to separate the DNA fragments based on their size. A DNA ladder is also run on the gel to determine the size of the amplified products. If the pathogen's DNA or RNA was present in the sample, the corresponding amplified band will be visible on the gel. • Result Interpretation: The presence or absence of the specific amplified band indicates the presence or absence of the target pathogen in the plant sample.

div.head: Reverse Transcription PCR (RT-PCR):
div.p: RT-PCR is used to detect RNA-based pathogens (e.g., RNA viruses) by first converting RNA into complementary DNA (cDNA) using reverse transcriptase enzyme. The resulting cDNA is then amplified using PCR.

div.head: Real-Time PCR (qPCR):
div.p: Real-time PCR is a variation of PCR that allows quantification of the pathogen's DNA or RNA during the amplification process.
It provides information about the initial amount of pathogen present in the sample, making it useful for quantification and monitoring disease progression.
Steps of qPCR are as follows:

• Sample Collection and Preparation: The first step is to collect plant samples suspected of being infected by a pathogen. These samples could be leaves, stems, fruits, or any other plant part showing disease symptoms.

Proper sample preparation is essential to ensure the quality of extracted genetic material. • RNA Extraction: qRT-PCR detects RNA, so the genetic material needs to be extracted from the collected plant samples. RNA extraction methods may vary depending on the specific plant species and pathogen involved.

Commercial RNA extraction kits are commonly used for this purpose. • cDNA Synthesis: The extracted RNA is then reverse transcribed into complementary DNA (cDNA) using a reverse transcriptase enzyme. This step converts the RNA template into DNA, which will serve as a template for the subsequent PCR amplification. • Primer and Probe Design: Specific primers and a probe (also known as a

TaqMan probe) are designed based on the unique genetic sequences of the target pathogen. These primers and the probe are essential for selectively amplifying and detecting the pathogen's DNA during the qRT-PCR process. • qRT-PCR Setup: The qRT-PCR reaction mixture is prepared, containing the cDNA template, primers, probe, and the qPCR master mix, which includes the DNA polymerase and fluorescent dyes. The reaction mixture is dispensed into individual wells of a qRT-PCR plate. • Amplification and Detection: The qRT-PCR instrument cycles through a series of temperature changes, repeatedly amplifying the target DNA and allowing the fluorescence signal to be detected in real-time. The instrument measures the fluorescence emitted by the dye-bound probes at each cycle, and this fluorescence is proportional to the amount of amplified DNA. The data collected during this step is used to generate a quantification cycle (Ct) value. • Data Analysis: The Ct values obtained during qRT-PCR are used to determine the quantity of the target pathogen in the original sample.
Comparing the Ct values to a standard curve generated from known concentrations of the target pathogen can help estimate the pathogen load in the sample.

div.head: Loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP)
div.p: LAMP is a molecular method for nucleic acid amplification that is highly selective as it utilises an array of four different primers (forward and reverse and external and internal) that bind to six (or eight) distinct regions on the target gene (Notomi et al�, 2000). Under isothermal conditions, LAMP reactions can be carried out easily by utilising Bst DNA polymerase which is known to have high-displacement activity (Chen et al�, 2015;Notomi et al�, 2000). Unlike PCR, LAMP can be performed at a steady temperature, with a short reaction time. Amplification can be visually detected or quantified using the turbidity or colour of the reaction mixture (Mori et al�, 2001(Mori et al�, , 2004;;Iwamoto et al� 2003;Goto et al�, 2009).

There are various types of LAMP assays conventional LAMP, multiplex LAMP, in-disc LAMP, electric LAMP and reverse-transcription LAMP (Wong et al�, 2017). LAMP products have a distinctive ladder-like pattern, due to the variable number of inverted repeats (Tomlinson and Boonham, 2008). LAMP is not applicable for cloning purposes (Sahoo et al�, 2016), complicated primer design (Lau and Botella, 2017). LAMP is used to detect plant viruses, oomycetes fungi, and plant-parasitic nematodes. The process involves the use of several primers and a DNA polymerase with strand displacement activity.

div.head: Materials Required
div.p: • DNA sample containing the target sequence • LAMP primers (inner primers, outer primers, and loop primers)

• Bst DNA polymerase or a similar strand displacement DNA polymerase • Reaction buffer containing Mg 2+ and other necessary components for the enzyme • dNTPs (deoxynucleotide triphosphates) • Incubator or heat block set to a constant temperature (typically 60-65°C) Procedure Design Primers: Design specific LAMP primers that target the region of interest in the DNA sequence. LAMP primers consist of six sequences (F1, F2, B1, B2, LoopF, and LoopB), and their design requires a bioinformatics tool or software to ensure specificity. Prepare LAMP Reaction Mix:In a sterile microcentrifuge tube, prepare the LAMP reaction mix by combining the following components: a) DNA sample (containing the target sequence) or a template control (water if no DNA is to be used).
b) Forward inner primer (FIP) and backward inner primer (BIP) at a final concentration of around 1-2 µM each.
c) Forward outer primer (F3) and backward outer primer (B3) at a final concentration of around 0.2-0.4 µM each.
d) Forward loop primer (LF) and backward loop primer (LB) at a final concentration of around 0.2-0.4 µM each.
e) dNTPs at a final concentration of around 0.2-0.8 mM each.
f) Bst DNA polymerase or similar strand displacement DNA polymerase at the recommended concentration.
g) Reaction buffer containing Mg 2+ at the appropriate concentration for the enzyme.

Mix and prepare reaction tubes:Thoroughly mix the reaction components by pipetting or vortexing.Divide the reaction mix into separate tubes according to the number of reactions needed (e.g., samples, controls, and standards).

Incubate at Constant Temperature: Place the reaction tubes in an incubator or a heat block set to a constant temperature of around 60-65°C (depending on the specific DNA polymerase used) for typically 1 to 2 hours. Visualize Results (Optional): After the incubation period, you can visualize the LAMP results by using various methods such as gel electrophoresis, colorimetric indicators, or turbidity-based methods. These methods can help determine the presence or absence of the target DNA. Data Analysis: Analyse the LAMP results, interpreting the presence or absence of the amplified DNA and comparing it to positive and negative controls.

div.head: Recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA)
div.p: RPA is another isothermal DNA amplification and detection technique that requires the use of primers, nucleotides, DNA recombinase, a strand displacement polymerase enzyme and single-stranded DNA binding proteins. RPA depends on an enzymatic activity to separate the dsDNA in order to facilitate primer binding to the target sequences, hence it does not require an initial denaturation step to denature the target DNA (Piepenburg et al�, 2006;Labato and O' Sullivan, 2018).

div.head: Principle of RPA
div.p: RPA is based on the use of recombinase enzymes that facilitate the binding of primers to their target sequences on the DNA or RNA template. The recombinase creates a DNA strand displacement, allowing the primers to initiate the amplification process. The reaction occurs at a constant temperature (usually between 37-42°C), eliminating the need for a thermal cycler.

Steps 1. The recombinase proteins bind to the primers, a complex is formed that searches the DNA template for the complementary (target) sequence. 2. Once a match is identified, the recombinase-primer complex adheres to the complementary sequence by strand invasion (37-42°C), which results in the complementary strand displacement. 3. The complex will be stabilised by the ssDNA binding proteins in order to avoid the displaced strand from re-hybridizing with the template DNA. 4. In the presence of dNTPs, the strand displacement polymerase extends (37-42°C) the hybridised primer. The same process takes place in the reverse direction and the target DNA is exponentially amplified. Detection is done either by a lateral flow device or through gel electrophoresis.

Advantages of RPA for plant disease detection Speed: RPA can produce results in as little as 10-30 minutes, making it a rapid diagnostic tool for plant disease detection. Sensitivity: RPA is highly sensitive and can detect low amounts of target DNA or RNA, even in complex plant samples.

Simplicity: The isothermal nature of RPA and the minimal equipment requirements make it suitable for use in resource-limited settings and field conditions. Specificity: RPA primers can be designed to target specific pathogen sequences, ensuring high specificity for the detection of particular plant pathogens.

It is one of the fastest methods for nucleic acid amplification, the process typically completes in 20 minutes. RPA can be paired with a reverse transcription to target RNA templates for the purpose of RNA virus identification. The limitation of RPA is primer mismatching in case of similar DNA sequences which ultimately leads to false positive results (Babul et al�, 2018). RT-RPA has been used to detect plant viruses such as little cherry virus (Mekuria et al�, 2014) and plum pox virus (Zhang et al�, 2014).

div.head: HDA (Helicase Dependent Amplification)
div.p: One of the simplest techniques for isothermal nucleic acid amplification, HDA mimics the in vivo process of DNA replication by isothermally unwinding DNA duplexes rather than heating them as in the case of PCR. HDA technique was developed by New England Biolabs (Vincent et al�, 2004). Although this isothermal technique is quite similar to a traditional PCR, it does not rely on heat denaturation for separating the double-stranded DNA and it enables primers to anneal to their complementary target sequences. For primer annealing and primer extension under isothermal conditions, HDA generates singlestranded DNA via DNA helicase. Two additional proteins, MutL and a singlestranded DNA-binding protein (SSB), are needed in addition to DNA helicase to unwind double-stranded DNA in order to stabilise single-stranded DNA and prevent the re-hybridization of the complementary strands. Two oligonucleotide primers hybridise to the 5′ and 3′ borders of the target sequence shortly after DNA has been isothermally unwound and stabilised. A DNA polymerase then extends the annealed primers by adding deoxynucleotide-triphosphates (dNTPs), resulting in double-stranded amplification products.

The dNTPs mixture should consist of Mg 2+ which is a cofactor for both polymerase and helicase while dNTP is a cofactor of helicase. The coordination of helicase and polymerase activity is crucial. A thermostable UvrD helicase (Tte-UvrD) from Thermoanaerobacter tengcongensis that can be amplified at a higher temperature has been discovered, which represents an important breakthrough. HDA may be carried out at a higher temperature because to the use of Tte-UvrD, which also eliminates the requirement for SSB and MutL by reducing the re-annealing of single-stranded DNA.HDA is used to detect tobacco mosaic virus, citrus leprosis virus and bacteria, Staphylococcus and Streptococcus. HDA relies on a helicase enzyme that unwinds the doublestranded DNA, creating single-stranded templates for primer binding. A DNA polymerase then synthesizes new strands, leading to exponential amplification of the target DNA.

Steps involved in HDA for plant disease detection 1. Sample collection and preparation: Collect a plant sample suspected of harbouring the pathogen. Prepare the sample to extract the nucleic acids, which may include DNA or RNA, depending on the pathogen type. 2. Primer design: Design specific primers that recognize conserved regions of the pathogen's nucleic acid sequence. These primers will be used to initiate the amplification process. 3. HDA reaction setup: Mix the extracted nucleic acids with the designed primers, a DNA helicase enzyme, and a DNA polymerase with strand displacement activity. Additionally, include reaction buffer and necessary co-factors. 4. Isothermal amplification: The reaction is incubated at a constant temperature, typically between 60°C to 65°C, which allows the helicase enzyme to unwind the DNA and the DNA polymerase to amplify the target cleavage can be exploited to detect the presence of the target sequence.

Fluorescence-based reporters or lateral flow assays are commonly used to visualize the cleavage event. Both CRISPR-Cas9 and CRISPR-Cas12-based assays can be combined with different signal amplification strategies to increase sensitivity. For example, the use of isothermal amplification techniques like recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) or loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) can enhance the detection limit of the assay.
The workflow of a typical CRISPR-Cas-based assay for plant pathogen detection involves the following steps:
• Sample collection: Obtain a sample from the plant suspected of being infected with the pathogen.
• Nucleic acid extraction: Isolate and purify the DNA or RNA from the sample. This step is crucial for obtaining high-quality genetic material for subsequent analyses.
• Amplification (optional): If the target pathogen's genetic material is present in low abundance, a nucleic acid amplification step might be necessary to increase the detection sensitivity.
• CRISPR-Cas detection: Combine the extracted nucleic acids with the CRISPR-Cas components (Cas protein and guide RNA) that specifically target the pathogen's DNA or RNA.

• Signal readout: Analyse the reaction output using fluorescence-based methods, lateral flow assays, or other detection methods. The presence of a specific signal indicates the presence of the target pathogen.

div.head: Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS):
div.p: NGS technologies enable the sequencing of millions of DNA or RNA fragments in parallel. They can be used for metagenomic analyses of plant samples to identify multiple pathogens simultaneously. Steps for NGS are as follows:

• Sample Collection: The first step involves obtaining a biological sample from the patient or environment suspected of harbouring the pathogen. This could be blood, tissue, swabs, or other relevant specimens.
• Nucleic Acid Extraction: NGS requires high-quality DNA or RNA, so the next step is to isolate and extract the genetic material (DNA or RNA) from the collected samples.

• Library Preparation: In this step, the isolated genetic material is converted into a sequencing library. The library contains small fragments of the DNA or RNA, which are tagged with unique molecular identifiers, making it easier to identify and quantify the different sequences during data analysis.

• Sequencing: The prepared library is then loaded onto the NGS platform for sequencing. This process generates vast amounts of short DNA sequences (reads) from the genetic material in the library. • Data Analysis: The raw sequencing data is processed and analysed to identify the presence of pathogens. Bioinformatics tools are used to align the short reads to reference genomes or perform de novo assembly to reconstruct the pathogen's genome. The data is then compared to databases of known pathogens to determine which organisms are present in the sample. • Pathogen Identification: The final step involves the identification of pathogens based on the genomic data obtained. This could include bacteria, viruses, fungi, or other infectious agents. The information obtained from NGS can also reveal important characteristics of the pathogens, such as drug resistance mutations and virulence factors.

div.head: Advantages of NGS for pathogen detection
div.p: • Comprehensive: NGS can simultaneously detect multiple pathogens in a single sample, making it a valuable tool for detecting co-infections and mixed infections. • Sensitivity: NGS can detect low-abundance pathogens, which might be challenging with traditional diagnostic methods. • Speed: NGS enables rapid detection and identification of pathogens, reducing the time required for diagnosis. • Surveillance and Outbreak Investigation: NGS allows for real-time monitoring of pathogen evolution and spread, aiding in the investigation of outbreaks and transmission dynamics. • Personalized Medicine: NGS can provide insights into an individual's pathogen profile, enabling tailored treatment strategies based on the detected pathogens' genetic makeup. Remote Sensing and Drones: Remote sensing using satellite imagery, drones, or unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) allows for large-scale monitoring of crops. Hyperspectral sensors on drones can detect subtle changes in plant reflectance, indicating stress or disease presence. The data collected can be analysed using machine learning algorithms to identify disease patterns. Key techniques used in remote sensing for plant disease detection are: a) Multispectral and Hyperspectral Imaging: Multispectral and hyperspectral sensors capture data in multiple narrow or contiguous bands across the electromagnetic spectrum. This allows for the detection of subtle variations in plant health, including early signs of disease. Hyperspectral imaging provides even more detailed spectral information, enhancing disease identification accuracy. b) Thermal Imaging: Thermal cameras detect variations in plant temperature, which can indicate stress caused by diseases. Infected plants often exhibit different thermal profiles compared to healthy ones, enabling the identification of diseased areas. c) LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging): LiDAR sensors use laser pulses to measure the distance between the sensor and the target surface, creating high-resolution 3D models of the vegetation. This technology can be used to monitor changes in plant structure and health, which may be indicative of disease presence. d) Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs or Drones): Drones equipped with remote sensing instruments can fly over fields and capture high-resolution images and data, allowing for rapid and frequent monitoring of large areas. They provide valuable information on disease spread patterns and can target specific locations for disease control measures. e) Satellite Remote Sensing: Satellites equipped with various sensors can monitor vast agricultural areas from space. Satellite imagery allows for frequent and consistent monitoring, which is especially useful for largescale disease outbreak detection and tracking. f) Machine Learning and Image Analysis: The collected remote sensing data is processed using advanced image analysis techniques and machine learning algorithms. These algorithms can learn to recognize patterns associated with diseased plants and classify them accurately, leading to automated disease detection and mapping. g) Spectral Indices: Spectral indices, such as the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) and the Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI), are derived from remote sensing data and can indicate plant health and stress, which may be linked to disease presence.

div.head: Benefits of Remote Sensing for Plant Disease Detection
div.p: • Early Detection: Remote sensing allows for the early detection of diseases before they become visually apparent. This early warning can help implement timely interventions and minimize crop losses. • Precision Management: Remote sensing data can be used to create detailed disease maps, enabling farmers to target specific areas for treatment rather than applying treatments to entire fields. • Cost-Effective: By identifying and targeting diseased areas, farmers can reduce the unnecessary use of pesticides and other inputs, leading to cost savings and reduced environmental impact.

• Large-Scale Monitoring: Remote sensing covers vast areas, making it efficient for monitoring large agricultural regions, including remote and inaccessible locations. • Real-Time Monitoring: With advances in technology, remote sensing systems can provide real-time or near-real-time data, enabling prompt responses to disease outbreaks. • Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence: Machine learning algorithms, particularly deep learning techniques, have proven effective in analysing complex data sets, such as images and spectral data, to identify patterns associated with diseased plants. Models like convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can be trained on vast datasets to achieve high accuracy in disease detection.

div.head: Nanotechnology
div.p: Nanotechnology has shown great promise in various fields, including agriculture. When it comes to plant disease detection, nanotechnology offers innovative solutions that can improve accuracy, sensitivity, and real-time monitoring. Here are some ways nanotechnology can be used for plant disease detection: Nanosensors: Nanosensors are tiny devices capable of detecting specific biological or chemical substances. In plant disease detection, nanosensors can be designed to identify disease-causing pathogens or the biochemical markers produced by infected plants. They can be integrated into plant tissues, soil, or even carried by drones to scan large agricultural areas. Nanosensors enable rapid and precise detection, allowing farmers to respond promptly to disease outbreaks.

Nanomaterial-based assays: Nanomaterials, such as quantum dots, gold nanoparticles, or carbon nanotubes, can be used in diagnostic assays for plant diseases. These nanomaterials can be functionalized with specific antibodies or nucleic acid probes, allowing them to bind selectively to the target pathogens or biomarkers. When the target molecules are present, the nanomaterials emit characteristic signals that can be easily detected, providing a simple and sensitive detection method.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: More advanced methods for detecting plant diseases have been developed recently as a result of technological improvements. A quick, sophisticated, and on-site testing technique called a point-of-care (POC) diagnostic assay for plant disease detection is used to determine whether pathogens are present in plants. This kind of diagnostic tool is especially helpful Plant viruses, oomycetes fungus, and plant-parasitic nematodes can all be identified using loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP). To boost sensitivity, CRISPR-Cas9 and CRISPR-Cas12-based assays can be paired with various signal amplification techniques. In the presence of the target sequence, Cas9 will cleave the DNA, causing a double-stranded break. Then, using a variety of techniques, such as lateral flow tests, fluorescence-based assays, or PCR-based techniques, this cleavage event can be observed. Remote sensing techniques can be used to monitor and identify plant diseases from a distance, without direct physical contact with the plants. So that farmers can gain valuable insights into the health of their crops, identify diseases at early stages, and take appropriate actions to mitigate the spread and impact of plant diseases. Nanosensors can be used to detect infections that cause disease or the biochemical indicators that sick plants produce.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: There are multiple methods or trapping technologies available for pest management in agriculture. Some of the method discussed in literature are described here. Insect pests in an agricultural field can cause damage to the crops by infecting the crops with diseases and thus may affect adverse impact on its quality. Nowadays, farmers set up insect traps like sticky papers that comes in different colours such as yellow, green and blue to monitor the number of pests. Different insects are specifically/ particularly attracted to the colours of the sticky paper commonly such as yellow or blue (Espinoza et al�, 2016). Using sticky papers farmers can estimate the pest density trying to damage the crops. But for very small insects, the counting is very tedious and time-consuming and may be inaccurate at times. Pests and insects are drawn to plants because they emit volatile biological amalgams. Plants have their own self-defence mechanisms, and their reaction to insect attacks differs based on a variety of damaging factors. As a result, pest management has become an integral aspect of crop protection. Induced resistance is a prominent strategy in insect management strategies. This study is extremely useful in genetically developing plants to produce adequate and necessary self-protective chemicals (War et al�, 2012).

Because ultraviolet (UV) rays are visible to insects, they are easily drawn to them. To catch daylight insects, yellow pan traps with yellow sticky plates are used. Lamps with yellow illumination are also used at night to suppress pests. As a result, the extent of insect pest harm done to flowers, vegetables, and fruits can be limited. Hovering bug populations can be reduced by covering agricultural farms or fields with reflective materials (Shimoda and Honda, 2013).

In a pest control system that uses a wireless sensor grid, a scattered imaging device is incorporated. A host station, a master node, and a few client nodes are included in this design. The caught portion of the trapped insects is collected as a picture and relayed to the host station. The station includes a reference insect density. When the original insect density surpasses the reference density, an alarm is generated and sent to client nodes via the master node.

A ZigBee transceiver is used to convey pest information among nodes, which is then distributed to farmers through message or phone call (Priya et al�, 2013). The traditional sensor-node trapping method is modified with current web server capable sensors for dynamic response. Transmission at high speeds is guaranteed. This approach is also useful for remote field access. An agent program is used to evaluate the same, and the investigative results show that the given strategy is superior (Fukatsu and Hirafuji, 2005).

The Indian Agricultural Research Institute, New Delhi, conducted field research at regular intervals to find and analyse the efficacy of various light sources in light traps used to check many pest populations. The experiment made use of mercury lamps, black lights, and ultra violet lights. Mercury light trap had the greatest capability of the three light traps, followed by Black light trap and UV light trap (Ramamurthy et al�, 2010). In Australia, a wireless sensor design is being utilized to protect the fruit and vegetable production from fruit fly. Fruit fly detection and recognition algorithms use machine vision approaches. The average precision of this system has been determined experimentally to be 80% (Liu et al�, 2009).

To test the picture transfer rate, an image sensor network is created and compared to ZigBee. The JPEG images are verified, and the results show that the encoded images are more error resistant since they have numerous layers (Pekhteryev et al�, 2005). The efficacy of the trap is determined on the time of insect attack and the location of the bug. Predicting them in a manual trap is time-consuming. To improve the efficiency of the existing approaches, a sensor is added and insect recording is enabled. An insect detecting sensor comprised of phototransistors is developed. These photo gadgets are illuminated by infrared LEDs. The insects are discovered and recorded using wing strokes.

The data is transmitted from the field to the smartphone (Potamitis et al�, 2014;Kim et al�, 2008). Pests and diseases cause great economic loss to farmers through reduced yields and increased costs of pesticides and other control measures. Thereby use of different technologies of insect trapping yields both economic and environmental benefits. Environmental issues about pesticide usage, overexploitation of natural resources, changes in consumer habits, and advancements in technology are growing, bringing in a new agricultural revolution. This revolution involves the use of digital tools to boost productivity while also optimizing the management of natural resources and agricultural inputs (Fraser et al�, 2019).

div.head: Different methodologies of insect trapping for controlling of crop attacking insects
div.p: In the past, insecticides were employed to control the insects. Though they were effective in repelling insects, the quality of the harvest was poor. Furthermore, the fertility of the ground was harmed. Researchers are currently striving to restore soil quality through ecological farming. As a result, controlling approaches shouldn't put at risk field quality or yield.
Growing a trap crop is one of the most basic pest management approaches.

It is an attractive host plant that attracts attention and keeps insects away from the main crop throughout the considerable period of main crop. Pests and flies are more interested in the reproductive periods of host plants than in the vegetative phases. This principle is used in trap cropping to control pests. Some of the benefits of trap cropping include increased yield quality, the attraction of beneficial species, increased biodiversity, and reduced insecticide use. This strategy, however, does not provide a complete answer to all types of existing insect infestations. Rather, successful pest management necessitates knowledge and understanding of insect behaviour. Insect traps are the next step in pest management. They are used to monitor and reduce insect populations by employing baits such as food, visual lures, chemical attractants, and pheromones. The trap mechanism or bait varies depending on the crop or insects. The food traps are not meant to harm any animals. Food traps may attract a variety of insects. Proteins are attracted to flies and wasps. Lactic acid, flowery, and fruity scents attract mosquitos and other insects.

Visual lures come in a variety of vibrant colours and shapes. The strength and colour of light vary depending on the insect being trapped. Chemical attractants may just attract one sex. Certain insects are drawn to light traps with or without ultra violet light. The quantity of insects attracted by these traps may vary depending on environmental factors such as temperature and humidity. Flying and ground insects can be attracted by light traps.To catch diverse insects, a mix of light traps and other traps is utilized. Indoor pest monitoring employs sticky traps.

Because most traps are UV sensitive, only ultra violet traps are employed in the current system of insect traps. In the existing systems, there are no modern picture capture techniques. Furthermore, no timers or counters are used in the current systems. The recording of visual information and statistical data is critical in pest management. As a result, in this chapter, a technique for incorporating modern recording systems is presented.

div.head: Types of insects capturing trap
div.p: The overview of the following fundamental trap types, as well as modifications of those basic or common trap, types for various applications. Traps can be setup with or without attractant cues, and they can use a mix of visual (light, shape, colour), chemical (oviposition, pheromone, parapheromone, food/ host,), and audio signals to make traps more specific and effective. There are automated or self-monitoring devices that will send information to trap catch to an outside station. Traps of insects are a critical component of Insect Pest Management (IPM) systems. Though several systems of trapping or trapping system are addressed, the goal is to provide a broad framework of trap types with some illustrative examples. It is not aim or intended to be an exhaustive list of all traps discovered. However, different traps are utilized for insects that move through the air (wind-borne or flying insects), soil-dwelling or walking insects, aquatic and subterranean insects. Several of these are discussed more below.

div.head: Traps for Windblown insects and other flying insects Interception trap
div.p: This interception traps are often employed for faunal/insect surveys in ecological studies, but they can also be utilized for management of pest.
It is a hanging net with a top penetration that leads to a collecting tube in its most basic form. An example of an interception trap is the Malaise trap.

Insect migration has been studied using fixed interception traps with various collecting tubes for north-bound versus south-bound insects. Large funnelshaped nets have been put on moving trucks to collect flying insects like biting midges (Ceratopogonidae) or coupled to a suction mechanism to collect Russian wheat aphids, Diuraphisnoxia (Mordvilko).

div.head: Sticky traps
div.p: Panels, cylinders, or spheres coated with sticky substance are undoubtedly the most widely utilized traps in agricultural studies for faunal surveys. In their most basic form, Trap may be like panels coated or applied with substances that will trap insects that are blown onto the panel or fly into it. Panels can also be combined with a colour, a shape, and a chemical attractant. A thin coating of motor oil will keep very small insects in place, but most often larger insects get escaped. Sticky material such as Tangle-trap is applied to the surface to capture both small and large insects.

div.head: 3-dimensional triangular trap, Diamond traps and Wing traps
div.p: This is the other type of trap of low-cost that are employed in conjunction with an attractant. These are essentially a sticky trap with the adhesive surface covered on the interior of the trap, and they are both disposable and utilized with a sticky or adhesive component that is replaced after servicing. Trap known by name Delta trap are compact and light triangle (tent-shaped) traps that are easy to hang/suspend and transport. The Jackson trap is a type of delta trap that is used to catch a variety of tephritid fruit flies. It includes a removable base that is coated with sticky or adhesive substance and may be replaced as necessary during sampling. In public places such as stores and supermarkets, diamond-shaped traps are used to monitor interior pests. For pest Lepidoptera, wing traps with a ceiling and a floor are utilized.

div.head: Bucket traps
div.p: A bucket trap is another simple and low-cost trap. This trap can be used without a lid or as a closed container with provided holes for insects to enter on the top or sides. Small drainage holes near the bottom side of the trap can be used to allow trapped water to drain out. Bucket traps, such as the Nadel trap and the Mission trap, are translucent or semi-transparent, and closed containers with hole of entrance around the edge. The corn rootworm trap has a huge domeshaped top with a gap between the top and the bottomas well as the container.

The targeted insect determines the size and placement of the container, as well as the diameter and number of entry holes. These are typically used in conjunction with a chemical lure of some kind. These can be used without a lethal chemical to retrieve live insects, however caught insects may escape via the entrance openings. Bucket-type traps can be utilized with a variety of retention devices. Inside, a pesticide such as dimethyl 2, 2-dichlorovinyl phosphate (DDVP, mothballs) that can be applied.

div.head: Cone trap
div.p: This cone traps are similar to bucket traps with funnels; however, the funnel is much larger in comparison to the bucket or collecting tube. These are utilized with the funnel's big aperture facing the ground and the funnel's top heading up to a container. Heliothis traps and butterfly bait traps are two examples of these traps.

div.head: Traps for soil-dwelling insects and other walking arthropods Pitfall trap
div.p: This pitfall traps are effective for catching arthropods and other insects as they travel across a surface. This surface is often a ground or soil surface for capturing spiders, beetles and other ground-dwelling species, though they may also catch/seize flying insects as they move around the soil surface. Pitfall traps put on the top of stored grain have also been utilized to collect insect samples. Pitfall traps are usually made up of a container buried inside the ground substrate into which arthropods and other insects falls and gets trapped.

div.head: Grain Probe trap
div.p: Grain probe traps are a type of pitfall trap that has been modified for usage in stored grain. Grain probe traps are made up of an elongated cylinder along with drilled holes into the sides and above a funnel and receptacle of insects.

div.head: Shelter trap
div.p: Shelter traps are used for insects that harbourage in dark and are effective in situations where the trap must be hidden. These are typically used to catch insects as they walk or move across a surface, and they include small apertures that allow insects to enter the trap. The well-known instances of such traps are roach motels. They usually contain an adhesive material inside, to trap insects that enters or an oil or other chemical to keep attracted insects at bay. Shelter traps are used to catch stored goods insects and come in a variety of designs such as dome traps, stealth traps, and corner traps. They provide nesting site for Bees.

div.head: Emergence trap
div.p: Emergence traps are cone traps designed to catch adults with underground larval and pupal phases. These traps are made up of aluminium screening bent into the shape of a funnel, with the top aperture leading or forming to a collecting tube. To seal the trap to the earth, the trap is positioned the earth aligns with the surface, and soil is being pushed up around the edges. Adults emerge from belowground stages and enter the trap, where they are captured in the collecting tube.

div.head: Solar bait stations
div.p: Solar bait stations, on the other hand, can capture the underground stages of wireworms (Elateridae) and fake wireworms (Tenebrionidae). These are used to estimate wireworm numbers and determine seed treatment/non-treatment decisions. These insect larvae can be aggregated by establishing microenvironments with favourable temperature, moisture and feeding.

div.head: Traps for aquatic insects Interception trap
div.p: Interception traps collect aquatic insects as they move through the water and are comparable to wind-borne insect interception traps. These traps are frequently becoming narrow nets with round or rectangular apertures that can be placed in place to sample insects in running water or manually moved through the water. The size of the mesh determines the size of the insects kept; however very fine mesh may limit water circulation through the trap while retaining abundant trash.

div.head: Emergence trap
div.p: When aquatic insects with larval stages emerge as young one or adults, floating emergence traps are employed to capture them. Construction and operation are similar to that of soil-dwelling insect emergence traps. Communication and assessment of their surroundings are important. In insect traps, Semiochemicals with a behavioural influence on insect orientation or direction, that is, chemicals that cause an insect to move towards the source are utilized.

div.head: Automation trap or electronic trapping
div.p: Advancement in information and computer technology and remote sensing are expanding the field of precision agriculture, which refers to the use of computers to assist in management choices. The capacity to quickly transfer data obtained from traps into computer databases or spreadsheets is an essential component of precision agriculture and will aid in making decision timely for pest management.

Analysing the insect's wingbeats coming into a trap or flying above or overhead is one potential way for automating the insect identification procedure. In feasibility studies with aphids and mosquitoes, furrier transformations, closest neighbour classification, and artificial neural network classification schemes were investigated, and classification of species accuracy were reported up to 70-90%. An insect trap is designed with three layers of varying thickness to collect insects of various sizes, and the layers are covered in the proper bait to draw the insects. Using black lights (Ultra Violet) and LED lights, an autonomous monitoring system is ensured using a low-cost image sensor to record the photos of pests caught in the system. The battery is charged using a 10W solar panel to power the UV and LED lights using a battery. The energy produced by solar panels is kept in a battery that powers the system at night. Utilizing a solar charge controller ensures efficient charging prolongs the life of the battery and increases its capacity. The photographs that were captured are transferred to a remote-control station. The knowledge collected through this method is used to estimate the insect populations in farms. It is used in rice, brinjal and other field crop including in an open field for several insect pests such as Dragon fly, Cricket, Grasshopper, Flies, Bugs etc.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Insect trapping systems are chief components of Integrated Pest Management (IPM) programs. Decisions can be made by trapping data about whether to initiate or discontinue control measures, as well as to evaluate the efficacy of previously adopted control procedures. The use of detection trapping alerts employees to the appearance of a new insect pest in an earlier or previously pest-free area, allowing control measures to be performed in a scheduled time.
Early diagnosis and targeting of tiny infestations or incidence will aid pest management efforts.

The overview of the following fundamental trap types, as well as modifications of those basic or common trap, types for various applications. Traps can be setup with or without attractant cues, and they can use a mix of visual (light, shape, colour), chemical (oviposition, pheromone, parapheromone,food/ host,), and audio signals to make traps more specific and effective. There are automated or self-monitoring devices that will send information to trap catch to an outside station. Traps of insects are a critical component of Insect Pest Management (IPM) systems. Though several systems of trapping or trapping system are addressed, the goal is to provide a broad framework of trap types with some illustrative examples. It is not aim or intended to be an exhaustive list of all traps discovered.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Worldwide, 40% of a crop is lost to insect damage, particularly in developing countries. Some of the major insect pests belong to the Lepidoptera and Coleoptera, and their larval or grub stages cause major damage to economically important crops including tobacco, cotton, corn, sorghum, tomato, sunflower, pulses, wheat, and lucerne. Recently, some insecticides have been primarily used for agricultural pests. Over the years, most pesticides have led to insecticide resistance in major lepidopterans, a reduction in natural enemy populations, and harm to mammals and the environment. These problems have led researchers to develop new approaches, starting with transgenic Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) and newer biotechnological approaches for insect pest management, including the use of transgenic plants, Gene editing (RNAi), Gene drives, and the most recent one, CRISPR-Cas9 technology. Since the increase in the commercialization of genetically modified crops, most farmers have adopted this technology in recent years and also planted biotech crops. This has led to a reduction in the use of pesticides and an additional financial gain for farmers over the last 20 years.
Two main categories can be used to classify the application of biotechnology in pest management: (i) Traditional biotechnology and (ii) Modern biotechnology

div.head: i) Traditional biotechnological approaches used in pest management
div.p: Traditional biotechnology products for pest management include tissue culture methods, bioagents, and biofertilizers.

div.head: Tissue Culture Techniques
div.p: The tissue culture (TC) approach offers the rapid multiplication of diseasefree plants under controlled and aseptic conditions in a relatively short period. It provides a wide range of opportunities for the development, conservation, and utilization of genetic variability for the improvement of plants. These in vitro methods found extensive use in the synthesis of secondary metabolites (Alemu, 2020). Additionally, TC is used for in vitro disease-resistant plant screening.

div.head: Biocontrol-Agents used in pest management
div.p: The term "bio-agents" refers to a broad category of biological agents, such as microbial agents, natural enemies, parasitoids and parasites, and botanicals. These components can be used alone or combined to create effective and holistic Integrated Pest Management (IPM) strategies to sustain agricultural production (Barzman et al., 2015;Stenberg, 2017). A variety of biocontrol agents and biopesticides, which are frequently used interchangeably, have been used as traditional biotechnology products for the management of pests. Entomopathogenic fungi (Sultana et al., 2017); Entomopathogenic nematodes (Gozel and Gozel, 2016); Entomopathogenic bacteria and Insecticidal bacteria (Saiyad, 2017) Sterile Insect Technique (SIT) Control of fruit flies (Haymer, 2016) Botanicals or plant-based biocontrol agents Semiochemicals (Saha and Chandran, 2017), Pheromones (Kirk, 2017), Attractants (Drmic et al., 2017), and Push-pull technology for insect control (Pickett et al., 2014) IPM-involving biocontrol agents or biopesticides Integrated Insect control (Gangwar, 2017)

div.head: ii) Modern biotechnology Molecular Breeding for Pest Management
div.p: Host plant resistance (HPR) is one of the most effective preventative measures that can also be made more reliable and fast using DNA marker-based breeding techniques. DNA markers can be regularly used in various aspects of plant genome analysis, including genome fingerprinting, gene localization, genome mapping, population genetics, taxonomy, plant breeding, and diagnostics (Chauhan and Kumar, 2015).

div.head: Marker-Assisted Breeding
div.p: Using DNA markers that have a close relationship to the target loci as a substitute for or in addition to phenotype screening is known as markerassisted selection (MAS). Markers are utilized in breeding, also known as marker-assisted breeding (MAB) or molecular plant breeding, in addition to the selection of the best traits (Singh,2018). Through molecular breeding techniques, certain improved cultivars or varieties developed resistance to pests and diseases (Khera et al., 2013).

div.head: Variants of Plant Resistance to Pests
div.p: Plants' pest resistance can be developed more quickly than through conventional breeding using the molecular techniques discussed above. There are various types of resistance, including multiple, quantitative, and durable resistances. Gene stacking and gene pyramiding were used to create durable pest-resistant varieties. Pyramiding is the stacking of multiple genes that results in the simultaneous expression of several genes in a variety to produce durable resistance expression. Both gene stacking and gene pyramiding require combining two or more genes into a single plant, and the combined characteristics developed through this process are referred to as stacked traits (Alemu, 2020). Pyramiding of two insect resistance genes in rice (Liu et al., 2016) Genetic Engineering for Pest Management Genetic engineering, also called genetic modification, is the technique of direct, controlled, and artificial modification of an organism's genes that are used to change the genetic makeup of cells, including the transfer of genes within and across species boundaries, to create improved or entirely new organisms.

Therefore, plant genetic engineering is a technology that modifies the genome of a host crop by introducing a foreign donor gene under the control of specific gene regulatory sequences. There are different types of genetic engineering based on the differences between the gene donor and recipient, namely transgenic, intragenic, cisgenic, and subgenics.

div.head: i) Transgenics
div.p: Transgenics are a term used to describe genetic engineering processes that include transferring foreign genes from unrelated species. This category of genetically modified plants includes the majority of GM products over the last two decades (Sharma et al., 2012).

ii) Intragenics, Cisgenics, and Subgenics These are several GE techniques that involve gene transfer within the same species or closely related species. Cisgenesis is the process of genetically modifying a crop species using a complete copy of a natural gene along with its regulatory components, which are only found in sexually compatible plants (same crop species). Intragenesis is the transfer of novel gene and regulatory sequence combinations from one individual of the same species (belonging to the same breedable gene pool) to another species (Holme et al., 2013). The crop that has been genome-edited is referred to as "subgeneics" regardless of whatever genome editing technology is employed (Sticklen, 2015).

div.head: RNA Interference (RNAi)
div.p: RNA interference (RNAi), the sequence-specific suppression of gene expression, provides several opportunities, particularly for the study of gene function, the control of pest populations, and the elimination of disease-causing pathogens. RNAi is used to control insect pests by suppressing essential genes, leading to reduced fitness and mortality.

Target genes are identified in numerous insect pests with the aim of applying RNA interference (RNAi) in pest management. RNAi targets identified include genes involved in feeding and digestion, reproductive fitness, resistance to insecticides and plant allelochemicals, the production of dsRNases, and the transmission of plant viruses (Alemu, 2020).

div.head: RNAi protected the plant against a lepidopteran
div.p: The first RNAi-based insecticide, SmartStax Pro®, was developed by Monsanto and Dow AgroSciences and approved by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). This plant-incorporated protectant (PIP) will use a different strategy: this plant will express a variety of Bt proteins as well as dsRNA that targets the WCR Snf7 gene against western corn rootworms (Head et al., 2017). Early investigations on the western corn rootworm, Diabrotica virgifera virgifera (WCRW) (Baum et al., 2007), and the cotton bollworm, Helicoverpa armigera (CBW) (Mao et al�, 2007) provide proof of concept for the use of RNAi in insect pest management. The first successful RNAi plant protected against H� armigerawas demonstrated by silencing the CYP6AE14 gene, necessary for detoxifying gossypol content from the cotton crop (Gossypum hirsutum). These studies have been conducted to explore alternative target genes to control and understand the RNAi mechanism in lepidopterans.

In H� armigera-fed transgenic tobacco plants producing dsRNA targeted to the nuclear ecdysone receptor complex (HaEcR), completely necessary for insect development, larval death, and molting abnormalities were found (Mao et al., 2007). A lepidopteran pest, Spodoptera exigua, was more easily resistant to the transgenic tobacco that expressed dsRNA for HaEcR.

When Baum et al. (2007) exposed larval WCRW to 290 dsRNAs, they found 14 genes that affected larval performance. One of them, vacuolar ATPase subunit A (V-ATPase), was continued for further examination. Low dosages of orally administered V-ATPase-targeting dsRNA in an artificial diet inhibited the corresponding WCRW mRNA. Importantly, larvae raised on maize plants that have been genetically modified to express V-ATPase dsRNA also showed lower expression of the V-ATPase gene and significantly decreased plant root damage. (Baum et al., 2007).

div.head: RNAi protected the plant against a coleopteran
div.p: According to research, a pest beetle, Henosepilachna vigintioctopunctata, can survive for a shorter period after being exposed to exogenously applied gene-specific dsRNA. They were able to successfully cause 98%, 88%, and 60% mortality in first and third instars and adults after 10, 12, and 14 days by topically applying bacterially expressed dsRNA targeting the Snf7 gene, which encodes an important cellular component of endosomal sorting complexes necessary for transport. Similar to this, Ullah et al. (2020) also observed that after exposure to dsRNA targeting the chitin synthase 1 gene, survival and fecundity were considerably decreased in the cotton-melon aphid, Aphis gossypii, by 41% and 48%, respectively.

div.head: Advantages
div.p: • The RNAi pest management method is an increase in our understanding of the developmental, physiological, and molecular mechanisms of insect life stages, but also a great opportunity to develop new methods for insect pest management.

• RNAi is used for large-scale functional genomics studies in many economically important insect pests. • RNAi pest management techniques are used to identify the target genes for insecticide resistance and also improve our understanding of the mode of action of insecticides and the molecular mechanisms of insecticide resistance. • RNA Interference is a powerful research tool and budding technology for effective pest management in agriculture.

div.head: Limitation
div.p: • Lack of reliable dsRNA delivery methods • The inability to produce large amounts of dsRNA using economical methods. • It is difficult to achieve efficient RNAi if the trigger is rapidly degraded in an organism unless a high dose of dsRNA or siRNA is applied. • The easy development of resistance against RNAi products by mutations of target genes, mutations of RNAi core machinery genes, increased dsRNA excretion, enhanced dsRNA degradation, and decreased dsRNA uptake and transport.

div.head: Genome Editing Technologies
div.p: Recent years have seen a rapid development of a variety of new genetic modification techniques (nGMs) or new breeding techniques (NBTs), which are used for precisely modifying the genetic structure of a plant variety to introduce new traits or modify existing ones.A range of new genetic modification techniques (nGMs) or new breeding techniques (NBTs) has been rapidly developed in recent years and used to modify the genetic makeup of a plant variety in a more precise way to introduce new traits or modify existing ones. NBTs include novel methods that are commonly referred to as "genome editing" and that attempt to rapidly create characteristics that may be useful, like in CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing technology (Alemu, 2020). Genome editing allows genetic material to be added, removed, or altered at particular locations in the genome. A CRISPR array is composed of a series of repeats interspaced by spacer sequences acquired from invading genomes of foreign organisms. CRISPR-Cas9, which stands for clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats and CRISPR-associated protein 9, is a more recent one. The CRISPR-Cas9 technology is more effective, faster less expensive, and more accurate.

div.head: Role of CRISPER CAS system in insect application
Orcomut ants in Locusta migratoria
div.p: A miRNA (miR-276) gene was found to facilitate the synchrony of egg development and to directly determine the density of locusts. Insects depend significantly on their olfactory system. Chemical pheromone signals are initially taken in by peripheral tissues, then processed by antennal nerve tissues, before being integrated into the brain by the olfactory and other sensory organs to control insect behaviors including foraging, feeding, and mating. Using a sgRNA that targets the odorant receptor co-receptor (Orco) gene, scientists modified the locust genome using the CRISPR/Cas9 method.

The authors successfully generated Orco homozygous and heterozygous mutant lines and achieved highly effective mutation rates in the Orco gene by microinjecting Cas9-mRNA and Orco-sgRNA into locust eggs (Sunet al., 2017). This suggested new ideas for locust control and can be used for other insects from the orthopteran clade such as crickets.

div.head: CRISPR/Cas9 mediated knockout of the abdominal-A homeotic gene in Spodoptera furgiperda
div.p: This abdominal A gene is specifically responsible for the formation of abdominal segments. Sfabd-A single guide RNA (sgRNA) and Cas9 protein were injected into 244 FAW embryos in a study by Wu et al� (2018). Sixtytwo embryos injected with Sfabd-A sgRNA hatched. Twelve of these hatching eggs eventually transformed into larvae that exhibited typical aba-A mutant characteristics, including fused segments. The majority of mutant moths were sterile, and one female produced a few unviable eggs when it was outcrossed to a wild-type male. Genotyping of 20 unhatched Sfabd-A sgRNA-injected embryos and 42 moths that emerged from those embryos revealed that 100% of the unhatched embryos and 50% of the moths had indel mutations at the Sfabd-A genomic locus close to the guide RNA target site (Wu et al�, 2018). These findings prove that the CRISPR/Cas9 system is highly effective at editing the genome of the FAW.

div.head: Eradicating Malaria with CRISPR
div.p: By altering the sex-determining gene with CRISPR, researchers have made the male gene predominate over the female gene. After that, the altered organism was spread by gene drive in the expectation that females would eventually be eliminated from the population (Prabhune, 2019). Malaria can only be transmitted by female mosquitoes, so eliminating females would reduce the rate of human transmission.

div.head: CRISPR Mosquito: Gene Knockout Creates Malaria-Resistant Population
div.p: By eliminating a gene, mosquitoes can become resistant to the malaria parasites. Using CRISPR technology, they examined the effects of inactivating the gene for fibrinogen-related protein 1 (FREP1). RNAi-mediated gene silencing techniques had previously been used to study FREP1. The incidence of P. falciparum infection was reduced by 11% as a result of FREP1 silencing, however just as RNAi-based gene silencing only leads to partial protein depletion (Prabhune, 2019). On the other side, CRISPR can be used to completely knock out the gene, and this is used to disrupt the FREP1 gene.

Using CRISPR, researchers were able to demonstrate that FREP1 knockout mosquitoes exhibited more slowly than their wild-type counterparts, were less likely to feed blood meals when given the chance, and laid fewer and less viable eggs.

div.head: Malformations in Dendrolimus punctatus
div.p: In China and Southeast Asia, the pine caterpillar moth, D� punctatus, causes severe defoliation and lowers resin production, making it a devastating forest pest. To precisely and effectively alter gene expression in D� punctatus, researchers first introduced mutations into the Wnt-1 gene and indicated that the Wnt-1 gene is associated with segmentation and development. According to the findings, various mutant phenotypes, including deformed heads, legs with defects, and abnormal posterior segments, were seen. Therefore, they may provide a management strategy for a major defoliator (Sun et al., 2017).

div.head: CRISPR / Cas9 technology in Drosophila
div.p: As a model insect, D� melanogaster and the application of CRISPR / Cas9 technology in Drosophila has promoted the use of genome editing technology in other insects. CRISPR/Cas9 technology is used to introduce mutations into the Drosophila genome, generating a 4.6 kb deletion in the yellow locus by utilizing two target sgRNAs and a single-stranded oligonucleotide donor (ssODN) template (Singh,2022). It reduced pigmentation and reduced larval mouthparts. Furthermore, it has potential applications for generating designer flies.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Millions of dollars are lost each year due to insect crop devastation, and the use of chemical pesticides has unwanted side effects such as resistance and unintentional impacts on species that are beneficial (Kumar et al. 2019). For farmers, authorities, and academics, finding additional target-selective pest management techniques is of utmost importance. Enhancing performance, cost-effectiveness, and increasing the markets for bio pesticides are all made possible by biotechnology. Molecular methods are used to detect and track the emergence and spread of particular types that are natural enemies (Tipvadee, 2002). It offers chances for the growth of beneficial insect natural enemies with features including pesticide resistance, cold tolerance, and sex ratio change. By using transgenic technology, GE has produced beneficial GM insect-resistant cultivars. Crops that express Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) Cry toxins have been planted all over the world and are an essential tool for pest management. The creation and use of transgenic plants containing insecticidal genes will reduce the need for chemical applications and boost the activity of natural enemies. Molecular methods are used to detect and track the emergence and spread of certain natural enemy biotypes. Biotechnology and genetic engineering can significantly enhance the production, formulation, and storage of entomopathogenic fungi (Yadav et al., 2018). Insect-resistant crops have been created using transgenic technology during the past 20 years to lower yield loss and pesticide use (Christou et al., 2006). Transgenic plants that express genes for the Bacillus thuringiensis, or Bt, delta-endotoxin, protease inhibitors, -amylase inhibitors, enzymes, and plant lectins have been shown to be resistant to insects (Padul et al., 2012). One of the most remarkable developments in plant transgenic technology, which has seen tremendous success both commercially and environmentally, is the development of Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) insect-resistant crops. A process known as RNAi occurs when cells are exposed to double-stranded RNA (dsRNA), which causes the paired messenger RNA (mRNA) to degrade in a sequence-specific way. This technique has been suggested as a viable method for controlling insect pests since suppressing expression of critical genes might cause insect mortality (Kunte et al�, 2020).

Since its discovery as a bacterial adaptive immune system, CRISPR/Cas9 has been modified into a ground-breaking method of editing the genome. CRISPR/Cas9-based editing of genomes has been used to a broad spectrum of species for diverse scientific reasons due to its high degree of specificity and ease of handling. Different methods have been used for pest control using CRISPR/Cas9-like systems, which makes the pests susceptible to different insecticides, impairing the pests' ability to reproduce, preventing the pests from metamorphosing, and providing numerous additional advantages (Singh et al., 2022). The system of CRISPR/Cas9 has the potential to provide novel ways for insect pest management. In this chapter, we covered how to manage insect pests utilising molecular techniques such as DNA barcoding, editing the genome (CRISPR/Cas technology) or GE, and RNAi).

div.head: Using CRISPR/Cas technology for pest control
div.p: This technology used to create insect-resistant plants seems to be incredibly effective in reducing production costs and boosting farm profitability. The production of plant types that are resistant to insects or other methods for the sustainable control of insect pests to maintain food security show great potential as a result of the fast advancement in CRISPR technology procedures and their many applications. It has recently developed into a tool for genome editing that is very efficient, quick, and quick (Moon et al., 2022). Mega nucleases, zinc finger nucleases (ZFNs), transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs), and clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)/CRISPR-associated proteins (Cas) are the four main categories of sequence-specific nucleases used in gene editing to date (Gilles and Averof 2014;Baltes and Voytas, 2015). With the use of this technology, epigenetic editing-also known as modifying numerous genes at onceor even a single nucleotide is now possible. A method for editing genes or genomes that focuses on a specific DNA region, produces a clean cut at the target spot, and renders a gene inactive or substitutes one gene version with another. It was created in 2012 by Jennifer Doudna, Emmanuelle Charpentier, and Fang Zhang. In 2020, the first two of these scientists shared the Chemistry Nobel Prize. The most recent gene editing technology is this. According to Devashish et al. (2015), there are three types of CRISPR-Cas9 editing systems: type I, type II, and type III. In type II systems, the Cas9 nuclease needs DNA that fits a single RNA guide (sgRNA). The guide RNA and the Cas protein are the two main elements of the CRISPR-Cas system. The nuclease enzyme Cas9 protein, also known as the molecular scissors, is in charge of cutting the DNA. Cas9 is guided to the desired location in the genome by molecules called guide-RNAs, where it will sever the old sequence and insert the new one (Courtier-Orgogozo et al., 2015). Figure 1 depicts three distinct CRISPR-based genome-editing techniques for controlling insect infestations. (2017) altered Spodoptera exigua, producing a mutant insect with a high level of resistance to the insecticides chlorantraniliprole, cyantraniliprole, and fubendiamide. Helicoverpa armigera was modified using CRISPR/ Cas9 to alter the cadherin gene in order to show how the gene contributes to the development of resistance against Bt toxin. Similar to other significant agricultural pests, the beetroot armyworm (Spodoptera exigua) is generally managed using chemical pesticides. Resistance to the pesticide Spinosad has recently been identified in S. exigua field populations. Zuo et al. (2020) effectively created a homozygous strain (Se6-KO) with a 1760-bp deletion inside Se6 in S� exigua, resulting in a premature termination of Se6 via the CRISPR/Cas9 genome-editing method. They convincingly confirmed the functional involvement of Se6 in spinosyn pesticide resistance and offer an example of genome editing being used to validate an objective premature truncation linked with resistance. Moon et al. (2022) have examined and explored the application of CRISPR-Cas technology for genome editing in for a long-time insect pest management.

Gene drive, which is based on the CRISPR/Cas-9 gene editing system, is a strong technique which encourages the inheritance of the gene drive tool directly through sexual reproduction, allowing it to spread swiftly through a population. Courtier Orgogozo et al. (2017) presented another potential use of CRISPR-based gene drive, notably pest species management to boost crop yield. Singh et al. (2022) emphasised recent advances in CRISPR/Cas9 investigation and explored its potential applications in pest control.

div.head: DNA barcoding for pest control
div.p: The successful implementation of an integrated pest management (IPM) programme depends on accurate identification of agricultural pests. However, additional molecular approaches like DNA barcoding are utilised since conventional morphological methods have certain limits. Especially for juvenile stages when morphometric investigations are confusing, DNA barcoding is a trustworthy method for identifying agricultural pests. It will also aid in the creation of more potent pest management strategies for controlling pest species. In order to assess the precision of phenotypic identification of insect pests through DNA barcoding, Tahir et al� A total of 247 insect pests comprising 18 genera, 10 families, and 22 species were recognised morphologically. Nevertheless, molecular identifications proved the existence of 20 species, 11 families, and 16 genera of agricultural pests. Using this molecular method, specimens that were misclassified through morphological investigations were assigned to the correct taxon. Results showed that some pest species have apparent barcode gaps. There are two pest species called Tribolium confusum and Tribolium castaneum (Coleoptera: Tenebrionidae) that are extremely difficult to distinguish from one another based just on appearance. Such pests are identified in grain that has been kept in their juvenile stages, which makes identification more challenging. Integrative pest control urgently needs precise identification of these pests. DNA barcoding was utilised by Aslam et al. (2019) to precisely identify these pests at any stage of life. To enhance invasive pest detection at U.S. ports of entry in 2019, Madden et al� employed DNA barcoding. Thrips are a main sucking pest and plant virus vector that causes significant economic damage in agriculture. Thrips identification is critical for efficient pest management tactics. Tyagi et al. (2017) undertook the greatest DNA barcoding effort on 370 sequences from 89 thrips morphospecies, comprising 104 unique sequences from 39 morphospecies, include four species' type specimens. For 73 species (82%), the results of various species delimitation approaches (BIN, ABGD, GMYC, and bPTP) were compatible with the corresponding morphological identifications. The presence of more than one MOTU in 14 morphospecies suggests cryptic diversity, which includes two significant vector species (Frankliniellas chultzei and Thrips palmi). Yet, four morphospecies (Thrips moundi, Thrips carthami, Haplothrips andersi, and Haplothrips gowdeyi) had low genetic distances with overlapping in the barcode gap, requiring further analysis with multiple molecular markers along with additional specimens from diverse geographical areas for better taxonomic judgement.

div.head: RNA interference (RNAi)
div.p: It is a new technique that appears to be a viable alternative to chemical pesticides. RNAi allows for the silencing of critical genes in insects, resulting in the selective destruction of specific species. Dicer (a member of the ribonuclease III family of nucleases) first converts dsRNA into 20-25bp pieces known as small interfering RNA (siRNA). Following that, siRNAs are taken up by the RNA induced silencing complex (RISC), where they are unravelled into two single strands, the "guide" strand and the "passenger" strand. The complex's "passenger" strand is removed, and the "guide" strand subsequently guides the RISC complex to connect with the complementary mRNA, inhibiting translation of this target mRNA." The RISC component argonaute-2 protein is crucial in the identification and mRNA degradation process. It has the PAZ and PIWI domains. The guide strand, which acts as a template to trap the desired mRNA, is kept by the PAZ domain. According to Yu et al. (2013) and Darrington et al. (2017), the PIWI domain splits the trapped mRNA into two pieces that are then further broken down by cytoplasmic nucleases. Insects are exposed to dsRNA via three major routes: eating, topical application, and injections into the hemolymph. In 2020, Kunte et al. examined the administration of dsRNA by food. In conclusion, new techniques have been established to increase the effectiveness of this approach, including the use of transgenic plants, microbes, and formulations based on nanotechnology. The challenges that must be solved for this technology to be effective as a pest management tool were highlighted by Kunte et al. in 2020. Similar to this, Adeyinka et al. (2020) highlighted the existence of nucleases and doublestranded RNA binding protein while describing certain genes implicated in insect dsRNA absorption and discussing the causes of variable RNAi response in insect pests. Recent innovations in dsRNA delivery for effective and efficient knockdown of insect pests were examined. In conclusion, topical dsRNA administration in conjunction with a complex nanoparticle has enormous promise for RNAi-mediated pest management. In their study of RNAi-mediated control of hemipteran insects, Jain et al. (2021) offered viable solutions, such as in-silico strategies combined with laboratory-based toxicity testing to avoid any unintended impacts on helpful species. The researchers subsequently investigated strategies to reduce dsRNA degradation in the environment and in insects, such as stacking and dsRNA effector formation. Finally, to minimise the cost of dsRNA manufacture and application and to enhance broad-spectrum hemipteran pest control, we investigate nontransformative RNAi techniques, concatomerization of RNAi sequences, and pyramiding RNAi with active components. Horak (2020) sequence-directed suppression of protein synthesis by RNA interference has recently been proposed as a viable method for controlling pest species.
