RNAi, which is intended to be species-specific, has a lower impact on non-target species and the environment. It is important to conduct further study to advance the field of RNAi-based control of vertebrate pest species. According to Yan et al. (2021), nanoparticles like chitosan, liposomes, and cationic dendrimers have advantages over traditional double-stranded (ds)RNA delivery systems for delivering dsRNA/small interfering (si)RNA to increase RNAi efficiency. This advances the research and application of RNAi-based pest management strategies. The challenges of traditional dsRNA delivery systems are illustrated, the mechanism of nanoparticle-mediated RNAi is revealed, recent developments and effective uses for nanoparticle-mediated RNAi in pest management are summarised, and finally the potential of nanoparticle-based RNA pesticides are addressed. The use of RNAi encourages the development of innovative ways to plant protection in an environmentally friendly way. Commercially viable genetically engineered crops expressing dsRNA have been produced as insect pest control tools. List et al. (2022) discovered interesting trends that support the assumption that fundamental toxicological principles applicable to RNAi bioassays and give preliminary guidance for future study comparable to studies on conventional insecticides. To speed the development of RNAi as an effective pest management tool, we urge for training that incorporates genetic, organismal, and toxicological techniques.

div.head: Genetic engineering (GE)
div.p: Although the word "genetic engineering" is frequently perceived as emotional or even frivolous, it is likely the one that the majority would choose. The techniques used to manipulate genes might be referred to by a variety of names. recognise. However, a number of alternative terminologies, such as genetic manipulation, cloning of genes, recombinant DNA technology, and the new genetics, can also be used to characterise the technique (Nicholl, 2023). A multifaceted integrated pest management strategy should include genetically modified crops that offer resistance to pests and illnesses or tolerance to herbicides. Unfortunately, the majority of these methods use natural resources, but genetic engineering has lately become a practical substitute for controlling plant insect pests. When introduced into bacteria using r-DNA technology, insecticidal proteins have demonstrated effectiveness against coleopteran and lepidopteran pests. Native expression of secondary plant metabolites, such as flavonoids, exhibits detrimental effects on the development of insect pests. Insecticidal potential of plants engineered to produce enzymes that break down insect cell walls, which include structural polysaccharides like chitin, was effective. The fundamental idea behind food digestion is the hydrolysis of the meal by an enzyme. When insects encounter enzymatic inhibitors (which include protease inhibitors, alpha-amylase inhibitors, etc.) found in the feeding section of the plants, such gut enzymes become attenuated. These genes, which all produce insecticides, are widely distributed in the natural world (Dash et al. (2021). The development of transgenes against plant insect pests via genetic engineering is a triumph in this field.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Pest insects pose a significant risk to agricultural production globally, contributing significantly to economic loss and endangering global food security. Numerous pests have evolved a strong resistance to chemical insecticides as a result of their widespread use, and some of these pesticides have unintended side effects on pollinators and natural foes. The development of insect pest resistant plants using the DNA barcoding gene editing, genetic engineering and RNAi looks to be a very effective way to reduce production costs and boost farm profitability. For ecologically friendly practises, the advancement of molecular crop protection strategies is necessary.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Biopesticides are biological substances or plant-based products that are used for decreasing the number of pests in an ecosystem. These substances, which include natural enemies, microbial products, phytochemicals, and semiochemicals, are derived from plants and animals. Biopesticides use nontoxic approaches to keep pests under control. They are widely recognised as low-risk items that are secure for both humans and the environment. It makes use of natural pest such as parasitoids, predators, nematodes, and semiochemicals as well as botanicals, microbiological pathogens like fungi, bacteria, and viruses. Synthetic pesticides have created resistance, resurgence and an emergence of secondary pests as an outcome of their unchecked use. Strict restrictions on the use of synthetic pesticides and the increased demand for organic practises have promoted the use of biopesticides. By employing biopesticides, pests can be controlled without endangering the environment or people's health. Biopesticides play a significant role in promoting sustainable agriculture. Prior to reaching the economic threshold level, they significantly reduce arthropod populations, which results in mortality (Betz et al., 2000). Integrated pest management programmes (IPM) urgently need to incorporate biopesticides since they have low phytotoxicity, leave no residues, and are environmentally beneficial. (Bhattacharyya et al�, 2016).

div.head: Perspectives on Biological Pesticides: Indian and Global Context
div.p: The worldwide market for insecticides as a whole is made up of 15% by the biopesticides industry, which has a $3 billion global market share and accounts for 5% of all crop protection. At the moment, biopesticides are expanding at a 15% yearly pace. Only 15% of bio inputs for managing insect pests have been found in effective natural control schemes. Over 125 different kinds of natural enemies are marketed as biopesticides. Surprisingly, 90% of the microbial biopesticides currently on the market are made from the single entomopathogenic bacteria Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt.). The world's three largest manufacturers and consumers of biopesticides are the United States, the European Union, and India. The India Biological pesticides Market Insights research predicts that the Indian biopesticides market would increase from INR 197 billion in 2018 to INR 316 billion by 2024. Given that pest-related crop losses in India total about USD 9259 million annually, the market for biopesticides has a large potential. However, biopesticides now only account for 5% of the worldwide pesticide business. 200 entomopathogenic fungi-based products are already available, and the Central Insecticides Board and Registration Committee (CIBRC) of India has registered around 970 microbiological formulations. Beauveria bassiana, B� brongniartii, Metarhizium anisopliae, Lecanicillium lecanii, and Hirsutella thompsonii are frequently used to manage arthropods. Additionally, there are 45 commercially available Bt-based products, with the kurstaki strain of B� thuringiensis being heavily employed against lepidopteran pests like loopers and bollworms. The B� thuringiensis israelensis and sphaericus subspecies are both effective against mosquitoes. Nuclear polyhedrosis viruses (NPV) control both armyworms and bollworms. In India, lepidopteran pests of rice, sugarcane, and apples were successfully controlled for the first time with the aid of Steinernema carpocapsae (DD-136 strain). Other natural strains of entomopathogenic nematodes, such as Heterorhabditis indica, Steinernema carpocapsae, S� abbasi, and S� thermophilum, as well as S. riobrave, have been used to control pests in agricultural fields. A commercial product with a 12-month shelf life has been created in India using a wettable formulation derived from H. indica.

div.head: Classifications of biopesticides
div.p: Biopesticides are divided into three main categories by the Environmental Protection Agency based on the type of component they include. 1. Microbial biopsticide. 2. Biochemical pesticide.

div.head: Plant-incorporated protectants
div.p: This category includes the bulk of broad-spectrum bio-pesticides for pest control, also referred to as microbial pesticides. To control insect pests, they use microorganisms including bacteria, viruses, fungus, and protozoans as active components. These biopesticides are highly precise in their targeting of the designated species. Microbial biopesticides exhibit host-specificity, are self-sustaining, and are environmentally safe. Because it effectively eliminates a wide variety of pests, including coleopterans, lepidopterans, and dipterans, Bt. (Bacillus thuringiensis) is one of the most widely employed bacteria for insect pest control. Plant kinds that have been genetically modified to produce pesticides are referred to as genetically modified crops, or Plant-Incorporated Protectants (PIPs). The plants are given the genetic material needed to produce these chemicals, which gives them pest resistance. The incorporation of pesticidal proteins derived from bacteria or fungi into the plants gives them the ability to fend off particular pests. An illustrative example of this is the use of Bt� protein, which is introduced into plants via genetic engineering to develop PIPs. Bt� toxin is highly specific to its host and typically causes rapid mortality in pests, usually within 48 hours PIPs do not affect vertebrates or the environment in any negative ways. Biochemical pesticides are naturally occurring compounds and secondary metabolites that have the ability to control pests. Herbal pesticides are another name for these substances. In India, neem-based formulations are frequently used as biochemical pesticides. Examples include neem oil, NSKE, and concentrations made from neem bark and leaves. Additional biochemical pesticides include pyrethrin from Chrysanthemum cinerariaefolium and essential oils from canola, tea tree, lemongrass (Cymbopogon citratus), and tea tree. Diatomaceous earth (DE), which is formed from fossilised sediments of various siliceous species, mostly diatoms and other algae, is used to treat a range of field pests. DE has a high capacity for absorption, which can erode and desiccate the insect's cuticle, killing it instantly. Semiochemicals, which are categorised as biochemical pesticides, include a wide range of compounds, including pheromones used for sex, repellents for insects, and kairomones. These chemical cues, which are mostly produced by insects, cause individuals of the same or different species to change their behaviour. Insect sex pheromones are among the commercially accessible semiochemicals that are frequently used. Through mating disruption techniques, sex pheromones have proven to be very effective in regulating adults. For the management of flying insects, particularly lepidopterans and coleopterans, mass trapping using sex pheromones is very helpful in integrated pest management (IPM) programmes.

div.head: Bacterial-Derived Biopesticides
div.p: In general, there are three types of bacterial pesticides: 1. Obligate pathogens: This pathogen requires certain environments and mediums for sporulation, reproduction, and growth. They only infect a small number of hosts, and many of those hosts can produce spores.

div.head: Mode of Action of Bacillus thuringiensis
div.p: When B� thuringiensis undergoes spore development, it creates protein crystals that are essential for breaking down gut cells in insects that are sensitive to it. The bacterium produces cry and cyt proteins, which are crystalline proteins.

The alkaline pH environment of the insect's stomach, which ranges from 9.0 to 11.0, is activated when the larva consumes the cry protein (Bravo et al� 2007). These proteins bind to the receptor location in the gut, causing the cells of the midgut to develop holes. The midgut is consequently paralysed by the Bt.

toxin, which also induces cell lysing and the discharge of gut contents into the insect's hemocoel. The death of the organism is the result of this disruption of the pH equilibrium. The benefits of bacterial pesticides Use of bacterial biopesticides is harmless and does not harm humans, animals, or other living things. Specificity: Bacterial biopesticides only affect certain types of pests while leaving harmless insects alone.
Compatibility with other techniques: Synthetic chemical biopesticides and the majority of bacterial insecticides can coexist without conflict.

Bacterial biopesticides are non-hazardous and can be used even when the crop is being harvested because they don't leave any residues behind. Natural persistence: Bacterial biopesticides can establish themselves and maintain their efficacy throughout several crop growing seasons.

div.head: Drawbacks of Microbial Insecticides
div.p: 1. Only particular species or groups of insects are harmed by microbial pesticides. They can successfully eradicate some pests while permitting others to live and possibly cause harm. 2. Synthetic pesticides may harm beneficial creatures when used in pest management programmes that make use of predators and parasitoids. As a result, it is essential to carefully choose the right chemical pesticides. 3. Several conditions, like as heat and ultraviolet rays, can affect how effective microbial pesticides are. As a result, it is suggested that you only use them in the morning.

4. Specific preparation and storage techniques are needed for microbial pesticides. To maintain the infections' virulence, it is essential to store microbial pesticides in accordance with the directions on the package. 5. The challenge of mass-multiplying natural competitors year-round restricts their marketability. Additionally, compared to chemical pesticides, the product registration process and a lack of availability restrict the use of biopesticides. 6. The effectiveness of biopesticides in the field is influenced by a variety of climatic conditions, including temperature, relative humidity, and rainfall.
The weather needs to be ideal for biocontrol agents to grow and survive in the field.

div.head: Role of viruses as biopesticides
div.p: Since baculoviruses (BV) are highly pathogenic to insects and other arthropods, they are frequently utilised as viral biopesticides. BVs are little viruses with double-stranded DNA that spread by parasitic reproduction. There are three subgroups of the genus Baculoviruses: cytoplasmic polyhedrosis viruses (CPVs), granulosis viruses (GVs), and nuclear polyhedrosis viruses (NPVs). These viruses fall under the inclusion virus category. The number of occlusion bodies they possess as well as the pattern of the protein coating around them can be used to identify them. In contrast to NPV and CPV, which make polyhedral bodies made up of several virus particles, GV produces granular occlusion bodies with a single viral particle. Different baculoviruses uncoil NPV, CPV, and GV in different ways. NPVs uncoat within the nucleus, CPVs uncoat in the cytoplasm, and GVs uncoat within the nuclear pore complex to infect the host. The occlusion bodies are essential to the virus' ability to survive outside of the host.

div.head: Mode of Action of Viruses
div.p: In terms of how they work, viruses operate similarly to bacteria in that they need to be consumed by insect larvae to cause illness. Once swallowed, the virus crosses the intestinal epithelium to enter the insect's body, where it causes systemic infection in the host cells. When it comes to the NPV virus, it passes through the intestinal epithelium and enters the hemocoel, fat body, and other tissue cells. It alters tissue integrity and ultimately causes the cadavers to liquefy. However, the infection brought on by GV is only present in the midgut of the insect (Fig. 1.2).

div.head: Fig. 2. Mode of action of virus
div.p: The Method for Preparing NPV and CPV Third-to fourth-instar Helicoverpa armigera larvae typically contract viruses by eating infected food. Over a period of 5 to 10 days, the viral disease's last stage manifests itself. The larvae ascend higher in the plant canopy before dying once the virus has completely infected them. Thousands of millions of polyhedra are now ejected by the infected larvae, assisting in the propagation of virus particles from the deceased animals to the canopy's underside. Through infecting healthy larvae, this behaviour promotes in the virus's spread.

In commercial production, larvae are either collected from the field or reared in a lab and fed a contaminated diet. The larvae are deprived for 24 hours prior to virus inoculation. The virus infects the larvae, which are suspended upsidedown in tubes, in three to four days. By being soaked in water, infected larvae are collected and disinfected. The cleaned larvae are then crushed in a crusher and pestle with 1:2.5 (w/v) sterile cooled distilled water. The homogenate is repeatedly rinsed with sterile distilled water in a ratio of 1:7.5 to 12.5 (w/v) based on the original weight of the cadaver larvae before being filtered through a double layer of muslin fabric. To separate the polyhedra from the waste, the filtrate is centrifuged. For Helicoverpa armigera larvae, the effective (LC50) of NPV is 1 x 109/ml. HNPV should be used to gramme, pigeon pea, and cotton at 250-300 LE, 500 LE, and 250 LE/ha, respectively. Viruses have a high level of host specificity and can significantly reduce host populations. Commercially available viral biopesticides include Cydia pomonella granulovirus (CpGV), Spodoptera exigua multi-enveloped nuclear polyhedrosis virus (SeMNPV), and Helicoverpa zea single-enveloped nuclear polyhedrosis virus (HzSNVP).
The efficacy of these biopesticides in pest management techniques has been demonstrated.

div.head: Disadvantages of Viral Biopesticides
div.p: 1. It can be used to deal with a range of pests, comparable to chemical pesticides. 2. A prolonged period of deadly infection is required for the target pests to be effectively controlled. 3. Viral inactivation can be caused by environmental conditions including ultraviolet radiation, extremely high temperatures, and others.

div.head: Fungi as Biopesticides
div.p: Due of the wide variety of hosts they can be found on in both field and storage settings, fungi-based biopesticides are extremely adaptable. The use of fungus-based biopesticides is widely recognised as safe and highly targeted. These goods come from parasitic or saprophytic fungi that thrive inside an insect's body and eat the interior tissues. Beauveria bassiana, Metarhizium anisopliae, Nomuraea rileyi, Trichoderma viride, Paecilomyces farinosus, and Verticillium lecanii are a few of the most widely used commercial fungal biopesticides on the market. These fungus-based biopesticides have proven successful at reducing pest populations. To maintain their own life, these fungi form either necessary or facultative relationships-symbiotic or antagonisticwith insects. Fungi must come into contact with the intended pests and then infect them in order to have a harmful effect. Aphids, beetles and their larvae, leaf and plant hoppers, pod borers, cutworms, grasshoppers, as well as other lepidopteran and coleopteran pests have all been successfully controlled by commercial formulations of Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae� (Table 1.4).

div.head: Fungi Host range Products Beauveria bassiana
div.p: Effective against a variety of insect pests, including fire ants, rickets, white grubs, plant bugs, grasshoppers, flea beetles, mites, mosquito larvae, aphids, fungus gnats, whiteflies, and stored pest Botani Gard, Naturalis, Racer BB, Ostrinil, Brocari l, Mycotrol, Boverin, Naturalis-L, Mycotrol-0 BotaniGard, Naturalis-H&G Naturalis-T&O, Mycotrol,

div.head: Metarhizium anisopliae
div.p: Effective against a wide range of insect pests. Black vine weevil, locusts and grasshoppers Bio-Blast, Bio-Path, Green Muscle, Metaquino Pacer.

div.head: Effective against Rot disease
div.p: Ecosom TV, Tricon, Trieco

div.head: Trichoderma harzianum
div.p: Effective against a variety of soil pathogens Rootshield, BioTrek, Supresivit

div.head: Mode of Action of Biopesticides based on Fungi
div.p: The targeted pest dies as a result of the fungi's attack because their mycelium penetrates the insect's cuticle and enters the hemocoel. Conidia, or fungal spores, can cling to the host's cuticle due to their hydrophobic adhesion.

Conidia germination happens when a germ tube grows filamentously or when an appressorium forms. The cuticle is penetrated by fungi using hydrolytic enzymes. Additionally, they release poisons, organic acids, secondary metabolites, antimicrobial substances with detoxifying effects, and secondary metabolites. The insect dies when fungus spores cross the bug's cuticle and enter its hemocoel. The internal mycelium then continues to develop and eventually breaks through the insect corpse's outer layer, where it sporulates. Benefits of Biopesticides Derived from Fungi 1. Wide range of hosts: Fungi are more capable of infecting a variety of pests than bacteria and viruses, including those found in fields, storage facilities, and soil. 2. Ease of mass production with low substrate requirements is possible with commercially important fungi like Beauveria, Metarhizium, Lecanicillium, and Isaria. 3. Outstanding efficacy and target specificity: Fungi-based biopesticides exhibit extraordinary efficacy and specificity. 4. Ecologically sound: The nature of these biopesticides is ecologically sound.

Disadvantages of Fungi-Based Biopesticides 1. Ideal environmental requirements: Fungi are quite sensitive to their surroundings. Arthropod cuticle penetration and spore germination both require humidity levels of 80% or greater. 2. Both temperature and UV exposure affect the survival of fungi. 3. Difficulties exist in the creation and stabilisation of fragile conidia or robust resting phases. 4. In comparison to other microbiological techniques, it is a more expensive choice.

div.head: The Utilization of Entomopathogenic Nematodes (EPN) as Biopesticides
div.p: Unsegmented roundworms with a soft body known as entomopathogenic nematodes are naturally present in the water layer that surrounds soil particles. These worms, which are either facultative or obligatory parasites, use a variety of sensory cues to find their hosts, including vibrations, chemical signals, and carbon dioxide. Two nematode families, Steinernematidae and Heterorhabditidae, have been effectively identified and used as biological controls in the field of pest management programmes (Kachhawa, 2017).
Products including S� carpocapsae, S� thermophilum, H� bacteriophora, and H� indica are registered and marketed in India.

div.head: Mode of Action of EPN
div.p: 1. The mouth, anus, or breathing holes of an insect serve as natural entry points for the third stage juvenile non-feeding infected insects (0.4-1.5 mm long). 2. Nematodes enter the insect's body cavity and discharge symbiotic bacteria into the intestine of the host. The toxins these bacteria create cause the insect to die within a week. 3. The nematodes liquefy the host's remnants as they feed on the dead body. On the host cadaver, they multiply and create three generations of offspring. 4. The infectious juvenile nematodes eventually leave the dead host insect after infecting it and actively look for a new host. Nematode-infected pest stages ought to appear about 5-7 days following administration under ideal circumstances. Steinernematidae cause the death of insects that are brown or tan in colour, while Heterorhabditidae cause the death of red-colored insects. However, it's not always possible to see the presence of dead insects (Fig. 1.5). 4. Nematodes show resistance to a variety of chemical and biological insecticides, fungicides, herbicides, fertilisers, and growth regulators when exposed for brief periods of time (between 2 and 24 hours).

div.head: Drawbacks of EPN
div.p: 1. Nematodes are difficult to regulate because they are extremely sensitive to environmental conditions like UV radiation and high temperatures. 2. Nematodes are typically more expensive than standard chemical pesticides.

In a natural system, however, the population of nematodes and insect hosts typically maintains a balance. 3. If the worm population rises above this natural equilibrium, the insects may be wiped out, but the nematodes will then start to drop in number until the insect populations rise again. Therefore, nematode reapplication is necessary. 4. Nematodes are unable to infect insects that are mostly found on plants because they are unable to survive above the soil. Nematode formulations also only have a short shelf life.

5. Early in the morning or late at night treatment is advised to protect the nematodes from UV light. 6. In order to hydrate the soil and make it easier for nematodes to travel into the soil, pre-and post-irrigation are both required.

div.head: Use of protozoans as biopesticides
div.p: Microsporidians, commonly referred to as protozoans, are ubiquitous intracellular parasites that can infect lepidopteran and orthopteran insects. They can therefore be used in programmes for integrated pest management. These protozoans include Nosema sp. and Vairimorpha sp. as examples.

Despite the fact that they are pest-specific The use of protozoa as biopesticides has not been as successful as that of other species like bacteria, viruses, and fungi because they do not have the same pervasive and incapacitating effects on their targets. A list of successful commercially viable protozoan-based biopesticides can be found in Table 1.6.

div.head: Mode of Action of Protozoans
div.p: 1. To contract microsporidia, insects must eat them.

2. The microsporidian parasite preys on the Ostrinia nubilalis, also known as the European maize borer. 3. The microsporidia spores germinate in the midgut after ingestion, and the sporoplasm is then injected into the midgut cells. 4. The spores then spread to numerous tissues and organs, proliferating and causing tissue damage as well as the emergence of the disease known as septicaemia (Senthil-Nathan, 2015).

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The term "biological control" was first used by Smith in 1919 to signify the use of natural enemies (whether introduced or otherwise manipulated) to control insect pests. DeBach (1964) defined biological control as the action of parasites, predators, or pathogens that maintain another organism's population density at a lower average than would occur in their absence (Dhaliwal and Arora, 2016). According to a recent definition by Eilenberg et al. (2001) biological control or biocontrol is "The use of living organisms to suppress the population density or impact of a specific pest organism, making it less abundant or less damaging than it would otherwise be." The definition clearly states that 'living organisms' are used.

The main aim of biological management is to effective suppression of pest population by using the living agents. Biological control, in their opinion, is a process in which one species population reduces the numbers of another species through processes such as predation, parasitism, disease, or competition. Biological management is one of the best options for the sustainable management of insect. Some invasive species pest or non-native pests -species are travels from one place to another and their natural enemies are left behind, to control them and enable their spread, biological control has been an effective way to control invasive species or non-natives (CABI, 2022). Biological management should be understood as an integral component of IPM and an important part of pest-suppressing methods. (Prasad, 2019).

div.head: Why Biological management is necessary?
div.p: Biological management is an important practise in IPM. Bio pesticides are typically more target-specific and less hazardous to insects, birds, animals, and mammals than broad-spectrum conventional pesticides. They are often biodegradable in the natural environment, decreasing pollution and the likelihood of pests acquiring resistance to them. These compounds have high host specificity, usually limiting themselves to a few closely related insect species. These agents have no effect on other insect groups (natural enemies), plants, animals, or people. It is a more environmentally friendly and effective method of plant protection.

div.head: Major Characteristics of Natural Enemies
div.p: i) They should kill, reduce the rate of reproduction, inhibit growth, or shorten pest life. ii) They are usually specific to target species or to specific damaging life stages. iii) Their effectiveness may be affected by environmental conditions or host abundance. iv) The degree of control by naturally occurring pathogens may be variable. v) They are relatively slow acting; they may take several days ormore to provide sufficient management.

vi) They have the potential to generate epizootic outbreaks. vii) Microbial are non-toxic to humans in the conventional sense, safety precautions should be followed to minimize exposure. viii) They are non-toxic to animals and Eco-friendly in nature.

div.head: Naturally Occurring Bio-Control Agent
div.p: Natural biocontrol is the reduction of a pest population by its natural enemies, without human intervention. The insect has several naturally occurring 'natural enemies' or 'farmer's friends', these biological control agents are organisms that feed on insect's egg, larval, pupal and adult stage.A major challenge is to develop conditions that allow these beneficial organisms to reach their full potential.

div.head: Parasitoids
div.p: Parasitoids are free-living organisms whose adults lay eggs inside or attached to a single host organism and their immature stage develops by feeding on or in a single insect host after which they are fully grown and pupate and eventually kill the host. The adults may also feed on other resources, such as honeydew, plant nectar or pollen. The parasitoids must be adapted to the life cycle, physiology and defences of their hosts species so that they have narrow host range and many are highly specialized such co-evolved parasitoids can exert a strong impact on populations of the insect and are thus good candidates and accurate identification of the host and parasitoid species is critical in the use of biological management programmes.

div.head: Goniozus nephantidis Campoletis chloridae Brachymeria nephantidis Aphelinus mail
Types of Parasitism (Prasad, 2019) 1. Simple parasitism
div.p: Simple parasitism refers to a single parasitoid attack on the host, regardless of the number of eggs laid. Ex: Goniozus nephantidis on the larvae of coconut black headed caterpillar.

div.head: Hyper parasitism
div.p: A parasitoid that preys on another parasitoid. It is parasitism in the form of a parasite. In actually, the hyper parasite is a secondary parasite. These reduce the primary efficiency of primary parasite. Ex:

i) The moth Hyphantria (primary host) is firstly parasitized by the dipterantachnid larvae, Ernestia (primary parasitoid) and it is, thereafter parasitized bythe chalcid larvae, Perilampus, which is a hyper parasitoid.
ii) Aphid is primarily parasitized by the ichneumon, Aphidius (primary parasite) and after wards parasitized by the cynip gall fly, Charips or chalcid, Asaphesfly, which are hyper parasitoids.

iii) Aphanogmus fijiensis is a hyper parasitoid on Cotesia flavipes cocoons, which isa primary parasitoid of Chilo partellus. iv) The cabbage butterfly larvae Pieris (primary host) are parasitized first by hymenopteran Apanteles glomeratus (primary parasite) and it thereafter, parasitized by another hymenopteran-Natrrasticus rape which is a hyper parasitoid.

div.head: Multiple parasitisms
div.p: Parasitism by different species of parasitoids on the same host at a time is called multiple parasitisms. Ex: i) Bracon brevicornis, Eriborus trochanteratus and Goniozus nephantidis attack larvae of black headed caterpillar at the same time. ii) Chalcids, Eurytomamonemae and Chrysisshanghaiensis attack the fully grown caterpillar Monema flavescens at the same time. iii) Fruit fly, Dacus cucurbitae larvae is parasitized first by the braconid,

Opius flatcheri larvae and later on by the 2 nd parasite chalcid. Tetrastichus giffardianus. iv) Parasitization of lepidopteran eggs by two primary parasitoids, Trichogramma sp. and Telenomus sp. at the same time.

div.head: Super parasitism
div.p: Many individuals of the same species of parasitoid attack a single host at a time is called super parasitism. Ex: i) Trichospilus pupivora on the pupae of black headed caterpillar ii) Aphid parasitized by the braconid, Aphidius larvae iii) Caterpillar Artona is parasitized by the larvae of Chalcid, Elachertus

div.head: Clepto parasitism
div.p: A type of parasitism in which the adult parasitoid preferentially appropriates for its own progeny the previously paralyzed and parasitized host of another parasitoid.
Ex: Pine shoot moth attacked by either Eurytoma pini or Rhyacionia buoliana

div.head: Auto parasitism
div.p: It is also called as Adelpho parasitism. A special type of parasitism in which the female develops as a primary parasitoid, but the male is a secondary parasitoid through females of its own species.Ex : i) Males of Coccophagoides utilis develop as hyper parasite from unfertilized egg laid by parent female of C� utilis a primary parasitoid of purple citrus scale, Parlartoria oleae.
ii) Chalcidoid, Encarisa formosa females develop on the whitefly while males become internal hyper parasites of the larvae females of their own species.

div.head: Introduction of natural enemies
div.p: The natural enemy should be imported from location with similar climate conditions. It should be free of other hosts. It must be free of hyperparasites and other biological competitors to the hosts. Physiological suitability of the natural enemy to its host and suitability of the host plant ecosystem. The dispersal ability of an imported natural enemy should be limited.

div.head: Colonization
div.p: Colonisation is a mechanism for manipulating an organism to establish it in a new location. Colonisation is the most important activity in biological control, and its success is dependent on it.

The colonisation location should be completely sheltered from pesticides and cultural practises. To reduce activity, the natural enemy should be stored at a low temperature (16°C). Most parasitoids and predators regard adult stages to be more appropriate than juvenile stages because adults can quickly adapt to new environments and are less impacted by biotic and abiotic stresses. Natural enemies should be released in the field near an appropriate host. Natural enemies should be delivered in a container with suitable relative humidity.

To avoid direct heat, they must be released early in the morning or late in the evening. Before releasing natural enemies, they should be fed and mated. The number released should be determined by the natural enemy's ability to find hosts. Recovery attempts are made soon after the original release to determine if the released natural enemies are alive and under control.

div.head: Evaluation of natural enemies
div.p: Before evaluating the results, a minimum of three years is required. The efficiency of the natural enemy is assessed by comparing the pest's host population before and after release; correlating natural enemy and its host population fluctuations; contrasting the population on plots with and without a natural enemy.

They are two crucial systems that are commonly used to assess the efficiency of a natural enemy. i) Procedures for conducting experiments (Natural enemy exclusion method) ii) Analytical techniques (application of the life table analysis approach) Exclusion of natural enemies can be accomplished using the exclusion method by hand picking chemical barriers of insecticides and the employment of ants. Life and mortality, as well as the causes of death (life table), are determined through analytical techniques based on numerous environmental elements for distinct stages. The data is analysed to determine the relationship between pest density and variations in density, as well as the quantity of death caused by each measured mortality component.

div.head: Augmentation
div.p: It involves all activities aimed at increasing the number or impact of current natural enemies. These goals can be met by introducing more natural enemies into a system or byaltering the environment to encourage higher numbers or effectiveness. These releases differ from the introduction in that they must be repeated on a regular basis. Periodic releases are possible.

div.head: I. Inoculative releases
div.p: These can be created only once a year to re-establish natural enemies. Control is expected to originate from the progeny and later generations, rather than from the release itself.

div.head: II. Inundative releases
div.p: It includes mass culturing and release of natural enemies to directly decrease the pest population. These are helpful against pests that have a single or a few distinct generations each year. The control in this case comes from the release itself.

div.head: Conservation
div.p: The most commonly employed biological control method in IPM is conservation, which is defined as activities taken to preserve and increase natural enemies through environmental management. It is possible to conserve bio-control agents via.

div.head: I. Rationalizing use of pesticides
div.p: Pesticides repel or reduce the longevity, fertility, and searching abilities of natural enemies and reduce the population of flying adults. These can be prevented by using non-persistent pesticides, skipping row treatments with pesticides, using safe and selective pesticides, using the least recommended dose, applying at specified times in a limited area, and using microbial pesticides instead of synthetic pesticides.

div.head: II. Providing food and shelter
div.p: Nesting boxes and bird perches provide suited areas for predators to gather.

Plants that produce pollen and nectar are planted on the bunds to offer predators with refuge and additional food. Because natural enemies multiply in unsprayed niches, parasitoids might be introduced there to form reservoirs.

div.head: III.Beneficial management practices
div.p: • Chisel ploughing protects natural enemies of soil-dwelling bugs. The ability of entomopathogenic fungal infections to be more effective is enhanced by manipulating relative humidity and wetness periods. • Organic manure use protects predaceous arthropods and soil-borne microbial antagonists of insect pests. If crop wastes are not burned, some parasitoids may be preserved. Parasitoids should be released when hyperparasites activity is minimum. • Retaining crop stubble and grass weed heaps helps to maintain the predator populations.

• Keep a portion of the refuge in net mesh cages to allow parasitoids to emerge in the field. • Overwintering parasitoids or their eggs are collected and housed in cages, and parasitoids that emerge from these cages are used early in the season.

div.head: IV. Suppression of ants
div.p: Ant suppression or physical banishment increases the efficiency of predators.

div.head: V. Determining the need for action
div.p: The natural enemy's time and period of attack should synchronize with the availability of an appropriate stage of the pest. The necessity for intervention must be evaluated for each crop ecosystem based on the population of natural enemies and their ability to feed or parasitize. When parasitoids are active predators, no parasitoids should be released; similarly, when entomo pathogenic are active parasitoids, no parasitoids should be released for effective treatment.

div.head: VI. Influence of plant types
div.p: The pubescence of plant leaves influences searching efficiency, parasitism, oviposition, and prey consumption. The colour of the leaf and flower bud attracts or repels parasitoids. By offering shelter and food, varieties with more blooms, a larger canopy, and more nectaries boost the efficacy of natural enemies. The morphological traits of the host plant, as well as the changes in volatile content profiles between cultivars, have a crucial impact in the predators' and parasitoids' efficiency.

div.head: VII. Trap cropping
div.p: Growing trap crops alongside the main crop boosts the overall efficiency of parasitoids and predators.

div.head: Predators
div.p: Predators are free-living organisms that must consume various preys to complete their life cycle. They prefer to feed on prey that is smaller than them. Insect predators include coccinellid beetles, ground beetles, anthocorid bugs, syrphid flies, and predatory mites. Coleoptera, Neuroptera, Hymenoptera, Diptera, Hemiptera, and Odonata are the most common insect orders that prey on other insects and mites. Moreover, some kinds of mites and spiders feed on a wide variety of insects and mites. Some predators, such as praying mantids, dragonflies, and beetles, utilise biting or chewing mouth parts to eat their prey, whilst others, such as assassin bugs, lacewing larvae, and hover fly larvae, use piercing and sucking mouth parts to feed on their prey's body fluids. Sucking feeders frequently inject a potent poison that swiftly immobilises their prey (Dhaliwal and Arora, 2016). Adult predators might be monophagous (only feed on one species), oligophagous (feed on several species), or polyphagous (feed on many species). Polyphagous predators, if extremely mobile, may be efficient in pest management in disturbed environments, whereas monophagous or oligophagous predators tend to be connected with prey in more stable systems. While many predators are carnivorous as juveniles and adults, others also eat on nectar, honeydew, or plant food. Female predators frequently deposit their eggs close to their prey. Predators frequently utilise visual and chemical cues to locate plants with prey, while prey pheromones may also be used as cues.

Predators frequently concentrate on more common prey species, which may make them less efficient at controlling a pest at low densities but may make them especially successful at suppressing pest outbreaks (Dhaliwal and Arora, 2016).

div.head: Entomopathogens
div.p: Entomopathogens are microorganisms that cause disease in arthropods such as insects, mites, and ticks and play a vital role in their control. Several naturally occurring bacteria, fungi, nematodes, and viruses infect and cause disease in a range of arthropod pests; this disease may lower the feeding rate, growth, and metabolic activity of insect pests, as well as slow or prevent reproduction, or kill them. Diseases can reproduce and spread spontaneously across an insect population under specific environmental conditions, particularly when the insect population density is high. (Stoner). Some entomopathogens are commercially produced in vitro (bacteria, fungi, and nematodes) or in vivo (nematodes and viruses).The use of entomopathogens as biopesticides in pest management is known as microbial control, and it can be an important aspect of integrated pest management (IPM) against a variety of pests (Mary Barbercheck, 2009).

The active ingredient in this form of pesticide is a microorganism such as a virus, fungus, protozoan, or bacterium. Each microbe has a unique active ingredient for controlling certain pests, yet microbial pesticides can control or kill a wide range of pests that harm agricultural production. One fungus can control weeds while another can control or kill insects and pests (Nazir et al�, 2020).

div.head: Bacteria
div.p: Entomopathogenic bacteria are classified as spore formers and non-spore formers (Ex: Pseudomonas spp.), which are further separated into obligate spore formers (Ex: Bacillus popillae) and facultative spore formers. The facultative spore formers could be crystal forming or non-crystalliferous (for example, Bacillus cereus). Spore-forming bacteria are more commonly used in pest control than non-spore-forming bacteria. Because of the deadly nature of the crystals they create, the crystalliferous spore formers are more commonly utilised for pest control than the non-crystalliferous spore formers. Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is the most studied crystalliferous bacteria example. Ex. Bacillus thuringiensis var. kurstaki used against Lepidoptera Bacillus thuringiensis var. galleraie used against wax moth Bacillus thuringiensis var. sandiego and Bacillus thuringiensis var. tenebrionis used against beetles and weevils

div.head: Viruses
div.p: Baculoviruses are the most frequent and extensively researched group of viruses that are harmful to insects. Except for Autographa californica nuclear polyhedrosis virus, Baculoviruses are species or genus specific. Viruses are obligatory parasites, which mean they require a host to multiply. These are mostly made up of double-stranded DNA. A polyhedron-shaped protein coat protects an infective baculovirus particle (virion). The presence of polyhedral shaped viral occlusions (polyhedra) containing randomly occluded viral particles distinguishes the genus NPV. Polyhedroses are distinguished by the production of polyhedron-shaped inclusion bodies in the host's diseased tissues. Granuloses virus (GV) has one nucleocapsid per envelope and granule-shaped viral occlusions (capsules) that contain one or, in rare cases, two or more virions.Non-occluded baculoviruses have one nucleocapsid per envelope and no occlusion structures. Baculovirus infection begins when an insect consumes virus particles on a plant. Oryctes rhinoceros virus infection causes Malayan illness in the rhinoceros beetle, Oryctes rhinoceros.

Ex. Amsacta albistriga and A� moorei NPVs used to suppress red hairy caterpillars on groundnut Ha -NPV used against Helicoverpa armigera on chickpea and groundnut SI-NPV is used to control Spodoptera litura on tobacco, cotton and black gram Chilo infuscutellus GV is used to against shoot borer on sugarcane

div.head: Fungi
div.p: Entomopathogenic fungi (EPF) reproduce both sexually and asexually in their insect hosts. In its asexual form it produces spores known as conidia which are wind dispersed. Once they are released they may land upon another insect host, or once again return to the soil where they reproduce sexually. Insect-pathogenic fungi usually need moisture to enable infection, and natural epizootics are most common during wet or humid conditions. The effectiveness of these fungi against pest insects depends on having the correct fungal species and strain with the susceptible insect life stage, at the appropriate humidity, soil texture (to reach ground-dwelling pest species), and temperature. The fungal spores, which can be carried by wind or water, must contact the pest insect to cause infection. Unlike bacterial and viral pathogens of insects, fungal pathogens infect the insect with contact and do not need to be consumed by their host to cause infection. Ex. Beauveria bassiana causes a disease to European corn borer, codling moth, cabbage caterpillar and whitefly and the disease known as the white muscardine.
Metarhizium spp. causes a disease known as green muscardine. It is effective against rootworm, white grubs, and some root weevils.
Verticillium lecanii is used against whitefly and thrips and aphids.
Nomuraea rileyi is used against green clover worm, cabbage looper, cabbage worm, armyworms, corn earworm, and tobacco budworm.
Hirsutella thompsonii and Neozygites floridana are used against mites.

div.head: Rickettsiae
div.p: The pathogenic rickettsiae differ mainly from bacteria in that they are Gram negative, obligate intracellular pathogens with typical bacterial cell walls and no flagella. Rickettsia is a genus of non-motile, Gram-negative, nonspore forming, highly pleomorphic bacteria that can present as cocci (0.1 um in diameter), rods (1-4 μm long) or thread -like (10 µm long). Obligate intracellular parasites, the Rickettsia survival depends on entry, growth, and replication within the cytoplasm of eukaryotic host cells (typically endothelial cells). Because of this, Rickettsia cannot live in artificial nutrient environments and are grown either in tissue or embryo cultures.
Ex. Rickettsiella popilliae is used against Japanese beetle causing blue disease.
Rickettsiella melolontha is used against scarabaeid beetles.

div.head: Nematode
div.p: Entomopathogenic nematodes (EPNs) are soil-inhabiting, lethal insect parasitoids, live inside the body of their host, and so they are designated endoparasitic. The most commonly studied genera are those that are useful in the biological control of insect pests, the Steinernematidae and Heterorhabditidae.
Ex. Steinernema capocapsae used against Lepidopterous larvae. Heterorhabditis bacteriophora used against Soil-dwelling insect

div.head: Protozoa
div.p: The pathogen enters the insect body through the gut wall, spreads to different tissues and organs, and multiplies, sometimes causing tissue breakdown and septicemia. These are relatively slow acting organisms, taking days or weeks to debilitate their host. Protozoa kill the insects either directly or by reducing the fecundity of the adult. Their effect on host is chronic. They prolong the larval life in the field, thus exposing the insect longer to predators and parasitoids. These are called debilitating infections. They are always associated with other pathogens.
Ex. Vairimorpha necatrix is used against corn earworm and European cornborer, various armyworms, fall webworm, and cabbage looper.
Nosema melolonthae is used against chaffer beetles. Farinocystis triboli is used against red flour beetle.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Biological control of insect pest means the use of natural enemies such as parasitoids, predators and entomopathogens to suppress their population and damage caused by them. The most important and commonly used insect parasitoids include Trichogramma sp., Telenomus sp., Chelonus sp., Bracon sp., Goniozus sp., and Epiricamia melanoleuca. Ladybird beetles, ground beetles, tiger beetles, green lacewings, Preying mantids, reduviid bug, damselflies, robber fly, and hoverflies are key insect predators. Spiders and some mite groups are also insect and mite pest predators. Entomopathogens are disease-causing bacteria, fungi, protozoans, and viruses. Natural enemies were introduced into the environment and their potential was expanded by encouraging them to multiply. Natural enemies are more likely to survive when a non-toxic control strategy is utilised. Bio-control is an important part of integrated pest management and helps to reduce the use of synthetic chemical pesticides. Biocontrol of insect reduces risk of resistance development in insect, high efficiency, low cost, eco-friendly, safety for beneficial organisms, reduction of residues in environment, target-specific in nature and increased biodiversity in human managed ecosystems.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The paper summarises recent findings on nematodes, viruses, bacteria, and other entomopathogenic microorganisms (EM) and their potential application in biological pest control. It's important to carefully research the ecological characteristics of EM and their function in nature in order to utilise various biological control tactics against insect hosts efficiently. Since it identifies frequent patterns in the interactions between insect pests and EM that have just lately been uncovered through research, this chapter aids readers in understanding the significant advancements in this sector. Weeds, arthropods (mainly insects and mites), mollusks (slugs and snails), some vertebrates, and plant pathogens (oomycetes, fungus, bacteria, nematodes, and viruses) are only a few examples of the diverse species that are considered agricultural pests. These pests reduce agricultural output and product quality by munching on crops. It is estimated that there are millions of pest species in the globe, and they have a significant detrimental influence on agricultural output, decreasing ideal world crop yields by 40%. Insect pests and mites serve as hosts for a number of naturally occurring fungus, bacteria, nematodes, and viruses that infect a range of organisms. Insect pests alone are thought to be responsible for 10.80% of all agricultural losses experienced globally in the years after the "green revolution." Up to 80% of the time, the intensive use of pesticides has resulted in the development of insecticide resistance to one or more modes of action. Entomopathogens, which include bacteria, viruses, protozoa, and fungi, are therefore thought of as controlling factors in pest infestations.

Insect population management relies heavily on naturally occurring entomopathogens. Tanzini et al� (2001) used the term "entomopathogens" to refer to microorganisms that control insect pest populations to levels that do not result in any financial harm to crops. Delgado and Murcia (2011) expanded the definition of the term to include populations of microorganisms that prey on pests by invading them, disrupting their life cycles, and using them as hosts. These microbes were categorised as either facultative or obligate parasites with high survival potential that prey on insect pests. In addition to being successful against insect pests, using entomopathogenic microorganisms (EM) as pest management agents is also environmentally safe, with reduced pesticide residues for both people and non-target creatures. Natural enemies are crucial in preventing pests in an agro-ecosystem from reaching the economic injury level (EIL). Depending on the type of pest population and the biological features of the control agent, biological control agents (BCAs) are applied in a variety of methods. BCAs have a number of enticing qualities, including host specificity, the ability to control toxic residues, the lack of phytotoxic effects, safety for humans, and the possibility for long-term insect pest control. However, the natural enemy and the ecology of the pest are necessary for effective BCA use. It is necessary to create effective biological control agents using entomopathogens (viruses, fungus, bacteria, and nematodes), which calls for a complete understanding of bioassay techniques as well as manufacturing, formulation, and application methods.

div.head: Entomopathogenic Fungi
div.p: Worldwide, entomopathogenic fungi (EPF) are essential to biological pest management. EPF are heterotrophic, eukaryotic microorganisms that may reproduce sexually and asexually, exist as unicellular or multicellular (filaments), and produce a variety of infectious propagules. Environmental factors including UV radiation, temperature, and humidity have an impact on the effectiveness of EPF in the field. Among those with the greatest EPF (Entomophthoromycota) diversity are the orders Onygenales (Ascosphaera genus), Entomophthorales, Hypocreales, and Neozygitales. Important entomopathogenic taxa include Beauveria, Metarhizium, Nomuraea, Entomophthora, Verticillium, and Neozygites, which are found in a variety of taxonomic groups. EPF can have an impact on insects from the orders Coleoptera, Lepidoptera, Hemiptera, Orthoptera, Diptera, and Hymenoptera. Few fungi, like those in the family Hypocreales, can potentially live on a variety of hosts, but others, like those in the genus Entomophthorales, are only found on certain kinds of insects. Lepidopterous larvae, aphids, and thrips are examples of entomopathogenic fungi that have been seen to infect several mite and insect pest species. These pests are significant economic agricultural pests in many countries. Due to their host-specificity, EPF rarely harm non-target species while developing deadly illnesses and controlling insect and mite populations in nature.

The appressoria, which are specialised structures, are generally covered in a mucus layer of proteins and glucans and are created when the spores adhere to the insect cuticle. The mechanical pressure and enzyme activity (lipases, proteases, and chitinases) in the germ tube are what cause the insect cuticle to penetrate. Most EPF develop vegetatively inside the hemocoel of the insect. Toxins created and released by an infection or mechanical harm caused by mycelia developing inside an insect (mummification) are the main reasons of insect mortality. Toxins such destruxin, bavericin, and efrapeptins are secreted by Beauveria, Metarhizium, and Tolypocladium; their actions and contributions to the pathogenesis process are well characterised. The fungus continues its life cycle on new hosts after the insect dies by producing hundreds to thousands of fresh spores on the deceased carcass. Bemisia tabaci discovered that the host plant had an impact on the pathogenesis of the fungus B� bassiana (Hypocreales: Cordycipitaceae) to B� tabaci� B� bassiana is known for its pathogenicity to the sweet potato. The mortality brought on by B� bassiana as well as the development of newly generated conidia were significantly influenced by the host plant on which the nymphs were raised.

div.head: Entomopathogenic Bacteria
div.p: In general, biological control techniques, such as using bacterial entomopathogens, are thought to be safer than chemical pesticides and promise enormous financial gain. Their mode of action is often more complex than that of conventional pesticides, and they tend to target a variety of locations where resistant bugs are more likely to develop. Entomopathogenic bacteria can be used as a stand-alone pest management tool, but to achieve optimal efficiency and ecological safety, they work best when used in rotation or in conjunction with insecticides. Numerous studies have shown that synthetic pesticides and enteropathogenic microorganisms work well together.

Incorporating biopesticides into pest management programmes has the added advantages of protecting workers, reducing crop residues, allowing for more flexibility in harvesting due to a short or nonexistent pre-harvest time, and protecting the environment. Families include Bacillaceae, Streptococcaceae, Pseudomonadaceae, Enterobacteriaceae, and Micrococcaceae are home to the bulk of bacterial diseases that affect insects. Some of these bacteria are extremely virulent, but the majority of them are soft pathogens that spread to stressed insects and infect or afflict them. Bacillaceae has been the subject of the most speculation among them. While Bacillus sphaericus is effective against mosquitoes, Bacillus popillae causes milky sickness in scarabaeids.

In order to control caterpillars and beetles, Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt.) is frequently employed as an entomopathogenic agent. Bt. is a bacterium that forms spores, and the generation of proteinaceous protoxin crystals with insecticidal characteristics is correlated with the spores' creation. In the insect's stomach, the eaten crystals disintegrate and release endotoxin, an active pollutant. When consumed, special insecticidal endotoxins known as Cry proteins generate pores that harm the insect stomach epithelium. Bt. creates parasporal (crystal-like) structures with these endotoxins. Cry toxins that are marketed commercially have shown to be effective against a variety of insect pests, including as Lepidoptera, Coleoptera, and Diptera species, with little to no direct impact on unintended creatures. A study on the use of genetically modified Bt to suppress Leptinotarsa decemlineata (Coleoptera: Chrysomelidae) revealed no effect on non-target organisms such predatory Hemiptera. Bt. bioinsecticides applied to agricultural ecosystems and other habitats typically do not boost spore production, and their viability tends to decline, especially when exposed to sunshine. A microbial control treatment called Bt is sprayed on insect infestations to quickly kill the target insects and, in most cases, prevent them from reproducing. It's crucial to take the risk to species other than the targets into account when assessing the security and ecological effects of EPBs. Some plants have the Bt-endotoxin-encoding genes, making them resistant to a variety of insect pests. According to farmer surveys, growing Bt crops greatly reduces the need for conventional pesticides.

However, some problems have emerged, such as worries about the mirid insect on Bt cotton in China. A few years after Bt cotton became widely used and failed to control the mirid pests that were previously controlled by a wide range of insecticides, problems with the pests began to arise in China. Since they became commercially available, genetically modified (GM) maize and cotton plants that express lepidopteran-active endotoxins have transformed agriculture in the nations where they are grown. Currently, GM crops are grown in eight countries: Brazil, Paraguay, Argentina, South Africa, the United States, Canada, and China. GM crops do have certain drawbacks.

div.head: Entomopathogenic Viruses
div.p: Entomopathogenic viruses (EPV), commonly referred to as insect-killing viruses, have recently come to be recognised as important global contributors to biological pest management. The first virus-based insecticide to control the cotton bollworm was approved in the USA in 1970, despite the fact that several viruses were investigated for controlling insect pests in the early 1900s. There are now several viruses that are authorised for use in the management of insect pests, and continuing research attempts to find and assess additional viruses (López-Ferber, 2020). Numerous insect orders have been shown to contain EPVs, which can target a wide variety of insects. They serve as effective biological control agents, especially when used against insect pests that might contract viral diseases. EPVs can include either RNA (dsRNA and ssRNA) or double-or single-stranded DNA (dsDNA and ssDNA). Since the sixteenth century, insect pathogens known as EPVs have been reported. They have proven effective against a variety of plant pests in agro-ecosystems around the world. Numerous viral illnesses, including nucleopolyhedrosis, have been identified in honeybees (Apidae, Hymenoptera: Bombycidae), Bombyx mori L., silkworms, and other Lepidopteran species. Viral particles are made up of nucleic acids encased in a protein covering called a capsid, which is crucial to the host cell's infection process. Once a virus has subtly entered a cell, its nucleic acid seizes control of the host metabolic system and begins to produce numerous copies of itself until the cell inevitably perishes. Because they are obligatory parasites, viruses cannot grow in vitro. The 12 viral families into which EPVs are divided by the International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses (ICTV). Three of them-Polydnaviridae, Baculoviridae, and Ascoviridae are insect-specific families that are extremely host-specific and nonpathogenic to beneficial insects and other non-target animals, including mammals. Particularly the Baculoviridae family has been regarded as an environmentally safe substitute for chemical insecticides. There are two types of baculovirus (dsDNA) in the family Baculoviridae: granulovirus (GV) and nucleopolyhedrovirus (NPV), which are particularly important for suppressing lepidopteran pests on crops. The genomes of both groups are circular doublestranded DNAs. In both insect cells and human cells, the baculovirus has been extensively employed to make a variety of recombinant proteins. In terms of external symptoms, each group of EPVs exhibits different manifestations.

div.head: Entomopathogenic Nematodes
div.p: Entomopathogenic nematodes (EPNs) are roundworms with a soft body and no segments that are obligatory parasites of insects. They are about 0.5 mm in length and come from 23 different worm families. Heterorhabditidae and Steinernematidae, two EPN families, have been used successfully in pest control programmes as biological pesticides. These nematodes are found naturally in soil and hunt for their insect victims in response to chemical cues like CO2. EPNs made for sale are utilised as biological control agents for a variety of pest insects that live in soil. EPNs and bacteria interact in a mutualistic symbiotic way that aids in the biological control of insect pests. Since they are easily mass-produced and deployed using standard spray equipment, they are environmentally benign and adapt well to a variety of situations. Infectious juveniles enter the hemocoel of their host insect once inside and release symbiotic bacteria that have been stored in the nematode's gut. These bacteria cause septicemia, which kills the host in 24 to 48 hours.

Then, infectious juveniles feed on the bacterial populations that are expanding quickly and the rotting host tissues. The nematode goes through two to three generations inside the host corpse. As bacteria serve as food, the symbiotic connection with bacteria promotes nematode proliferation and pathogenicity. By introducing bacteria into the host, nematodes serve as vectors that provide the necessary circumstances for nematode survival and reproduction in the insect body. EPNs are well known for having the potential to be used as a biological control agent in plant protection, particularly those that belong to the Steinernematidae and Heterorhabditidae families. All Heterorhabditis nematode species are connected to Photorhabdus bacteria, but all Steinernema spp. are connected to Xenorhabdus bacteria. Heterorhabditids and steinernematids are widely distributed in soils all over the world. Their usefulness against different pest insects has been well studied. The mass manufacturing of EPNs can be carried out via in vivo or in vitro techniques. For example, Galleria mellonella larvae (Lepidoptera: Pyralidae) are frequently utilised as a substitute host to develop nematodes, and a liquid fermentation technique is used to produce nematodes on a big scale. In order to cultivate EPNs in big quantities for use in industry, huge fermenters are employed to expose worms to a pure culture of their symbiont in a nutritive media. Nematode virulence and viability tests, age, and the proportion of viable to non-viable worms are used to assess the nematode product's quality. EPNs enter the hemocoel of their host insects by the spiracles, mouth, anus, or, in the case of some species, the intersegment membranes of the cuticle. They inject symbiotic bacterial spore into the sick insect's hemolymph, where the bacteria quickly grow and induce septicemia, which kills the host within 24 to 48 hours. Nematodes develop, mature, and breed while still feeding on the dead host tissue. After 8-14 days, a new generation of infectious juveniles appears. The only free-living stage for EPNs is the infectious juvenile stage. Heterorhabditis and Steinernema are mutualistic partners with the bacteria Photorhabdus and Xenorhabdus, respectively. The juveniles expel symbiotic bacterial cells into the hemocoel from their intestines. Infected insects die as a result of the bacteria reproducing in their hemolymph. The nematodes continue to grow, reproduce, and eat host tissue. A distinct reproductive mechanism is used by heterorhabditid nematodes, which generate both men and females in the following generation after infectious juveniles develop into hermaphrodite adults. In contrast, all generations of steinernematid worms produce both men and females. EPNs do best in sandy soil with a pH range of 4-8, although they are sensitive to UV radiation, cold, dehydration, and excessive heat. Plant pathogens such as fungus, viruses, oomycetes, bacteria, and nematodes, weeds, arthropods (mostly insects and mites), molluscs (slugs and snails), and a few vertebrates are examples of agricultural pests. By eating crops, these pests reduce crop quality and yield. Pest species are thought to number in the millions worldwide, severely reducing agricultural productivity. In fact, it has been reported that these failures have resulted in a 40% reduction in the potential world crop output. Insects and mites are among the agricultural pests that are particularly difficult to control and account for roughly 10.80% of agricultural losses globally in the post-green revolution age. Due to their convenience, high efficacy, and ease of use, insecticides are now a vital tool for controlling insect pest infestations and have been widely employed to reduce these losses. However, the intensive use of chemicals has resulted in the development of insect pest resistance, with as much as 80% of cases observing resistance to one or more classes of insecticides. Entomopathogens, which include fungi, viruses, protozoa, and bacteria, are thought of as natural pest infestation regulators in order to deal with this problem. Entomopathogens manage insect pest populations and keep them at levels that don't hurt crop plants financially.

The word "entomopathogens" refers to microorganisms that prey on insect pests as a necessary component of their life cycles, defining them as obligatory or facultative parasites with a high possibility for survival. In addition to being effective against insect pests, using entomopathogenic microorganisms (EM) as pest control agents is also environmentally safe for both people and non-target creatures because it decreases pesticide residues. In agro-ecosystems, natural enemies are essential for keeping pest populations from rising to dangerous levels. Depending on the type of pest and the biological characteristics of the control agent, biological control agents (BCAs) are used in a variety of ways. Their host specificity, lack of toxic residues, lack of phytotoxic effects, safety for humans, and potential for self-sustaining pest control are only a few of their benefits. However, effective application necessitates a thorough comprehension of both the ecology of the insect and its natural antagonist. A full grasp of bioassay processes, manufacturing, formulation, and application strategies is required for the development of entomopathogens (fungi, bacteria, viruses, and nematodes) as effective biological control agents.

div.head: Conclusions
div.p: Chemical pesticides are frequently used to protect plants, however this strategy has increased insect resistance to several chemical compounds found in these products. Exploring all-natural approaches to combating insect infestations, such as entomopathogens, has received more attention in recent years. Microorganisms known as entomopathogens hunt out and kill insect pests, providing hope for efficient management of insect pests. Entomopathogens are being developed as environmentally benign substitutes for agricultural crops because they can act as biological control agents to improve agrosustainability. It is commonly acknowledged that using biological controls to protect agricultural crops from insect pests is a responsible ecological strategy.
The study of microbial pesticides offers a singular opportunity for performing prospective and predicative research within the field of pesticides.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Providing sufficient habitat and other food supplies to already-existing or naturally-occurring beneficial species is one of the most successful and long-lasting ways for reducing economic harm from pests. In contrast to monocultures or pesticide-treated fields, beneficial species such as predators, parasites, and pest-sicking "pathogens" are significantly more numerous in varied farms where pesticides are used less frequently.

div.head: What are Predators?
div.p: Predators: Insects are consumed by a diverse range of predators. Many creatures rely extensively on insects for food, including birds, amphibians, reptiles, fish, and mammals. These insectivorous animals eat a wide variety of insect species and rarely focus on pests unless they are in great numbers. Insects and other arthropods are more commonly used in biological control because they consume a restricted range of prey species and have shorter life cycles than other arthropod predators. This is due to the fact that their population density can shift in response to variations in the density of their prey. A few notable insect predators are lady beetles, ground beetles, rove beetles, flower bugs and other predatory true bugs, lacewings, and hover flies. Spiders, among other things, are insect predators and pests. A predator makes its living by catching and eating on other animals. Predators are often bigger and stronger than their victim. Many of the most prevalent predators in fruit production systems attack a wide variety of pest species and aid in pest population density regulation. Some examples of predators in the agro-ecosystem are-Ladybugs, also known as lady beetles or ladybird beetles, are a beloved pest control for farmers and gardeners. They are known for their ability to gobble up crop-destroying aphids, which are a significant issue for farms and gardens.

Ladybugs can consume 50-60 aphids daily and thousands over a lifetime, and can lay hundreds of eggs in a season. They also feed on mites, scales, mealy bugs, thrips, and white flies, which all damage crops. Spiders are extremely valuable to humans because they suppress insects and crop-eating pests. They are major insect controllers, averting starvation and suppressing crop-consuming insects. Spiders are unsettling because of their sticky webs and fast pounces, yet they are necessary for a healthy environment.

Coyotes, a pest animal and a nuisance, may assist farmers by being skilled predators that control rodent populations. They are omnivorous and consume fruits, berries, and vegetables, although meat accounts for 90% of their diet.

They may also attack and kill animals in packs, which ranchers may find offensive. Good fence and a livestock guard dog or two roaming the zone are usual solutions. Skunks are a Grade A nuisance that may help farms by eating problem insects such as caterpillars, grubs, field mice and voles. They consume berries, leaves, and grasses as well. Skunks, on the other hand, are not attracted to beekeepers due to their thick fur since they are not deterred by bee stings and would seek out honeybee colonies for food. Owls are super-carnivores that help farmers reduce rodent infestations and safeguard crops. They are expert nocturnal hunters, taking up to 10% of their total weight on rodents each night. Their enormous wingspan enables them to pursue phantom night stalkers, and they have also been observed preying on snakes, amphibians, and invertebrates. Owls' ability to track and hunt at night makes them a natural pest defence system. Lacewings attack aphids, spider mites, whiteflies thrips, leafhoppers, scales, mealybugs, psyllids, small caterpillars and insect eggs. Green lacewing larvae feed on insect pests. Both larvae and adult brown lacewings feed on pests. Green lacewings are commercially available. Syrphid (Hover) fly larvae attack Aphids, scales, thrips and other small softbodied insects Praying mantids attacks many, including aphids, flies, beetles. Feeds on pests as well as beneficials.
Mantids grasp their prey with spined front legs and hold them while they eat. Parasitic wasps attacks aphids, caterpillars and whiteflies.
Predatory mites attacks spider mites, thrips, fungus gnat larvae.

div.head: Role of predators in agro ecosystem
div.p: Pests are those creatures that prey on a resource that humans desire to conserve and are successful enough in doing so to cause either economic harm or just general aggravation. They make up a very small portion of the local bug species. Even many of the species that we would consider to be severe pests only seldom harm us or our resources in a substantial way. Predators are crucial in reducing the populations of potential pests. When insecticides have wiped off the predators of prospective bugs, this has been consistently shown. When insects are liberated from the care of their predators, they frequently turn into destructive pests, even though they were previously of little economic value. On the other hand, when a non-toxic technology is discovered to treat a significant pest, the reduced use of pesticides and increased survival of predators frequently reduces the numbers and damage of formerly important secondary pest species.

Avoid farming practises that kill beneficial predators in order to conserve and enhance rich predator populations. Instead, use measures that will help them survive. Begin by discontinuing practises that interfere with natural biological management, such as pesticide sprays, hedge clearance, and extensive herbicide usage to remove weeds in and around fields. During critical times of the growing season, even little changes in farming practises can drastically increase natural enemy populations. Simple applications of straw mulch provide damp, safe hiding places for nocturnal predators such as spiders and ground beetles. Straw mulch may also reduce the capacity of flying pests such as aphids and leafhoppers to "see" the crops they attack by decreasing the contrast between foliage and bare soil. Insect damage to mulched vegetable plots can be significant. Diversification has several advantages, as is true of the majority of the tactics discussed in this book. For instance, correctly chosen blooming plants or trees on field borders may serve as significant sources of beneficial insects, but they may also alter the microclimate of crops, contribute to organic matter, and yield timber or feed. Creating wild flower borders around agriculture fields increases the number of helpful insects that are actively seeking for pollen and nectar. The beneficials then relocate to nearby fields to assist in controlling insect problems. Many of these flowers also have the added benefit of being good bee food, which increases honey output, or they may be sold as cut flowers, which increases farm income.

div.head: Methods to conserve predators
div.p: Increase the number of predators: A fertilised, weeded, and watered monoculture is a dense, pure concentration of an insect pest's favourite diet. Over time, many people have adapted to these simple cropping techniques. Predators, on the other hand, do poorly since they are suited to natural systems. Tilling, weeding, spraying, harvesting, and other common farming practises degrade desirable habitat. Instead, try to meet their biological demands.

Predators require more than prey and hosts to complete their life cycles; they also require refuge places and alternate meals. Many adult parasites, for example, feed on pollen and nectar from adjacent flowering weeds while looking for hosts. Predaceous ground beetles, like many other predators, do not disperse far from their overwintering locations; access to fixed food sources is essential. Provide more resources: Increase predator populations by giving supplies to attract or retain them on your farm.

Allowing permanent populations of alternate prey to vary below harmful levels increases the survival and reproduction of helpful insects. To do this, put plants that host alternate prey surrounding your fields or even as strips within your fields. The relative quantity of aphids in cabbage influences the efficacy of general predators that devour diamond back moth larvae. Similarly, anthocorid bugs benefit from alternate meals when their primary prey, western flower thrips, is limited in many areas.

Another technique is to increase the number of the favoured host of a beneficial bug. Cabbage butterflies, for example, are the preferred host for two parasites (Trichogramma evanescens and Apanteles rebecula). With continuous releases of pregnant females, populations of this pest increased roughly tenfold in the spring.
