This allowed the two parasites -both parasitic wasps -to establish themselves sooner in the spring and remain active throughout the season.

Because of the apparent hazards, this approach should only be used in instances where pollen, nectar, or alternate prey cannot be acquired. Because the emphasis of pest resistance plant breeding is frequently restricted to a single plant/pest interaction, choosing for one resistant gene may mistakenly remove other genes influencing other pests or genes that have a role in attracting the insect's predators. Furthermore, newly produced varieties may stand better or yield more at the price of natural defences, which are sometimes sacrificed unwittingly for those other traits. Selecting for a single feature, such as height, may result in mistakenly selecting against any of the many inborn plant defences against pests.

Preserving present predators or predators: Using less pesticides is vital since most predators are extremely sensitive to them, reducing their field efficacy greatly. The original idea behind integrated pest management (IPM) was to combine biological and chemical control by employing the fewest pesticides required for economic production and delivering those that were required in a method that did not interfere with biological control agents. Pesticide-resistant cultivars, cultural practises that reduce pest abundance or damage, approaches for modifying insect mating or host-finding behaviour, and, in certain cases, physical measures of control can all assist to reduce pesticide use. However, many IPM initiatives have failed to get beyond the early development stage.

The influence of insecticides on predator populations is determined by their physiological effects and administration strategies. Herbicides and fungicides can be harmful to beneficial insects, but synthetic pyrethroids are even more dangerous. The toxicity of systemic and ingested pesticides on predators is lower. Predatory phytoseiid mites are prevented from ovipositing by fungicides such as benomyl, thiophanate-methyl, and carbendazim. To reduce pesticide influence on predators, carefully arrange treatments, restrict contact, and avoid susceptible life stages. Spot treatments or alternating strips over a field may also be beneficial. Supplying predators with habitat and resources: Unless they are released again every year, predators must have a suitable habitat to overwinter in as they are often dormant throughout the winter months. Others may spend the winter on agricultural wastes, other plants, or soil; however certain predators and diseases overwinter in the bodies of their hosts (who may then have their own overwintering requirements). Predacious mites overwintering in apple orchards are a well-known example. In these orchards, the ground cover offers protection from the pesticides used on the fruit trees, winter shelter, a supply of pollen, and alternative prey.

During the summer, many predators' adults may need or profit from pollen, nectar, or honeydew (made by aphids). Many agricultural plants only blossom consistently for a brief period of time, therefore additional supplies of pollen and nectar may be required from flowering plants inside the field or along its borders. However, plant diversity within a field can potentially hinder the effectiveness of host-finding. The availability of pollen and alternative food may stabilize the populations of generalist predators, but the efficiency of the predators still depends on their capacity to react swiftly, either by aggregation or multiplication, to outbreaks of the target pest. Thus, diversification of plants or other methods of supplementing the nutrition of predators must be done with knowledge of the behavior and biology of the natural enemy and pest.

For instance, the Colorado potato beetle's eggs and early instar larvae provide a viable food source for the local lady beetle Coleomegilla maculata. The quantity of aphid prey in nearby fields, such as alfalfa, brassicas, cucurbits, and corn crops, as well as the accessibility of pollen from corn and numerous weeds, including dandelion and yellow rocket, determines the number of potato beetles that feed on them. Despite the fact that this predator does not now effectively manage C� maculata numbers in the agricultural environment, it could be more successful in the future.

div.head: Creating a stable population and adding new predators:
div.p: The biology of the pest, its predators, and any potential unintended consequences (such as adverse impacts on native species that are not pests or on other predators of the pest) must all be thoroughly researched throughout this phase. It is necessary to quarantine suitable predators after they have been identified, researched, and collected in order to get rid of any parasites or diseases that may have affected the population. The predators are then gradually released, paying close attention to the right moment in the enemy and pest life cycles, in an area with a high concentration of the target pest and with little disruption to the freshly released enemies. Although this process is long and complex, when it is successful, the results can be impressive and permanent, as long as care is taken in production practices to minimize negative effects on the natural enemy.

div.head: Periodic releases of predators and mass culture Seasonal immunization releases
div.p: In certain circumstances, the weather or a lack of adequate hosts or prey prevent a natural enemy from successfully overwintering. In other circumstances, such as in greenhouses, the natural enemy's potential habitat is completely eliminated at the conclusion of the growing season or production cycle. Therefore, it may be necessary to periodically reintroduce the natural enemy to maintain pest control, especially in annual crops or other highly disturbed systems.

div.head: Biological insecticides or inundative release
div.p: Because they do not seek to create a population of predators that grows to a size where it achieves a long-term balance with the population of their hosts or prey, these two methods of biological control are fundamentally distinct from all the others. Instead, the plan is to release biological agents in sufficient numbers to reduce the insect population, much like a pesticide.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: In the relentless battle against crop weeds, farmers have traditionally relied on chemical herbicides and labour-intensive mechanical methods. However, concerns about environmental impact and herbicide resistance have driven the search for more sustainable and eco-friendly alternatives. One such solution is biological control, harnessing the power of predatory insects to manage weed populations naturally. This chapter delves into the fascinating world of biological control, exploring the key concepts, benefits, and implementation strategies of using predatory insects to combat crop weeds.

div.head: What is Biological Control
div.p: Biological control, also known as bio-control, is a method of pest management that involves using living organisms to control pest populations. Predatory insects are natural enemies of certain weed species, preying on them to regulate their numbers and limit their growth.

div.head: The Importance of Balance in Ecosystems
div.p: By embracing biological control, we embrace the principles of ecological balance. In natural ecosystems, various species coexist, with predators keeping prey populations in check. Implementing this balance in agriculture can reduce the need for chemical interventions and promote sustainable practices.

div.head: Predatory Insects as Weed Warriors Identifying Beneficial Predators
div.p: Numerous predatory insects have shown an appetite for specific weed species. Ladybugs (Coccinellidae) target aphids and other soft-bodied pests, while lacewings (Chrysopidae) prey on aphids, mites, and small caterpillars. Ground beetles (Carabidae) are voracious feeders, targeting a wide range of weed seeds and small pests.

div.head: Predator-Prey Dynamics
div.p: Understanding the intricate relationship between predators and their weed prey is essential for effective biological control. Predators have developed specific adaptations to locate, consume, and control weed populations, often leading to a delicate ecological dance.

div.head: Site-Specific Approach
div.p: Each farm's unique characteristics and weed challenges call for a tailored biological control strategy. By studying the ecosystem, climate, and weed species prevalent in a region, farmers can identify the most suitable predators for release.

div.head: Mass Rearing and Augmentation
div.p: To boost predator populations and increase their efficacy, mass rearing and augmentation techniques are employed. Specialized facilities rear predatory insects in controlled conditions, ensuring they are healthy and ready for release.

div.head: Predator Release and Monitoring Strategic Timing and Distribution
div.p: Releasing predators at the right time is critical for a successful bio-control program. Timely releases coincide with peak weed growth stages, maximizing the impact of the predatory insects.

div.head: Monitoring the Efficacy
div.p: Continuous monitoring is essential to evaluate the success of the bio-control program. Researchers and farmers observe weed density, predator populations, and crop health, making adjustments as needed to optimize results.

div.head: Integration with Other Weed Management Practices Enhancing Effectiveness through Integration
div.p: Biological control is most effective when integrated with other weed management practices. Combining techniques like crop rotation, cover cropping, and mulching can create a comprehensive and sustainable weed control approach.

div.head: Challenges and Limitations
div.p: While biological control offers numerous benefits, it is not a one-size-fitsall solution. Challenges such as varying weather conditions, the potential for unintended effects on non-target organisms, and the need for careful planning and coordination exist.

div.head: History of Biological Control
div.p: Biological control, also known as bio-control or biological pest control, is a method of managing pest populations using natural enemies or beneficial organisms. The concept of using predatory insects for weed control dates back thousands of years, with ancient civilizations employing various organisms to manage pests and unwanted plant growth. However, the modern scientific understanding and development of biological control began to take shape in the 19th century.

div.head: Early Attempts and Notable Successes
div.p: Ancient Practices: In ancient China, farmers used ants to control citrus pests, and the Chinese also practiced the release of predatory ants to manage caterpillar pests in citrus orchards. Similarly, the ancient Egyptians employed cats to control rodent populations in granaries and storage facilities. Medieval Europe: During the medieval period, falconers in Europe used birds of prey to control rodent populations, helping protect crops from damage.

div.head: Introduction of Ladybugs:
div.p: In the late 19th century, the vedalia beetle (Rodolia cardinalis) was successfully introduced from Australia to California to control the cottony cushion scale (Icerya purchasi). This marked one of the earliest and most notable successes in the biological control of agricultural pests.

div.head: Cactoblastis Moth Control of Prickly Pear Cacti:
div.p: In the early 20th century, the cactoblastis moth (Cactoblastis cactorum) was introduced to Australia from South America to control invasive prickly pear cacti, which were causing significant agricultural and ecological damage. The cactoblastis moth proved highly effective in controlling the cacti population.

div.head: Key Milestones and Developments in the Use of Predatory Insects for Weed Control
div.p: The Green Revolution: In the mid-20th century, agricultural practices underwent significant changes with the advent of the Green Revolution.
During this period, the focus was primarily on increasing crop yields using chemical fertilizers and pesticides, which led to a decline in the emphasis on biological control methods.

div.head: Predatory Insects as Weed Control Agents
div.p: Predatory insects play a crucial role in natural ecosystems by preying on various pests, including insects, mites, and small animals. When it comes to weed control in agricultural settings, these beneficial insects can also be harnessed as effective agents to suppress weed populations. Predatory insects offer several advantages over chemical methods and can be an integral part of sustainable weed management strategies. Here's a closer look at their characteristics and benefits as weed control agents:

1. Target-Specific Feeding Predatory insects exhibit target-specific feeding behavior, meaning they primarily consume certain pest species. This trait is advantageous in weed control as they tend to focus on particular weed species, reducing the risk of non-target damage to crops or desirable vegetation.

div.head: Natural Enemy of Weeds
div.p: Predatory insects have evolved to be natural enemies of certain weed species.
Their biological adaptations, such as specialized mouthparts or digestive enzymes, allow them to efficiently consume and control weeds in a way that is often specific to the target weed.

div.head: Selective and Environmentally Friendly
div.p: Unlike broad-spectrum chemical herbicides, predatory insects are selective in their feeding and do not harm non-target organisms. This makes them environmentally friendly and suitable for integrated pest management (IPM) programs, which aim to balance pest control with conservation efforts.

div.head: Self-Sustaining Populations
div.p: Once established, populations of predatory insects can often self-sustain if sufficient prey or alternative food sources are available. This reduces the need for repeated introductions and can lead to long-term weed suppression.

div.head: Reduced Chemical Use
div.p: Using predatory insects for weed control can help reduce the reliance on chemical herbicides, contributing to a decrease in chemical residues in the environment and minimizing potential negative impacts on beneficial organisms.

div.head: Compatibility with Organic Farming
div.p: For organic farmers, the use of predatory insects aligns well with organic farming principles, as it is a natural and non-synthetic approach to pest and weed management.

div.head: Low Risk of Resistance
div.p: Unlike chemical herbicides, which can lead to the development of herbicideresistant weed populations, predatory insects are less likely to encounter resistance issues due to the complexity of their feeding behaviors and the multiple mechanisms they employ to control pests and weeds.

div.head: Limited Environmental Persistence
div.p: Chemical herbicides can persist in the environment for extended periods, potentially leading to unintended side effects. In contrast, predatory insects typically have a short lifespan and may not have long-lasting effects once their prey populations decline.

div.head: Potential for Use in Various Environments
div.p: Predatory insects can be used in a range of agricultural environments, including field crops, orchards, and greenhouse settings, providing versatile options for weed control across different agricultural systems.

While predatory insects offer significant benefits for weed control, successful implementation requires careful consideration of factors such as predator selection, population management, and compatibility with existing weed management strategies. Additionally, understanding the ecology and behavior of both the predatory insects and target weed species is critical to optimizing their effectiveness in suppressing weed populations and promoting sustainable agricultural practices.

div.head: Selection and Identification of Predatory Insects
div.p: Selection and identification of predatory insects for weed control is a crucial step in implementing a successful biological control program. It involves carefully evaluating potential predators to ensure they are effective against the target weed species while being compatible with the agricultural ecosystem.
Here are the key considerations and steps involved in the selection and identification process:

div.head: Identify the Target Weed Species
div.p: Begin by identifying the specific weed species that need to be controlled.
Understanding the biology, life cycle, and habitat preferences of the target weed will help in selecting appropriate predators that can efficiently attack and suppress the weed population.

div.head: Research Potential Predatory Insects
div.p: Conduct thorough research to identify potential predatory insects known to feed on the target weed or related species. Consult scientific literature, research papers, and reputable resources that document the feeding preferences and effectiveness of various predatory insects against specific weed species.

div.head: Determine Predator Specificity
div.p: Assess the feeding habits and specificity of each potential predatory insect.
Ideally, the predator should primarily target the desired weed species without causing harm to non-target plants, crops, or beneficial organisms.

div.head: Evaluate Environmental Suitability
div.p: Consider the environmental conditions and climate of the agricultural area where the biological control will be implemented. Ensure that the selected predatory insects can thrive and effectively control weeds under the prevailing conditions.

div.head: Assess Life Cycle and Behavior
div.p: Understanding the life cycle and behavior of the predatory insects is essential.

Consider factors such as their reproductive rates, feeding patterns, and dispersal abilities. Predators with overlapping generations and a preference for the target weed throughout their life stages are more likely to provide sustained weed control.

div.head: Determine Availability and Source of Predators
div.p: Identify sources from which the predatory insects can be obtained. This could include commercial suppliers, biological control organizations, or research institutions that rear and distribute beneficial organisms for pest management.

div.head: Conduct Field Trials and Observations
div.p: Before implementing large-scale biological control, conduct small-scale field trials to assess the effectiveness of the selected predatory insects against the target weed. Monitor predator populations and weed suppression over time to gauge the success of the approach.

div.head: Consider Complementary Predators
div.p: In some cases, a combination of different predatory insect species may be more effective in controlling the target weed. Evaluate the potential for synergistic effects by using complementary predators that have different feeding behaviors or life cycle timings.

div.head: Monitor for Unintended Consequences
div.p: During and after introducing predatory insects, closely monitor their impact on the weed population and other components of the ecosystem. Look for any unintended consequences, such as non-target damage or shifts in pest populations, and adjust the management approach if necessary.

div.head: Develop an Integrated Weed Management Plan
div.p: Integrate the use of predatory insects into a comprehensive weed management plan that may include other methods such as cultural practices, mechanical weed removal, and herbicide use. An integrated approach is often the most effective and sustainable way to manage weeds populations in agriculture.

Ultimately, successful selection and identification of predatory insects for weed control require a well-informed decision-making process based on scientific knowledge, field observations, and a thorough understanding of the target weed and the predators under consideration. Collaboration with entomologists, weed scientists, and experienced biological control practitioners can significantly enhance the chances of success in implementing a bio-control program.

div.head: Augmentation and Conservation of Predatory Insects
div.p: Augmentation and conservation of predatory insects are essential components of successful biological control programs for weed management. Augmentation involves the deliberate release of additional predatory insects into the target area to boost their populations, while conservation focuses on creating and enhancing habitats to support the long-term survival and effectiveness of these beneficial organisms. Here are the key considerations for both augmentation and conservation: Augmentation of Predatory Insects Assessment of Pest Pressure: Before augmentation, assess the level of weed infestation and pest pressure in the area. Determine whether the existing population of predatory insects is sufficient to provide effective weed control or if additional releases are necessary.

Source and Quality of Predators: Obtain predatory insects from reputable suppliers or specialized rearing facilities to ensure that the released individuals are healthy, free from diseases or parasites, and have a high likelihood of survival and establishment. Timing of Releases: Time the releases of predatory insects to coincide with the life stages of the target weed species. Releasing predators when the weed is most susceptible to herbivory maximizes their impact. Distribution Strategy: Distribute the released predatory insects evenly across the infested area to ensure uniform weed control. Pay attention to areas where weed growth is most concentrated or vulnerable Monitoring and Evaluation: Continuously monitor the population dynamics of the released predators and the impact on the target weed. Regular evaluations help to assess the success of the augmentation strategy and make adjustments as needed.

div.head: Conservation of Predatory Insects
div.p: Habitat Management: Design and manage the agricultural landscape to provide suitable habitats for predatory insects. Incorporate features such as hedgerows, wildflower strips, and cover crops to offer shelter, alternative food sources, and overwintering sites. Minimize Pesticide Use: Reduce or eliminate the use of broad-spectrum pesticides in the target area to avoid unintentional harm to predatory insects. Employ selective and targeted pesticides only when necessary. Diverse Plantings: Encourage the establishment of diverse plant communities within and around crop fields. A variety of plant species can attract and sustain a diverse range of predatory insects, increasing the resilience of the overall ecosystem.

Natural Enemy Reservoirs: Identify and protect areas where native predatory insects thrive naturally, such as nearby woodlands or natural habitats. These areas can act as reservoirs, providing a source of predators that can colonize the crop fields when needed.

Reduced Soil Disturbance: Minimize unnecessary soil disturbance and tillage practices, as these activities can disrupt predator populations and their overwintering sites. Biological Corridors: Create corridors or connections between natural areas to facilitate the movement of predatory insects between different parts of the landscape, enhancing their ability to find and control weed populations.

Crop Rotation and Diversification: Implement crop rotation and diversification strategies to disrupt weed growth cycles and reduce the build-up of weed populations. This can positively impact the success of predatory insects in managing weeds.

div.head: Monitoring and Assessment
div.p: Monitoring and assessing the biological control of crop weeds through predatory insects is an essential aspect of integrated pest management (IPM) strategies. Biological control involves using natural enemies, such as predatory insects, to reduce weed populations and minimize the use of chemical pesticides. Here are some key steps in monitoring and assessing the effectiveness of biological control using predatory insects:

div.head: Identification of Target Weed and Predatory Insects
div.p: Identify the weed species that need to be controlled and the predatory insects that can potentially prey on those weeds. It's crucial to have a good understanding of the specific weed and predator species involved.

div.head: Establishment of Predatory Insects
div.p: If predatory insects are not already present in the area, consider introducing them into the crop ecosystem. This process is known as "augmentation," and it involves releasing an adequate number of predatory insects to control the weed infestation effectively.

div.head: Monitoring Weed and Predator Populations
div.p: Regularly monitor the population levels of the target weed and the introduced predatory insects. This can be done through visual observations, trapping, and sampling techniques. Monitoring should start before the release of predators to establish a baseline, and it should continue throughout the growing season.

div.head: Assessing Weed Damage
div.p: Determine the level of weed damage in the crop fields and compare it with areas where no biological control measures have been implemented. This comparison will help evaluate the effectiveness of the predatory insects in reducing weed populations.

div.head: Monitoring Predator Establishment and Persistence
div.p: Check whether the released predatory insects have successfully established in the ecosystem and if they persist over time. Factors like climate, habitat suitability, and the presence of alternative prey can influence their survival and effectiveness.

div.head: Measuring Weed Suppression
div.p: Quantify the reduction in weed populations achieved through the actions of predatory insects. This can be done using various metrics, such as weed density, weed biomass, or weed cover.

div.head: Assessing Crop Yield and Quality
div.p: Evaluate the impact of weed suppression on crop yield and quality. Healthy crops with minimal weed interference should result in higher yields and betterquality produce.

div.head: Economic Evaluation
div.p: Conduct an economic analysis to determine the cost-effectiveness of using biological control through predatory insects compared to other weed control methods, such as chemical herbicides.

div.head: Adjusting Strategies
div.p: Based on monitoring and assessment results, make necessary adjustments to the biological control program. This might include altering the release rates of predatory insects, introducing additional natural enemies, or implementing supplementary control measures if needed.

div.head: Data Recording and Reporting
div.p: Keep detailed records of monitoring and assessment results. This information will be valuable for future decision-making and for sharing the outcomes of the biological control program with relevant stakeholders.

div.head: Future Prospects and Challenges
div.p: The biological control of crop weeds through predatory insects holds significant promise as a sustainable and environmentally friendly approach to managing agricultural pests. However, there are also some challenges that need to be addressed to realize its full potential. Here are the future prospects and challenges associated with this method:

div.head: Challenges
div.p: Host Specificity: Some predatory insects may not be highly specific to the target weed species and could also prey on non-target plants or beneficial insects. Ensuring that introduced predators focus primarily on the target weed is critical to avoid unintended consequences. Availability of Effective Predators: Finding and sourcing effective predatory insects can be a challenge in some regions, particularly for specific weed species and certain agricultural ecosystems.

div.head: Environmental Factors:
div.p: The success of biological control can be influenced by environmental factors like temperature, humidity, and habitat availability. Adverse conditions may limit the effectiveness of predatory insects.
Regulatory Hurdles: Introducing non-native predators or other biological control agents may require strict regulatory approvals, which can be timeconsuming and costly.

div.head: Timing and Coordination:
div.p: The successful implementation of biological control often requires precise timing and coordination with the weed's growth stage and the release of predatory insects.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Biological control of crop weeds through predatory insects presents a promising and eco-friendly solution for modern agriculture. By embracing the natural balance of predator-prey relationships, farmers can minimize the use of chemical herbicides, protect beneficial organisms, and preserve the environment. The integration of this innovative approach with other sustainable practices empowers farmers to foster healthier crops, ensure food security, and promote the long-term well-being of our planet. As we look to the future, embracing biological control may hold the key to a more harmonious and sustainable agricultural landscape.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Insect pests cause a loss of 18-26% of global yearly agricultural yields. A wide range of the losses 13-16% occur in the field prior to harvest. Furthermore, post-harvest pests are responsible for a significant portion of agricultural product storage losses. Poor management practises might result in the loss of 50-60% of stored grains over the storage period of time.Farmers have used chemical pesticides to control insect pests for many years but the effects on non-target organisms, groundwater contamination, residues on food crops and the emergence of insect pests resistant to chemical pesticides have forced scientists to concentrate on the development of alternative eco-friendly measures.Biological pest management is one of the most effective alternatives to chemical pesticides. Biological control, defined as pest population reduction by natural enemies is an alternative to manufactured chemical pesticides that generally requires human intervention (Hoffmann and Frodsham 1993). Entomopathogenic fungi are an important aspect of an integrated strategy that can provide considerable and selective insect control among the numerous biocontrol agents. Entomopathogenic fungi are fungi that kill insects by attacking and infecting their insect hosts (Singkaravanit et al�, 2010). They are probably the most adaptable biological control agents due to their broad host range. These fungi are a diverse group of over 100 genera with over 750 species described from various insects. Entomopathogenic fungi were among the first living species applied for biological pest management. Because of their eco-friendliness and bio-persistence, these entomopathogens are recommended to kill insects at various phases of their life cycle (Gul et al�, 2014). The most entomopathogenic fungi are found in the Hypocreales (Ascomycota) group, which has a broad host range, and the Entomophthoromycota (Zygomycota) group which is more specialised. Entomopathogens such as Metarhizium anisopliae and Beauveria bassiana have been thoroughly studied due to their pathogenicity to a variety of insects. Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae were among the first entomopathogenic fungi to be effectively employed for myco-biocontrol of insect pest.

The Metarhizium and Beauveria genera are two of the most important EPF groups for practical pest management. They have received the greatest attention globally due to the fact that they are commonly found in nature and have a huge number of hosts that their control is highly effective and that they are simple to utilise in the production of commercial products. They are distinguished by the ability to produce free conidia from reproductive structures (conidiophores) that are easily transported by the wind. EPF are found in a variety of habitats and may be found in a wide range of geographic and climatic areas in both cultivated and natural soils (Vega et al�, 2008).

div.head: Biology and Life Cycle of EPF
div.p: These are related to insects because they utilise chitin as a carbon source for their exoskeleton; their conidia cling to the bodies of the insects and the cycle begins (Fig. 1). Entomopathogenic fungus has a parasitic phase (from host infection to host death) and a saprophytic phase (after host death). Entomopathogenic fungi infect their hosts through the exterior cuticle whereas other entomopathogens (bacteria and viruses) that enter the insects through food. Steps of infection involves: the spore adhering to the insect cuticle, the germ tube penetrating the cuticle, the fungus developing inside the insect body and fungal hyphae colonising the hemocoel. Entomopathogenic fungus spores are frequently coated with a coating of mucus made up of proteins and glucans which aids in their adhesion to the insect cuticle. Several entomopathogenic fungi form specialised structures called appressoria when their spores germinate. The epicuticular surface of the germinating spore is attached by the appressorium.

The mechanical pressure and enzymatic activity of the germ tube cause penetration of the insect cuticle. The secretion of successive lipases, proteases and chitinases plays a crucial role in penetration. Most entomopathogenic fungi develop within insects as yeast-like propagules (blastospores), hyphal bodies or protoplasts that lack a cell wall. These structures flow throughout the hemocoel. Mechanical damage induced by developing mycelia inside the insect (mummification) or poisons generated and released by the pathogen are the most common causes of insect death. Toxins having insecticidal activity such as destruxins, oosporein, beauvericin, bassianolide, bassianin, beauveriolides, bassiacridin, cordycepin and ciclosporin have been obtained from EPF & their action and involvement in the disease process has been studied. (Hamil et al�, 1969;Susuki et al�, 1977;Quesada-Moraga et al�, 2006;Wang and Wang, 2017).

div.head: Isolation of Entomopathogenic Fungi 1. Isolation from Soil
div.p: Using the Galleria bait approach based on Zimmermann, the entomopathogenic fungi were isolated from soil samples. Soil samples were taken from 0-20 cm deep in a circle 1 m in diameter around tree trunks. A total of 1.5 kg of soil was taken in plastic bags and transported to the laboratory in a chilled box. Larger wax moth's larvae G� mellonella larvae were distributed in the cups at @ of 3 larvae per cup and wetted to about 15 percent moisture content. Infected larvae were moved to 5centimetre diameter Petri plates furnished with wetted filter paper after a week of incubation at 25°C and left for development and sporulation. Both the quantity of infected G� mellonella larvae in each sample as well as the quantity of positive samples (samples containing entomopathogenic fungus) were counted.

div.head: Isolation of EPF from the Infected Insects
div.p: Infected insects covering in fungal mycelium were collected in the field and transported to the laboratory. Different stages of Cassidavittata and Scrobipalpaocellatella have been identified dead and completely covered by fungal mycelium. The insect cadavers were placed on moist filter paper in a Petri plate and incubated for a week at 24 ±1°C. The new fungal generation was isolated from the surface of an insect carcass and grown on Petri plates using PDA media. Weekly purifications of fungal cultures were done until pure cultures were obtained.

div.head: Mass production of Entomopathogenic Fungi
div.p: Fungi were grown and maintained on the following medium.

div.head: Peptone Medium
div.p: This medium contains 10 g peptone, 40 g dextrose, 2 g yeast extract, 15 g agar, 500 mL chloramphenicol and made-up total volume one litre by adding distilled water. The medium was autoclaved at 15 psi and 120°C for 20 minutes and poured into Petri plates (9 cm diameter 1.5 cm). Poured Petri plates inoculated with entomopathogenic fungi and incubate at 25 ±2°C and 85 ±5 R.H. Every 15 to 30 days, the fungal isolates were re-cultured and stored at 4°C.

div.head: Potato Dextrose Agar
div.p: PDA medium, which contained 200 g peeled potatoes, 20 g dextrose, 20 g agar, and 1000 mL distilled water. PDA media was autoclaved for 20 minutes at 15 psi and 120 °C and poured into Petri plates (9 cm diameter 1.5 cm). Every 14-30 days, the fungal isolates were re-cultured and stored at 4 °C. To restore the isolate's virulence, they were transferred through their native host or the wax moth larvae G� mellonella.

div.head: Rice Grains
div.p: Isolates of Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae were grown on wetted rice grains. Two kilogrammes of wet rice grains were soaked in boiling water for ten minutes and placed in thermal bags. These bags were autoclaved for 20 minutes at 15 psi and 120 °C, then inoculated with isolates and cultured at 25 ±2 °C for 15 days. Conidia were extracted using distilled water and filtered through cheese cloth to remove mycelium clumps.

div.head: Application technology of EPF for insect pest management
div.p: The techniques of application of an entomopathogenic fungus influences the treatment's efficacy. Several aspects must be considered, including spore dispersion and coverage to be sure that the pest comes into touch with the fungus as well as environmental conditions that promote the survival and germination of fungus before, during and after application. Dipping the plant or roots, spraying the foliage, treating the soil and passive transmission by vectors are the four basic techniques of fungal application.

div.head: Dipping
div.p: One of the probable applications is dip plant parts or roots in spore suspensions of entomopathogenic fungi while this approach is not widely used, it has gained popularity in recent years for the control of western flower thrips, Frankliniella occidentalis. The fungus is assumed to target the larval stage in the soil, However, there is a lack of research proving the efficacy of this technique.

div.head: Foliar spray
div.p: Foliar sprays have historically been the most common method of applying fungus most likely because farmers are familiar with this technique by its use to chemical pesticides. Extensive research has been conducted to maximise fungal efficacy by spraying the vegetation. Fungi from the ground can be applied using a wide range of applicators. Air application has also been used to treat huge areas in Africa to combat locust and grasshopper infestations. It is important to choose the appropriate sprayer for the agricultural field as well as the appropriate formulation for the circumstances. For example, in greenhouses, it is usual practise to apply fungal sprays in the late afternoon to capitalise on the greater relative humidity that occurs at that timewhich facilitates fungal germination.

Ensuring that fungal spores are evenly distributed to the place where the target insect occurs is arguably the most necessary aspect impacting effective control although it is difficult to attain. Kim et al�, (2010a) described using supernatant from a Beauveria bassiana liquid culture which included a thermotolerant chitinase and an enzyme associated to pathogenicity to improve the efficacy of a fungal spray against aphids. The supernatant fraction was combined with 0.01% polyoxyethylene-(3)-isotridecyl ether (TDE-3) as a spreading agent and integrated into the fungal preparation at 0.5% (w/v) with Attagel (an inert powder used as a thickener developed by BSAF Corp.). This is only one of several ways to improve fungal efficacy using enhanced application and formulation methods.

div.head: Soil treatments
div.p: Soil treatment with fungal spores is possibly the most sensible application approach but it is only appropriate if the target insect has a sensitive soil phase.

In general, natural soil water content levels are adequate to encourage conidial germination and mycelial growth allowing fungal inoculum to be sustained throughout time. Many agricultural settings waters their crops on a regular basis to ensure optimal moisture levels for the growth of fungi. Kim et al�, (2010b) evaluated the downward migration of Beauveria bassiana in the potting medium with varying amounts of wetness and top-watering due to concerns about spores being leached out during watering. They discovered that the concentration of Beauveria bassiana was highest at the top 3 cm of the potting medium and that there was no crucial movement of the spores after 18 days of frequent watering.

Another advantage of introducing fungus to soil rather than foliage is that the conidia are shielded from UV radiation damage within the soil. Temperature also has a significant impact on fungal effectiveness. Soil helps to moderate big temperature swings which generally encourage fungus growth and infectivity.
The presence of antagonistic fungi, bacterial or fungal-feeding arthropods and microorganisms is a key potential adverse side effect of treating soil with fungi.

div.head: Indirect Transmission by Vector
div.p: Entomopathogenic fungi are easily transmitted in nature by non-pest species to pest-infested areas. For example, Collembolans which are found in soil are known to carry fungal conidia that adhere to their cuticle. According to research, certain species were able to transport enough fungal propagules to kill mealworms (Dromph, 2003). Other natural cases of indirect vectoring have been described (Baverstock et al�, 2010) but the impact of this random transmission on pest populations is unknown. Kapongo et al�, (2008) evaluated the ability of utilising bumble bees (Bombus impatiens) a popular pollinator in greenhouse-grown vegetables, to transfer Beauveria bassiana to greenhouse whitefly and tarnished plant bug, Lygus lineolaris, in caged greenhouse experiments. Following fungal dissemination by bees, death rates of up to 56% for whiteflies and 67% for the tarnished plant bug were reported depending on the spore concentration observed. The bees exposed to the fungus died, indicating the delicate balance between positive and negative effects that must be established for a system like this to succeed.

Several commercially available formulations of entomofungal pathogens for crop pest control have been produced. Table 01 depicted a list of commercially available fungi, crops, and target insect pests.

div.head: Advantages
div.p: • They are only harmful to insects/arthropods and not plants or animals. They do not leave toxic residues on crops as some chemical pesticides do.
• They are somewhat host-specific allowing them to fit into an IPM programme that includes other beneficials such as predators and parasitoids.

• The majority are considered safe for humans and pose no disposal threat especially when compared to chemical insecticides (Laird et al�, 1990). • Compared to many parasitoids and predators, entomopathogenic fungi may be mass-produced on a solid substrate in huge quantities at a cheap cost and they can be maintained with little loss of viability or efficacy. • The majority of entomopathogenic fungi can endure a wide range of temperatures (often 0-40°C).

div.head: Disadvantages
div.p: • More time consuming than chemical pesticides, for example, fungus take 2-3 weeks to kill the insects. In comparison, this chemical pesticide may do the same thing in a short time as 2-3 hours. • Due to the high specificity of the fungal pesticide additional control methods are necessary for other insects. • The production cost of bioinsecticides is substantially greater with a shorter shelf life and the requirement of cold storage for spore maintenance. • The use of the biocontrol agent must coincide with contributing conditions such as increased humidity, a low number of pests and a fungicidal-free environment.
• Poses a danger to those with immunological deficiencies or who are immunocompromised.
• Because persistence and efficacy fluctuate amongst insect species regular optimisation of approaches is essential to maintain long-term impacts.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: One of the most promising agents for the biological control of insects is entomopathogenic fungi. Entomopathogenic fungi are a group of fungi that target and infect their insect hosts to kill them (Singkaravanitet al�, 2010). The Entomophthoromycota (Zygomycota) which are more specialised and the Hypocreales (Ascomycota) group which has a wide variety of hosts have the most entomopathogenic fungus species. Entomopathogenic fungi go through a parasitic phase (from host infection to host death) and a saprophytic phase (after host death) throughout their life cycle. Utilising the Zimmermann-based Galleria bait approach to isolate the entomopathogenic fungus from soil samples it may be more significant to focus on mass-producing EPF at cheap cost and in huge quantities. Growers rely on the commercial availability of substances made from fungi and those that are reasonably simple to make have a higher likelihood of being sold. The majority of commercially available fungus are Beauveria, Metarhizium, Lecanicillium and Isaria species since they are all reasonably simple to mass manufacture. For usage against agricultural insect pests, fungi such as Beauveria bassiana, Vuillemin, Isaria fumosorosea and Metarhizium anisopliae are currently being tested. Entomopathogenic fungi will soon play a significant role in the treatment of insect pests. But in terms of effectiveness and acceptance, it still lags far below synthetic chemicals.

Understanding their further functions in nature as endophytes, competitors of pathogens that are associated with the rhizosphere and even plant growthpromoting factors requires further study. As a result, the EPF deserves the best possible prospects in the advancement of agriculture because of its various responsibilities.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Chemical communication between plants and insects is an essential part of mutual relationships, in which both partners benefit from the communication.

The role of volatiles in pollination systems provides an excellent model for understanding the ecology and evolution of chemical communication between plants and insects. Infochemical:-A chemical that conveys information in an interaction between two individuals evoking in the receiver a behaviour or physiological response. Chemicals involved in interspecific communications were given the name of Allelochemicals by R. H. Whittaker in 1970. Allelochemicals affect the survival, growth & development of herbivorous insects as well as their natural enemies. Plant produced substances that serves as attractants, arrestants, oviposition and feeding stimulants for herbivores are the best known kairomones. These plant derived substances also influence organisms of the third trophic level when they are requested by herbivores & used by natural enemies to locate the herbivore. Communication can be active or passive. For e.g. a beetle may actively defend itself by ejecting a noxious spray when attacked or a caterpillar may leave secretions on a leaf while feeding that provide a cue for a parasitoid searching for the caterpillar. Chemical communications play a critical role in life for survival & for finding food & mates. An insect produce strongly repellent chemicals against a predator or parasitoid & actively reject them at the time of attack. Chemical ecology is the science that addresses the role of chemical cues in the interaction of organisms with their environment. Example of chemical communication is the use of sex pheromones by insects such as the silk moth Bombyx mori�

div.head: What is tritrophic interaction
div.p: An interaction among host plants, herbivores and their natural enemies (parasitoids & predators) is called tritrophic interaction. It leads to effective defence & attack at each level.
Two types of defence 1. Intrinsic defence: When the plant alone produces defence through physical means or through production of chemicals or both it is called intrinsic defence.
2. Extrinsic defence: When the natural enemies of insect pests benefit the host plants by reducing the pest abundance is called extrinsic defence.
1. Intrinsic defence

The physical, morphological, physiological and chemical characteristics of plants interact with the parasitoids and predators of insects by influencing their host seeking ability and affecting the efficacy with which they locate and utilize hosts.
• Plants provide nutrition to the natural enemies in the form of pollen and nectar.
• Insects such as aphids and whiteflies which secrete honey dew attract ants which also keep herbivores away.

• Plants that promote the effectiveness of the carnivores enemies of their own enemies & in this way the effect of the herbivore. The bitrophic defence is referred to as direct or intrinsic defence & the defence that employes carnivores is called indirect or extrinsic defence.

Attack-defense interactions in (A) a bitrophic context and (B) in a tritrophic context Plant volatiles play a critical role as signals in tri-trophic level interactions. These are interactions involving three trophic levels; for example, plants (1st trophic level), herbivores (2nd trophic level), and the natural enemies of herbivores (predators and parasitoids) (3 rd trophic level). Plants damaged by herbivores often produce a blend of volatiles commonly referred to as herbivore-induced plant volatiles (HIPVs) .The release of HIPVs may signal the presence of potential prey or hosts and, therefore, can be exploited by natural enemies to locate the prey organism (Sabelis et al., 1999;Verkerk, 2004). Herbivore-induced plant volatiles (HIPV) play an important role in host/prey searching behavior of predators and parasitoids of herbivorous arthropods (Heil, 2008;Dicke and Baldwin, 2010). Bark beetles (Curculionidae: Scolytinae) provide a useful system for exploring ecological and evolutionary implications of semiochemical interactions among predators and prey. They spend all but a brief portion of their life cycle within the subcortical tissues of host plants, and rely on chemical signaling for a variety of life history and ecological processes, including mating, host plant procurement, and niche partitioning (Wood 1982). Methyl salicylate is a known plant defence inducer and has been used successfully in the field to protect cereal crops against pest aphids (Prinsloo et al., 2007). Several different plant species, including corn (Zea mays) infested by caterpillars of the beet army worm Spodoptera exigua, take revenge on this pest by attracting the parasitoid Cotesia marginiventris� In lima beans, various coordinated defence strategies against mite infestation have been discovered. First, they change their scent to make them unattractive to the mites. Then the plants emit scents that are perceived by other plants, which then do precisely the same thing to warn surrounding lima beans before the mites even reach them. Some of the emitted substances have the effect of attracting other mites that eat the attacking red mites .Similar defence processes have been described in tomato plants (Kant et al�, 2004, Pearce andRyan, 2003). Tetranychus urticae Koch infestation or JA treatment induced the emission of volatiles in cucumber plants that attract the predatory mite Phytoseiulus persimilis� In Lima bean plants, infestation by the spider mite Tetranychus urticae leads to the production of volatile terpenoid chemicals such as (E)-P.ocimene, 4, 8-dimethyl-1,3(E), 7-nonatriene and linalool as well as methyl salicylate. The volatile compounds produced by the plants have been found to attract the predato mite Phytoseilus persimilis. Jasmonic acid (JA) and its volatile derivative methyl jasmonate (MeJA) are phytohormones involved in plant defenses against herbivores (Karban & Baldwin, 1997), Salicylic acid is a phytohormone often associated with plant resistance against pathogens but also against sucking insects such as aphids and whiteflies (Walling, 2000) 'Activation of both the JA and SA pathways by T� urticae is required to attract predatory mites to damaged lima bean plants (Dicke et al�, 1999). Rodriguez-Saona et al� (2011a) used MeSA to attract natural enemies in agricultural fields and found that natural enemies (i.e.,coccinellids, syrphids, lacewings, predatory bugs, and parasitic Hymenoptera Ant plants are prime examples of a plant-predator symbiotic mutualism. Plants provide resources, largely constitutively produced to ant species that may be specialized to nest and feed on these plants. The ants consume extra floral nectar (EFN) and food bodies (FBs). The production of ant food rewards increases predator visitations, and thus predation on herbivores may be higher on EFN-producing plants than on plants not producing EFN (Heil & McKey 2003). In (a) volatiles from one plant genotype induce responses in a second genotype that affect plant volatile emission and herbivore host quality. In (b) mixed odours of the different plant genotypes affect natural enemy orientation

div.head: Predator-released semiochemicals and tritrophic interactions
div.p: In tritrophic interactions the semiochemicals released by natural enemies cannot be neglected because these also alter the behavior or physiology of plants or herbivores. Some predators release synomones which are acquired from plants, as many of the predators and parasitoids obtain food from plants. Plants respond to synomones produced by predators. predators may also produce allomones to attract their prey, or to camouflage their own true identity. Carnivore populations are expected to vary in their responses to plant volatiles & other major foraging cues. The conspiracy between plant & carnivore is based on the feeding activity of the herbivore itself. Escape from carnivores can be a driving force in the evolution of plant selection by herbivores. The chances of a herbivore successfully escaping from carnivores through plant selection are best when there is a relative focus of the carnivores on plant stimuli over herbivore stimuli i.e carnivores specialized at the plant level (Vet& Dicke 1992).

div.head: Synergism in tritrophic interactions
div.p: Synergism plays an important role in insect-plant interactions as well as tritrophic interactions. • Piperonyl butoxide is known to be an effective synergist in conjunction with diflubenzuron in the case of Spodoptera exigua increasing the toxicity of diflubenzuron.

Future line of study 1. Need to study whether chemical communication is more in natural or managed ecosystems. 2. Need to reveal fascinating new aspects of the ecology of plant communications. 3. Exogenous elicitors may be developed synthetically and applied to a crop and utilized to increase time of searching by the natural enemies. 4. Very little is known about the interaction of plants and insects under tropical conditions. 5. Future attempts should be made to understand why plant species differ in the way they attract carnivores and under what circumstances they do so. 6. In order to fully appreciate the influence of chemical signaling on community and ecosystem processes one needs thorough knowledge of the mechanism of chemical information nveyance from the gene to individual.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: In most developing nations, the primary method of managing insect pests on various crops is by synthetic insecticides. One of the fastest and most efficient ways to reduce insect populations is by chemical control where a farmer quickly achieves extraordinary results. However, overusing pesticides indiscriminately and without proper research for longer periods of time led to a number of issues, including the risk of environmental contamination, loss of biodiversity, development of insecticideresistant pest populations, resurgence, outbreaks of secondary pests, increase in chemical inputs, toxicological hazards due to accumulation of pesticide residues in the food chain, etc. These issues ultimately contribute not only to inefficient insect control but also environmental and health hazards. The use of chemical pesticides for the management of insect pests must there fore be rationalised immediately. The preference for entomopathogenic Nuclear Polyhedrosis Virus (NPV) among the different biological control agents employed as an alternative to synthetic insecticides has been increasing in recent years. The normal physiological functions of the targeted species are also disrupted by novel insecticides that have been developed from natural sources by considering insect development mechanisms like moulting as well as chloride channel activator. The public's rising concern regarding the potential health risks of synthetic pesticides has recently prompted researchers to look at alternative pest control methods, such as the use of integrated pest management (IPM). IPM is the most efficient, environmentally responsible, and socially acceptable method of managing pests, diseases, and weeds. It integrates cultural, biological, and chemical methods. Under the integrated pest management (IPM) programme, insect harmful viruses like baculoviruses have been utilised successfully for biological control.

div.head: History
div.p: In the past Germany was the country that initially introduced NPV to Lymantria monacha populations in pine forests in 1892. The first field application of NPV was made against gipsy moths in the United States in 1913. A Californian farmer collected C�eurytheme larvae that had been killed by NPV and used them as an inoculum for a lucerne crop. The first viral pesticide (Elcar) used to treat cotton bollworm (Helicoverpa zea) was registered in the United States in 1975. Arora and Padmanaban (2002). Because they leave no residue and have no negative effects on beneficial predators and parasites, baculoviruses offer an alternative to chemical insecticides for treating certain insect pests. More than 65 crops, including maize, wheat, sorghum, different legumes, solanaceous crop, malvaceous crop, etc., are attacked by the Helicoverpa and Heliothis Cunningham (1995). Numerous significant Helicoverpa and Heliothis species, including Helicoverpa armigera, Helicoverpa assulta, Heliothis virescens, and Helicoverpa zea, were discovered to be NPV sensitive.

div.head: Structure
div.p: The virus that causes nuclear polyhedrosis is a obligate pathogen. The virus has a rod-shaped nucleocapsid that measures between 250 and 400 nm in diameter and 40 and 70 nm in height. The virions, are the proteinaceous polyhedral occlusion bodies that house the nucleocapsids in their envelope. The viruses are released by the alkaline stomach juice and attack the ganglia, brain, adipose bodies, haemocytes, tracheal matrix, and nuclei of cell tissue Harappa (1970).

div.head: Infection
div.p: The normal way of infection is by ingestion of polyhedra or in some cases virions. Larvae should be susceptible because when polyhedra are consumed along with food, occlusion-causing protinaceous substances begin to disintegrate in an alkaline environment (pH > 9). The virions infect the midgut epithelial cells; fresh virions are produced in the nuclei of infected cells and infect hemocoel cells as well as other tissues like the body's fat. In that tissue, virions are enclosed in polyhedra and continue to exist there until cell lysis.

Polyhedra can be seen in the nuclei of infected cells 24 to 72 hours after the first infection. Larvae eventually perish and splatter on soil and foliage after a few days. Predators and parasites then consume or spread to other insects or locations. Infected insects have bland colours, are less active, and the ventral side of the larvae turns reddish pink. The epidermis becomes extremely brittle and eventually disintegrates in advanced stages, and the worms become flaccid Deb et al� (2015). On plants, infected larvae hang upside down. "Tree top disease" or "wipfelkrankheit" is the name given to this condition. Grasserie diseases are NPV-related illnesses that affect silkworms.

div.head: Mass Production of NPV
div.p: The mass production of the NPV is carried out in the early fifth instar stage of the Spodoptera litura which yields the maximum amount of the NPV. As a result, a continuous culture of the insects can be maintained in the host culture laboratory using the correct handling techniques. The larvae are raised on a diet held in 5 ml glass vials, and when they reach the required maturity, they are moved to a facility that produces viruses. Feeding the semi-synthetic food covered with a clean inoculum of the previously standardised NPV multiplies the NPV. This is carried out by placing aliquots of 10 ml of viral suspension of concentration of 1x108 polyhedral occlusion bodies (POB) either in glass vials or directly on the surface of the diet, and spreading the suspension evenly with a polished glass rod. After 15 minutes, larvae are released singly into each glass vial or cell, where they are then cultured for 10 days at 25ºC. The fifth day marks the start of the larvae's death. Individual cadavers are collected, transported to disposable 500 ml containers, and immediately frozen until processing. This processing method is comparable to that of Helicoverpa armigera Tataksuka et al� (2007).

The cadavers are homogenised in sterile, ice-cold water using a liquidiser or a cool, all-glass pestle and mortar at a ratio of 1:2.5 (w/v). The material is repeatedly washed in water after being filtered through double stratified cloth. For this purpose, the water ratio to be used is 1:7.5-12.5 (w/v) for the first weight of the processed corpse. The remaining mat on the cloth is thrown away, and also the filtrate may be semi-purified by differential activity.
To remove debris, the filtrate is centrifuged for 30 to 60 seconds at 500 rpm.

Next, the supernatant is centrifuged at 5,000 rpm for 20 minutes. The solid occlusion bodies (POB)-containing pellet is afterwards centrifuged in sterile water three times at a low rate before being subjected to high-speed activity. In order to calculate the strength of the letter box within the refined suspension, the pellet is eventually collected and generated up to a famed volume in water The in vivo production process is comprised of three steps: 1. Infect larvae susceptible species, 2. Grow infected larvae for a while to allow the infection to develop and the virus to replicate 3. Harvest the larvae and extract the virus However, it has not been discovered to be simple in practise to maintain a prolonged output of virus. Effective production can only be reached and maintained with well-trained personnel, sufficiently planned production procedures, the suitable equipment, and a high standard of process quality control.

div.head: Major Insect Pests and Their Management By NPV
div.p: Spodoptera litura Spodoptera litura (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae) is a polyphagous pest of different crops and it has attended global importance due to its damaging range and insecticide resistance problems. SlNPV can be good alternate for the control of Spodoptera litura on cabbage. It is a multi-crop pest that has gained attention due to its destructive range and chemical resistance issues. SlNPV is a viable alternative for Spodoptera litura management on cabbage.

div.head: Mode of Application of Slnpv
div.p: 500 lit/ha is the rate at which SlNPV is applied in the field. It is necessary to spray twice; the first time is 45 days after transplanting cabbage in the field, and the second time is 60 days afterwards.

div.head: Mode of Application of Hanpv
div.p: A concentration of 1.15x107 OBs/ml of HaNPV is sprayed using a knapsack resulted in a100% reduction in H� armigera larval infestation within 7 days on tomato plants in a hot house environment. A 10-fold lower concentration, 1.15 x 106 OBs/mL, resulted in a 100%reduction within 16 days. Worldwide, the American bollworm (ABW), Helicoverpa armigera (Hubner), is regarded as a significant pest of cotton, Gossypium hirsutum. It evolved resistance against numerous insecticides as a result of its destructive eating behaviour and constant intake of the same poisons. In order to combat this pest, a combination of bio-and synthetic-pesticides must be applied. The entomopathogenic viruses, like nuclear polyhedrosis virus (NPV), a member of baculoviruses could be possible candidates for more effective ABW control. Nawaz et al� (2019) compared the effectiveness of NPV and Spinosad 240SC (at a concentration of 250 mL per hal) against ABW in the controlled environment. Different NPV and Spinosad concentrations were applied to the ABW both singly and in combination with 0.1% Spinosad. According to the findings, mortality rates at 1 109, 1 108, 1 107, 1 106, and 1 105 POBs were 95%, 92%, 84%, 82%, and 78%, respectively, with the highest concentrations showing the greatest mortality (95%) rates. When mixed with food, spinosad causes 100% death at a concentration of 0.8%, followed by 50.87%, 42.10%, 26.31%, and 22.80% mortality at concentrations of 0.4%, 0.2%, 0.1%, and 0.5%, respectively. The findings of this study demonstrated that NPV is a useful strategy for microbial management of ABW. The Spodoptera litura (F.) (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae) Oriental leaf worm moth is one of the most ferocious insect pests in a number of commercially significant crops, including cotton. It now resists the majority of common chemical pesticides. Evaluation of a combined application of bio-and synthetic pesticide against this insect is crucial. For the control of this pest, nuclear polyhedrosis virus (NPV) can be a powerful substitute. The goal of the current investigation was to assess the effectiveness of NPV and emamectin benzoate (Proclaim® 19EC) in vitro against three geographically diverse populations of S. litura. A combination of emamectin benzoate (EB 0.1 ppm) and three different concentrations of NPV (NPV-1 2 109, NPV-2 3 109, and NPV-3 4 109 POB ml1) were used to treat second and fourth larval instars. The findings revealed that across all experiments, NPV-3 + EB had the greatest mortality rate (83.28%), followed by NPV-2 + EB, NPV-1 + EB, EB, NPV-3, NPV-2, and NPV-3. The pathogenicity of the used infections was found to be directly connected to reductions in pupation, adult emergence, and egg eclosion. The findings of this study showed that combination application of NPV + emamectin benzoate was an efficient method for biorational control of S� litura (Yasin et al�, 2020). Lepidopteran pests infected by nucleopolyhedroviruses (NPV, Baculoviridae), which have a proven track record as safe and efficient biological pesticides. Here, we outline a novel strategy for creating NPV-based pesticides. This innovation requires taking advantage of both the distinctive mode of transmission of these viruses as collective infectious units and the genotypic variety found in natural virus populations. The selection of genotypic variants, mixing, coinfection, and intraspecific coocclusion of variants within viral occlusion bodies are all presented as steps in a ten-step process. This method can be utilised to create highly harmful virus formulations for pest management using two instances. Due to the NPV-based insecticides' limited host range, this technology has recently been modified to produce specially formulated interspecific virus mixtures that can be used to control complexes of lepidopteran pests on specific crops, provided that a common host species is available for virus production. This method of NPV-based pesticide creation has the potential to be used in a variety of NPV-pest pathosystems. Williams et al� (2022).

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: The repeated use of synthetic pesticides against major pests could be the main reason of pest resurgence, and this study results would provide useful insight to build a framework for future investigations and to reduce the use of toxic chemicals for the control of insect pests of major crops. The study methodology for NPVs has developed over the past 25 years towards a more ecologically holistic approach with industry concerns. Although viral pesticides currently only account for a very small fraction of plant protection, their significance was recognised. Even though NPVs have gained popularity as safer alternatives to chemical insecticides, they continue to confront challenges in terms of manufacture, marketing, and use. Effective multidisciplinary research, governmental, corporate, and human relationships are crucial. Farmers, extension agents, and policymakers must have in-depth knowledge of biopesticides. Ineffective rules may result in poor performance and quality of products as well as a drop in confidence among consumers. There is tremendous potential for NPVs that can function well across a wider range of situations and hosts. Prioritize research to improve bio-agent integration into production systems, such as by alternating them with chemical pesticides and creating efficient bio-models for them.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Trichoderma spp. are free living, filamentous fungi that multiply rapidly on many substrates such as soil, decaying wood materials and as root colonizers. Trichoderma spp. have a high potential for reproduction and sporulation, as well as competitive ability and saprophytic survival. The escalating need for eco-friendly and sustainable agriculture has driven extensive research on biological control agents like Trichoderma. These beneficial fungi exhibit potent antagonistic activity against plant pathogens, offering a promising alternative to conventional chemical pesticides. This review aims to provide insights into the mass production of Trichoderma and its utilization as a biocontrol agent for managing plant diseases in agriculture. Trichoderma strains in natural habitats may provide more predictable and effective control of plant diseases. For effective biological control of soil borne plant pathogens, a major consideration is antagonist proliferation after introduction into the soil or rhizosphere. Successful antagonist should produce inoculums in excess to survive and proliferate in soil and rhizosphere. Trichoderma spp. are biocontrol agents extensively used in management of fungal diseases of crop plants exhibiting mycoparasitism against a wide range of plant pathogens. Their usage has been very much successful against soil borne diseases for which no resistant sources have been identified in the plants. The National Farmer Policy (2007) has strongly recommended the promotion of biopesticides for increasing agricultural production, sustaining the health of farmers and environment. Identification and quantification of ecological stresses affecting the establishment and the population dynamics of introduced Trichoderma strains in natural habitats may provide more predictable and effective control of plant diseases. Trichoderma spp� (and probably other organisms, such as nonpathogenic Fusarium and Rhizoctonia strains, mycorrhizae, and other fungi) infect roots and have intrinsic ability to be plant pathogens, but their infection are limited to root superficial cells that is a remarkable phenomenon. It also includes the clause that biopesticides would be treated at par with chemical pesticides in terms of support and promotion. Bioagents like, T� viride, T� harzianum and T� virens are being successfully used for the control of some of the dreaded diseases like, foot rot of black pepper, root rots of pulses, damping off, collar rots and Fusarium wilts of horticultural crops.

div.head: Characterization of Trichoderma species
div.p: Trichoderma encompasses a diverse group of fungi with substantial variation in their biocontrol efficacy. Their mycoparasitic nature and ability to produce an array of secondary metabolites contribute to their antagonistic activity against phytopathogens. Trichoderma strains are characterized based on their morphological, molecular, and biochemical traits, enabling the selection of the most suitable strains for specific biocontrol applications. To develop a successful Trichoderma formulation, Trichoderma should possess: 1. High rhizosphere competence. 2. Enhanced plant growth. 3. High competitive saprophytic ability. 4. Ease for mass multiplication. 5. Compatible with other bioagents. 6. Excellent and reliable control. 7. Safe to environment. 8. Broad spectrum of action. 9. Should tolerate desiccation, heat, oxidizing agents and UV radiations.

div.head: Environmental Factors
div.p: Temperature plays a crucial role in the mass multiplication of Trichoderma. The ideal temperature range for Trichoderma growth is around 25±2°C, while growth is significantly reduced below 10°C or above 35°C. Other environmental factors such as humidity and pH also influence the growth and viability of Trichoderma cultures. Proper control of these factors ensures optimal biomass production and viability of the biocontrol agent.

div.head: Methods of mass multiplication of Trichoderma
div.p: Trichoderma spp. are beneficial fungi used in agriculture and horticulture for their biocontrol and plant growth-promoting properties. Mass multiplication of Trichoderma involves propagating large quantities of these fungi for commercial or large-scale applications. Mass production of Trichoderma involves various fermentation methods, including liquid fermentation, solid fermentation, and semi-solid fermentation. Liquid fermentation offers efficient and rapid growth of Trichoderma in submerged cultures, while solid fermentation relies on surface colonization of solid substrates. Semisolid fermentation provides a balance between the advantages of liquid and solid fermentation. Additionally, formulation techniques like talc-based and press mud-based formulations improve shelf life and ease of application for commercial products.

div.head: Liquid fermentation
div.p: Liquid fermentation is conducted in bioreactors under optimized conditions of temperature, pH, and aeration. The liquid medium is enriched with nutrients to support Trichoderma growth and biomass production. Liquid formulation involves cultivating Trichoderma in liquid media such as nutrient broths or fermentation tanks. The fungi are allowed to grow and proliferate in the liquid medium. Once the desired biomass concentration is reached, the liquid formulation can be used directly or further processed to produce concentrated suspensions or spore solutions. Liquid formulations are convenient for application through irrigation systems or foliar sprays.

div.head: Solid fermentation
div.p: In solid fermentation, Trichoderma is cultivated on solid substrates such as grains, agricultural residues, or industrial by-products. The fermentation process allows Trichoderma to colonize the surface of the substrate. The process involves inoculating the chosen substrate with Trichoderma spores or mycelium and allowing them to grow and colonize the substrate. After sufficient growth, the Trichoderma biomass is harvested and dried to form solid pellets or granules, which can be used as biocontrol agents or soil amendments.

div.head: Sugarcane baggase
div.p: The sugarcane baggase is easily or almost freely available substrate for mass multiplication of fungal agent. The sugarcane baggase contains much more amount nutrition for growth of fungal agent Trichoderma candidum. The highest CFU count was recorded on solid.

div.head: Semi-solid fermentation
div.p: Semi-solid fermentation involves the cultivation of Trichoderma on substrates with intermediate moisture levels. It offers advantages like higher spore yield and simplified downstream processing.

div.head: Talc-based formulation
div.p: Talc-based formulations are produced by mixing Trichoderma propagules with talc powder, providing an easy-to-apply, dry, and durable product. Trichoderma is grown in the liquid medium is mixed with talc powder in the ratio of 1:2 and dried to 8% moisture under shade. The talc formulation of Trichoderma has shelf life of 3 to 4 months. It has become quite popular in India for management of several soil-borne diseases of various crops through seed treatment at 4 to 5 g/kg seed. Several private industries produce large quantities of talc formulations in India for supply to the farmers.

div.head: Vermiculite-wheat bran-based formulation
div.p: This formulation method combines vermiculite and wheat bran as carriers for Trichoderma biomass. Vermiculite is a mineral that retains moisture and provides aeration, while wheat bran acts as a nutrient source for the fungi. The Trichoderma spores or mycelium are mixed with the carrier materials, and the formulation is then processed into granules or pellets.

div.head: Press mud-based formulation
div.p: Press mud, a by-product of the sugar industry, can serve as a carrier for Trichoderma propagules. This cost-effective formulation enhances nutrient content and promotes sustained release.

div.head: Village level mass multiplication of Trichoderma
div.p: Trichoderma can be easily produced in rural areas by the farmers themselves using simple and cheap materials locally available in the village. Trichoderma can be produced three types: 1. Cheap grains (Sorghum, Maize, wheat) 2. Liquid media (Potato jiggery medium) 3. Agricultural by-products (Rice bran, Wheat bran)

Trichoderma grown on sorghum or other grains is powdered and used like talc formulation for disease control. Trichoderma grown in liquid media will be used to make the talc formulation of Trichoderma, which can be used for, seed treatment, root dip and soil application for disease control.

div.head: Uses as plant disease management
div.p: Trichoderma-based biocontrol agents have demonstrated efficacy in managing various plant diseases caused by fungi, bacteria, and nematodes. They stimulate plant growth, induce systemic resistance, and directly antagonize pathogens, leading to enhanced crop health and reduced disease incidence. Trichoderma spp� has gained significant attention and use in plant disease management due to their biocontrol capabilities. These beneficial fungi can effectively suppress plant pathogens and contribute to sustainable agriculture. Here are some of the key ways Trichoderma is used for plant disease management:

div.head: Biological Control of Soil-Borne Pathogens
div.p: Trichoderma species are well-known for their antagonistic activity against various soil-borne pathogens, such as Fusarium, Rhizoctonia, Pythium, and Sclerotinia� When applied to the soil, Trichoderma colonizes the rhizosphere (root zone) and competes with the pathogenic fungi for space and nutrients. It also produces antifungal compounds that inhibit the growth of pathogens, thus reducing disease incidence.

div.head: Seed Treatment
div.p: Trichoderma-based formulations can be used for seed treatment. Coating seeds with Trichoderma spores or mycelium helps establish a beneficial microbial population around the seedlings' roots from the beginning. This early protection can prevent soil-borne pathogens from infecting the emerging seedlings.

div.head: Root Drench and Soil Drench
div.p: Trichoderma products in liquid formulations can be applied as root drench or soil drench to protect plants from soil-borne diseases. When applied to the base of plants or directly to the root zone, Trichoderma establishes a protective barrier and prevents pathogens from infecting the root system.

div.head: Foliar Spray
div.p: Some Trichoderma strains have the ability to colonize above-ground plant parts. Foliar sprays of Trichodermaa-based products can enhance plant defenses against foliar diseases caused by certain pathogens. The presence of Trichoderma on the leaf surface can induce systemic resistance, priming the plant to respond more effectively to pathogen attacks.

div.head: Post-Harvest Disease Management
