div.p: Trichoderma-based products can also be used for post-harvest disease management. Applying these formulations on harvested fruits or vegetables can suppress the growth of post-harvest pathogens, extending the shelf life of produce and reducing post-harvest losses.

div.head: Inducing Systemic Resistance
div.p: Trichoderma spp� can trigger the plant's systemic resistance mechanisms. When a plant is exposed to Trichoderma, it initiates a signaling cascade that leads to the activation of defence genes, making the plant more resistant to various diseases.

div.head: Nematode Management
div.p: Certain Trichoderma strains have nematode-suppressive activity. They can help control plant-parasitic nematodes, either directly through predation or indirectly by stimulating the plant's defence against nematode infection.

div.head: Commercial product for biocontrol agent
div.p: The successful mass production of Trichoderma has facilitated the development of commercial biocontrol products. These formulations offer convenient and practical solutions for integrated disease management practices in modern agriculture. Trichoderma harzianum strain T22: This strain of Trichoderma is commonly used as a biofungicide to protect against various plant pathogenic fungi. Trichoderma asperellum: Another popular strain used in commercial biofungicides to control plant diseases. Trichoderma virens: This strain is known for its ability to colonize plant roots and protect against soil-borne pathogens. Trichoderma koningii: Used in biofungicides to target diseases caused by soil-borne pathogens. Trichoderma viride: A widely used strain in commercial products for its effectiveness against plant pathogens.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Trichoderma species possess immense potential as biocontrol agents for managing plant diseases in agriculture. Their mass production through various fermentation methods and formulation techniques has opened up new avenues for sustainable crop protection. Emphasizing the commercialization and adoption of Trichoderma-based products can contribute to environmentally friendly and economically viable strategies for plant disease management. Trichoderma spp� possess many qualities and they have great potential use in agriculture such as amend abiotic stresses, improving physiological response to stresses,alleviating uptake of nutrients in plants, enhancing nitrogen-use efficiency in different crops, and assisting to improve photosynthetic efficiency. Nonetheless, continuous efforts are necessary to raise awareness, educate farmers, and integrate Trichoderma biocontrol into mainstream agricultural practices to maximize its potential for disease management and overall crop health. Embracing Trichoderma as a biocontrol agent holds the promise of achieving sustainable and resilient agricultural systems while preserving the environment for future generations.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Semiochemicals, which can refer to both intraspecies and interspecies communication chemicals, was first coined by Law and Regnier in 1971 (simeon-marker or signal). Semiochemicals are chemical signals that cause different behavioral reactions in organisms. In most groups of animals, chemical communication seems to be the main means of information transfer. Since 1971, the term "semiochemical" has been in use. It is derived from the Greek word "semeon," which meaning "sign" or "signal. Semiochemicals are communication agents that alter the behaviour of living things by carrying information between them (Dicke M and Sabelis M W, 1988). They are emanated by one individual and result in a reaction in another. According to Krieger and Breer (1999), the majority of invertebrates use smell as their primary sensory modality to perceive their surrounding environment. Pheromones and allelochemicals are two categories of semiochemicals that function inside and between species, respectively. Allelochemicals can be further classified into signals that are beneficial to the emitter (allomones), the receiver (kairomones), or both (synomones). Pheromones have evolved for communication reasons and can be classified as sex, alarm, aggregation, or territory marking signals. (Nordlund et al�, 1981). Chemical communication is often the main method by which animals exchange information, as observed in various animal groups. The presence of a highly developed sense of smell allows for the detection of even faint signals from prospective partners, as well as distinguishing between favourable and unfavourable environments for feeding, mating, and egg-laying. Semiochemicals refer to organic substances utilized by insects to communicate particular chemical messages that impact behaviour or physiology (El-Sayed, 2015). They play a crucial role in modifying the actions and physical processes of these insects.According to Bruce et al� (2015), the attraction of insects to plants and other hosts depends on the identification of particular Semiochemicals or particular combinations of Semiochemicals.

div.head: Historical background
div.p: Numerous animals were used in the pheromone research. In fact, more than 80% of all species surveyed have been the subject of studies on insect semiochemicals, particularly pheromones. This is likely because there is growing interest in their possible application in the control of insect pests. The French naturalist Jean Henri Fabre wrote the earliest works on the possible function of pheromones when he noted in 1870 that the female peacock moth, Saturniapyri, could draw dozens of male moths from great distances (Schwarcz, 2003). In 1870, Joseph Linter observed that female spice bush silk moths attracted 50 males, and he recommended that chemists step up to assist entomologists in the identification of pheromones.

div.head: I) Allelochemical
div.p: A substance that is significant to organisms of a species different from source, for reasons other than food. Allomone: A chemical generated by an organism that, when it comes into contact with a member of a different species, causes the recipient to exhibit behavioural or physiological responses that are advantageous to the emitter but unfavourable to the recipient in terms of adaptation. Kairomone: A chemical that an organism produces or acquires that, when it comes into contact with a member of another species, causes the recipient to react in a way that is advantageous for receiver's ability to adapt. Synomone: A substance produced or obtained by an organism that, when it comes into touch with a member of a different species, causes the recipient to exhibit behaviour or experience physiological changes that are advantageous to both the emitter and the receiver in terms of adaptation. Apneumone: Achemical released by non-living substance that is beneficial to the receiver but detrimental to other organism in the substance.

div.head: II) Pheromones
div.p: A chemical that an organism secretes outside the body and that elicits a particular response in a receiving organism of the same species.

div.head: a) Sex pheromones
div.p: Sex pheromones are only released by one sex and cause the other sex to act in ways that promote mating. The majority of the time, females release them, while males can also release them. More than 150 kinds of insects have been reported to release sex pheromones, and roughly 50 species are produced by male insects. When the two sexes of an insect are brought together, aphrodisiacs are compounds that help with courtship. Aphrodisiacs are frequently made by male.
Differences between male and female produced pheromones

div.head: b) Aggregation pheromones
div.p: These pheromones are particularly prevalent in some species of beetles and they drive insects to gather or congregate at food sources, reproductive habits, and hibernation places. They are particularly well studied in relation to bark beetles like Dendroctonus spp. that attack trees. Both men and women find them attractive. They typically function over a long period of time and have the ability to draw thousands of people. Aggregation pheromones have chemical structures that are more complicated, less stable, and less easy to manufacture and application. They also elicit considerably more complex behaviour that is less responsive to manipulation.

div.head: c) Trial marking pheromone
div.p: Trail pheromones are produced by ants, termites, and some lepidopteran insect larvae that are foraging. They mostly serve as a guide for other colonists regarding where to obtain necessities. In order to create appealing foraging areas and to scent-mark unproductive food sources, bees use trail pheromones during foraging. As a rule, trail pheromones are less volatile than alarm pheromones. It may be possible to use bumble bees for pollination more effectively by identifying and synthesising their trail pheromone.

div.head: d) Alarm pheromones
div.p: These are typical of social insects that typically congregate, including ants, bees, and aphids. This kind of pheromone has three main purposes: to alert conspecifics, to elicit a defensive reaction, and to spur avoidance. For instance, a honey bees stinger that was left inside the victim's body generates an alarm scent that draws other bees and prompts them to sting.

div.head: e) Host-marking pheromones
div.p: By preventing pests from landing, eating, or ovipositing on their host plants, these pheromones lessen intraspecific competition. Being one of the few pheromones that repel insects rather than attracting them, they are so rare. The Coleopteran, Lepidoptera, Diptera, Homoptera, Orthoptera, and Hymenoptera are only a few of the insect orders from which they are known. The females of cherry fruit flies, R� cerasi (Linnaeus), and apple maggot, Rhagoletis pomonella (walsh), which lay their eggs in fruit, mark the surface to ward off other females. Numerous insects, including tsetse flies, house flies, and other dipteran species, produce mating-deterrent pheromones.

div.head: f) Recruitment pheromones
div.p: These are often utilised by social insects to keep the group in order, recruit new members, and plan their actions. In honeybees, worker bees emit the vasonov pheromone to attract new workers from outside the hive and to direct returning forager bees back to the colony.Bees elevate their vasonov gland-containing abdomens and vigorously fan their wings in order to produce this hormone. When bark beetles locate a good tree for colonisation, we can also discover these pheromones in them.

div.head: g) Recognition pheromones
div.p: To differentiate colony members from non-colony members, social insects such as ants, bees, and termites are used. These pheromones often consist of a mixture of straight or branched-chain simple hydrocarbons. One of the most significant pheromones discovered is the termite egg recognition pheromone (TERP), which is known to powerfully trigger the worker conduct of eggcarrying and grooming.

div.head: h) Retinue pheromones
div.p: These compounds, which the honey bee queen is said to release, are supposed to trigger the retinue attraction that motivates workers to feed, groom, and collect the queen's pheromones and spread them throughout the colony. The queen retinue pheromone contains nine distinct components, according to research.

div.head: Semiochemicals and their applications in insect pest control
div.p: Sex pheromones, primarily those of lepidopteran insects, which were among the first to be recognised and synthesised, are currently the semiochemicals that are utilised most frequently in the management of insect pests. Pheromones offer important properties such approximate species specificity, potency, and low toxicity when it comes to pest management. The semiochemicals serve not as 'killing agents' per se but rather as monitoring instruments (to optimise the timing of conventional chemical controls), therefore they are not a straight replacement for chemical insecticides. According to Reddy et al� (2005), the objectives of employing semiochemicals in pest management include • Tracking pest population trends • To ascertain if control is allowed • To change the behaviours of an insect pest or its foes to the pest's harm.

div.head: Advantages of using semiochemicals are
div.p: • They only negatively impact the targeted pest.
• They are comparatively non-toxic and only needed in small quantities.
• Environmentally safe and non-persistent.

According to Soroker et al� (2015), Semiochemicals have been utilised to manage insect pests for more than a century. Semiochemicals called insect sex pheromones are frequently utilised to control insect pests, notably those from the order Lepidoptera. It is also common practise to use aggregation pheromones from the Coleoptera order to manage economically significant agricultural insect pests. The carob moth Ectomyelois ceratoniae, the autumn armyworm Spodoptera frugiperda, the tomato leaf miner Tutaabsoluta, fruit flies Bactrocera sp., mountain pine beetle (MPB) Dendroctonus ponderosae, Asian citrus psyllid Diaphorina citri, and the red palm weevil (RPW) have all been successfully controlled by semiochemicals. Due to their natural origin, low environmental persistence, and species specificity-all of which greatly increase their toxicity to non-target organisms-semiochemicals are regarded as safe and environmentally benign substances.

div.head: Semiochemical-based pest control techniques Attract and kill (A&K)
div.p: The approach, also known as attract and kill, attract and infect, or attract and sterilise, uses an attractant or semiochemical to entice an insect to a point source holding a killing agent (insecticide, pathogen, or sterilant), as the name implies. By killing the target insect, lowering its fitness and fecundity, or rendering it inoperable by infecting it with a disease, the approach lowers the insect population.

According to Wizgall et al� (2010), semiochemicals have a practical use in detecting the presence and the amount of pest populations. The monitoring system assists in making decisions about pest control strategies that keep the pest population below the point at which it becomes economically unviable. Kairomones or pheromone baits or traps can be used for this. The biological control agent Rhizophagus grandis, a predator of the spruce bark beetle Dendroctonus micans population, has been observed using kairomone-based tarps often (Hosking et al�, 2003). Pheromone bait traps are more successful in monitoring insects than kairomones because pheromones are strong attractants and species-specific attractants (El-Sayed et al�, 2006). Insect pest damage can be evaluated using a pheromone-based monitoring system, together with population trends and abundance. The size of trap captures is used to establish catch thresholds, which are then used to time the application of control measures or to make that decision (Meurisse et al�, 2008).

div.head: Monitoring
div.p: The monitoring system aids in the choice of control measures to limit the pest population below the point at which it becomes unprofitable. Insect behaviour is now monitored by sex pheromones, aggregation pheromones, and kairomones. As a predator of the Dendroctonus micans population of the spruce bark beetle, Rhizophagus grandis, a biological control agent, kairomone-based traps have been most frequently employed to monitor this population (Hosking et al�, 2003). In addition to monitoring the presence of invasive species and preventing their establishment and spread, these pheromone traps are utilised to identify low-density populations (Trematerra, 2012).

div.head: Mating Disruption Strategy
div.p: In order to disrupt mating, a large quantity of artificial pheromones is released into the pest's habitat in a quantity that makes it difficult for the male to find the female, decreasing the organism's likelihood of reproduction and, as a result, lowering the prevalence of the insect in the following generation. This method was initially proven for the cabbage looper, but it is now a widely acknowledged choice for controlling a variety of lepidopteran pests of fruits and vegetables. After undertaking an extensive disruption programme, hot spots and edge infestations of the Oriental fruit fly moth were eliminated in Australia.According to some reports, the gipsy moth (Lymantria dispar) in North American forests, the codling moth (Cydia pomonella) in apple and pear trees worldwide, and the grapevine moth (Lobesiabotrana) in the EU and Chile were the three species with the highest land area under mating disruption technique (Witzgall et al�, 2010). In a recent study, the effectiveness of pheromone-based mating disruption strategies of the swede midge (Contarinia nasturtii), a member of the dipteran family, was evaluated in small-scale and large-scale fields of broccoli and cauliflower. In the large-scale studies, the crop damage was reduced on average by 91% and by 59% in the case of broccoli (Samitez et al�, 2012).

div.head: Mass Trapping Strategy
div.p: Mass trapping tries to capture a sizable fraction of a pest population prior to mating, oviposition, or feeding in order to stop crop damage. Using this method successfully requires a highly effective trap and an extremely alluring lure. The lure should be extremely active, eventually outcompeting the attracted substance that occurs naturally. Lepidoptera require that males be captured prior to mating, and this is most likely to be successful with insects that only pair once. Leucinodes orbonalis, a moth that damages the fruit and shoots of brinjal plants, has been controlled as a result of a combination of pest population decline and increased influence of natural enemies, whose numbers increased after insecticide usage was stopped (Tamiru and Khan 2017). When it comes to coleopterans, aggregation pheromone-based trapping tries to lower the population of both sexes before eggs are deposited or harm is caused by adults being fed. Recently, the mass trapping of the New Guinea sugarcane weevil, Rhabdoscleus obscurus, with ethyl acetate, cut sugarcane, and insecticide (permethrin) was demonstrated in Guam (Reddy et al�, 2005), as well as with the boll weevil, Anthonomus grandis, and the banana weevil, Cosmopolites sordidus (Miller et al�, 2010).

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: In the last two to three decades, the use of semiochemicals in pest management has advanced quickly due to a number of special advantages. These include the fact that they are species-specific and have no harmful effects on organisms other than the target species. Finally, because they are used sparingly while killing a large number of insects, they are undoubtedly cost-effective. This strategy saves manpower because a huge number of insect pests can be brought in from afar and exterminated directly at the door. Semiochemicals can't produce quick effects, hence they can't be used for immediate control measures. Only one sex can be attracted by sex pheromones; the other sex may still be present and still cause harm. Additionally, it is yet uncertain what semiochemicals are for many kinds of insect pests.The widespread usage of semiochemicals, such as tomato pinworm and pink bollworm sex pheromones, increases the risk of resistance forming. It is challenging to create long-lasting controlled release dispensers for chemicals that are frequently very volatile and prone to deterioration.The size of the prospective market, the cost of registration, and the competitiveness of the product's price all have a significant impact on the commercialization of goods based on semiochemicals. The development of goods based on Semiochemicals has undoubtedly been impeded by the big agrochemical companies' lack of economic interest.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The challenge for all disciplines of agriculture is to increases production and improves quality of agricultural produce. This is applicable to the discipline of plant protection and manage risk. In the past many pathogens are responsible for food scarcities including famines. In addition to endemic problems there are many crop pests which are entered India from other foreign countries because in India did not have an effective control measure (plant quarantine) system to stop the introduction of exotic pests, diseases and weeds. Cottony cushion scale, woolly aphid, San Jose scale, golden cyst nematode of potatoes are some exotic pests introduced into our country and cause extensive damage. In recent yearsthe increases in quantum of import and export of plant commodities is a more possibility of moving insect pests and diseases from their native habitation to new location. The methods of crop protection isto excludes the pests from entering in to new area. The method of exclusion of the pests is enforced by legal measures commonly known as Quarantine. It can be defined "as a legal restriction on the movement of Agricultural commodities for exclusion, prevention or delay in the spread of plant pests and diseases in new location.

div.head: Objectives of Plant Quarantine
div.p: • Tracing, identification and control of quarantine pests.
• Pest risk analysis, evaluation and determination of quarantine conditions for imported and transit plant shipments.

• Inspection, sampling, testing and undertaking quarantine regulations on imported and transit agricultural shipments. • Supervision and undertaking post-entry quarantine regulations on plants which are subject to the regulations. • Inspection, sampling, testing and issuing phytosanitary certificate for exporting plant consignments.

div.head: Importance
div.p: • Prevent the introduction and spread of non-native pests that are destroy the crops by regulating/restricting the import of materials. • Principles of plant quarantine as related to international trade General Principles 1. Sovereignty With the aim of preventing the introduction of quarantine pests into their territories, it is recognized that countries may exercise the sovereign right to utilize phytosanitary measures to regulate the entry of plants material capable of harbouring plant pests. 2. Necessity Countries shall institute and plant protection centresare restrictive measures by phytosanitary considerations, to prevent the introduction of quarantine pests. 3. Minimal impact Phytosanitary measures is consistent with the pest risk involved, and shall represent the least restrictive measures available which result in the minimum impediment to the international movement of people, commodities and conveyances. 4. Modification As conditions change or limitations, and as new facts become available, phytosanitary measures shall be modified promptly, either by inclusion of prohibitions, restrictions or requirements necessary for their success, or by removal of those found to be unnecessary. 5. Transparency Countries shall publish and disseminate phytosanitary prohibitions, restrictions and requirements and do not hide any wrong information, on request, make available the rationale for such measures. 6. Harmonization Phytosanitary measures shall be based on IPPCwhich are possible, on international standards, guidelines and recommendations, developed within the framework. 7. Equivalence Countries shall recognize as being equivalent those phytosanitary measures that are not identical but which have the same effect.

8. Dispute settlement It is preferable the dispute between two countries regarding phytosanitary measures be resolved at a technical bilateral level (two countries). If such a solution cannot be achieved within a reasonable period of time, further action may be undertaken by means of a multilateral settlement system (more than two countries). Specific Principles 9. Cooperation Countries shall cooperate to prevent the spread and introduction of quarantine pests, and to promote measures for their official control. Cooperation by both importer and exportercountries. 10. Technical authority Countries shall provide an official Plant Protection Organization and Institute. 11. Risk analysiswithin the guideline of the IPPC,countries shall use pest risk analysis methods based on biological and economic evidence and, wherever possible, follow procedures developed. 12. Managed risk Because some risk of the introduction of a quarantine pest always exists, countries shall agree to a policy of risk management when formulating phytosanitary measures. 13. Pest free areas Countries shall recognize the status of areas in which a specific pest does not occur (nonnative pest). On request, the countries in whose territories the pest free areas lie shall demonstrate this status based, where available, on procedures developed within the framework of the IPPC. 14. Emergency action Countries may, in the face of a new and/or unexpected phytosanitary situation, take immediate emergency measures on the basis of a preliminary pest risk analysis. Such emergency measures shall be temporary in their application, and their validity will be subjected to a detailed pest risk analysis as soon as possible. 15. Notification of non-compliance Importing countries shall promptly inform exporting countries of any non-compliance with phytosanitary prohibitions, restrictions or requirements before the imports the commodities. 16. Non-discrimination Phytosanitary measures shall be applied without discrimination between countries of the same phytosanitary status, if such countries can demonstrate that they apply identical or equivalent phytosanitary measures in pest management. Which are benefited for both the countries.

div.head: Guidelines for Import of Germplasm
div.p: • The plant product or plant material are import from a country where the pathogens is or minimum absent.

• The plant product import from a country with a standard plant quarantine service, so that regularly inspection and treatment is done. • Planting material are obtained from the safest known source or selected country. • Planting material obtain without chemical uses seeds so that detection of pathogens is facilitated. • Import the good, clean, healthy-looking type seeds.
• Obtain an official certificate ofimporting country to free from pests and diseases from the exporting country. • Nucleic acid hybridization,

• Polymarised chain reaction (PCR): These methods are used for molecular testing. Types of Plant Quarantine International quarantine International Standards for Phytosanitary Measures are governed by the Secretariat of the International Plant Protection Convention (IPPC) as part of the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization's (FAO) global programme of policy and technical assistance these standards, guidelines and recommendations to achieve international harmonization of phytosanitary measures, with the aim to facilitate trade and avoid the use of unjustifiable measures as barriers to tradein plant quarantine. Domestic quarantine Plant quarantine are governed by Destructive Insects and Pests Act (DIP Act) 1914. Therules and regulations issued prohibiting the movement of insects and disease their host from one state to another in India and therules and regulations framed from time to time there under by the Govt. of India (Anonymous, 1975). India brought forward a comprehensive Plants, Fruits and Seeds Order, 1984' which came into force in June 1985 called domestic quarantine (Anonymous, 1985). Plant quarantine measures: The measures suggested for effective plant quarantine are as follows. Import control i) Embargoes: Total prohibition of import of certain kind of infected material from particular region /country. This is most effective measure to exclude infected plant material. These are highly infective area which are totally banned by IIPC. ii) Inspection of seed lots: The examination of seed samples must be by the most sensitive and reliable method for detection of dangerous pathogens. A sample may subject to more than one method which are detect by various technique and method. iii) Post Entry Quarantine (PEQ): because it is difficult to detect all types of seed borne pathogens by simple tests, it may be necessary to subject seeds to PEQ. When pathogen are not which are not provide facilitate the environment condition so pathogen are not born in plant materials so require the PEQ. Seeds are subjected to a period of grow in quarantine station under strict supervision in importing countries.

div.head: Export control i) Field inspection of field crops:
div.p: The seed crop is inspected regularly for diseases infected plants are rouged and other weeding practises. The crop should meet requirements of the importing country.

ii) Inspection of seed lots: The seed lot is thoroughly examined for the presence or absence of the microorganisms and well cleared before export. The sample should meet the importing country. iii) Seed treatment: The seed lot should be treated by any chemical as per the requirements of the exporting country. However, the treatment should confine to regulations of the importing country. iv) Phytosanitary certificate: Phytosanitary certificates are issued by the exporting country for the healthy planting material are transport along with the seed as per the international plant protection convention.

div.head: Phytosanitary Certificate
div.p: It is an official document required when transport regulated articles such as plants, plant products or other regulated materials. It is generated from the exporting country's Department of agriculture, horticulture, food or water resources. It can only be issued by an authorized officer from a government department that is authorized by a National Plant Protection Agency (NPPO) and national plant protection station. The agency is in force to protect the threat of spreading pests, contamination or diseases into the country of import or new place. The importer will require a phytosanitary certificate to import regulated material including commodities such as plants, bulks & tubers or seeds for propagation, fruits & vegetables, cut flowers & branches, grain and growing medium. They may also be required for certain plant products that have been processed and have a potential for introducing regulated pests (examples include cotton or wood). Sometimes they can also be required to cover contaminated materials such as empty shipping containers, vehicles or other organisms. If the transporter cannot provide a phytosanitary certificate to the importer, it's highly likely that the goods will not be cleared through customs which is not transported from the arrived region. The commodities may be seized and completely destroyed. Phytosanitary certificates can be in paper form or, where it is accepted by the NPPO of the importing country, in electronic or by fax.

div.head: Problems in Plant Quarantine
div.p: • Don't detect the all type of infection of insect pests and pathogens by conventional method.
• Lack the organized services, institute and other quarantine at state level.
• Lack of inter-state border PQ check posts and railway and road links which are transfera the insect and microorganism with the plant product.

• The method may not be sensitive enough and hidden infestation to reveals traces of infection. Which are not control the spread of insect pest and disease. • The latent infectious may pass or not justified so undefected under post entry quarantine.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: PQ is regulatory program of managing and suppress the insect pests, disease and weeds associated with movement of plant/plant product between two countries and also between state or with in country.

PEQ is accomplished through prohibition, interception, elimination and eradication of pests, diseases and weeds at post entry point. An effective implementation of PEQ can management in India without risking environmental safety, economically, and ecological balance.Chemically based postharvest treatments are being replaced by biological and physical treatments and systems approaches. Drawing upon a wider knowledge base, by assessing risks in a more interdisciplinary fashion and including stakeholders from an inspection at early stage or before the regulations and actions should become more sustainable.Current implementation of quarantine is greatly emphasized for managing of pests, which in turn helps in conserving the productivity of crops and movement of pathogen from foreign countries.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: An agrochemical is a chemical product used in industrial agriculture. In most of the cases, agrochemicals refer to pesticides (insecticides, herbicides, fungicides, algaecides, rodenticides, molluscicides and nematicides), fertilizers, soil conditioners, liming and acidifying agents and plant growth regulators that provide benefits and manage agricultural ecosystem. Agrochemicals were introduced to enhance crop yields and minimized the crop losses due to pests and categories as fertilizers and pesticides. In the past sixty years with the wake of the green revolution (1960s), use of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium based inorganic fertilizers and pesticides has greatly increased food production by improving efficient and economical crop production in order to fulfil the food needs of the rapidly expanding world population (Pal et al�, 2006). The history of pesticide use can be divided into three periods of time.

During the first period before the 1870s, pests were controlled by using various natural compounds. During the second period, between 1870 and 1945, people began to use inorganic synthetic materials. At the end of the 1800s, people in Sweden used copper and sulfur compounds against fungal attack in fruit and potatoes. Since then, people have been using many inorganic chemicals, including the Bordeaux mixture, based on copper sulfate and lime arsenic, as pesticides. The third period started after 1945, represented by the use of synthetic pesticides with the discovery of the effects of DDT, BHC, Aldrin, Dieldrin, Endrin, Chlordane, Parathion, Captan and 2,4-D. After the entry of pesticides in agricultural ecosystem it become an integral part of agriculture and the research has continued into producing more selective pesticides. The pest, disease and weeds can loss more than half of our crops without pesticides, thus the pesticides enable farmers to produce safe, quantity & quality foods at affordable prices. According to Food and Agriculture Organization, the world highest pesticide consumption (13.35 kg active ingredient ha -1 ) was recorded in Taiwan followed by Japan (11.89 kg a.i.). In India, the present pesticide consumption is around 337g ha -1 (technical grade) which is lower than the world average of 645 g ha -1 and other developing countries. During 2021-22, the total cultivated area of India was about 173.99 mha and only 20.96% of that was devoid of any pesticide application. As per the record of Central Insecticide Board and Registration Committee (CIB&RC) updated in October 2022, a total of 318 pesticides have been registered in India for use in agriculture against various pests and diseases. These agrochemicals and pesticides are expensive and polluting (Bahadur et al�, 2015). As per current research, the global agrochemicals and pesticides market was estimated at 278428.78 USD million as of 2023 and it's anticipated to reach USD 320916.55 million by 2028, with a CAGR of 2.4% during the forecast years (Anonymous, 2023). The over and misuse of agrochemical and pesticides has precedence to immense health problem, economic loss and various environmental problem and had an impact on soil microorganisms as well as the entire ecosystem (Onder et al�, 2011). The overuse of agrochemicals also leads to environmental pollution such as air, water, soil pollution, etc. and cause imbalance in the ecosystem and it pollute water resources by getting into bodies of water (Aktar et al�, 2009). The primary decreasing biotic factors in agriculture are weeds and insects, which reduce crop production, productivity, and resource use effectiveness (Oliveira et al�, 2014). Besides, synthetic fertilizers and pesticides helped by increasing agricultural yield and shield crops from disease pathogens and phytophagous pests, respectively. In general, the dosage of the chemical, the characteristics of the soil, and many environmental conditions will affect the effects of pesticides on microorganisms differently.

div.head: Fig. 2. Impact of agrochemicals on environment
Agrochemicals and their effects
div.p: The persistent and careless use of agrochemicals has made various issues viz., deterioration of soil sterility, pest resurgence, destruction of pollinators and natural enemies, pesticide resistance, drug-resistant phytopathogens, human and animal health hazard, diseases and disorders of human limbs, destruction of native flora and fauna, issues related to bioaccumulation and bio magnification, climate change, environmental pollution etc. worse, posing an alarmingly serious threat to life and health. Unbalanced nutrient levels and decreased crop yielding capability are results of overusing these compounds, which also produce residues. According to Han and Farooq (2000), a severe problem arises when bacteria develop a resistance to pesticides and fish in various bodies of water have died due to pesticide usage.

div.head: Effect on environments
div.p: A healthy environment is essential for optimum human health and contamination of soil and water through human and industrial waste and agrochemicals is a universal problem and fact in developing countries. The harmful effects of agrochemicals on the environment are brought on by its chemical resources, which also appear to have a detrimental influence on people, animal and whole nature. Unrestrained domestic and industrial waste in urban and rural situations and pollution of reservoirs caused by agrochemical and pesticides runoff are an increasing concern. Deforestation and consequent soil erosion further aggravate these issues. Misuse of agrochemicals is a key cause of pollution of potable water, reservoirs, and watersheds and it is mostly attributable to irresponsible behaviour of farmers and agrochemical sellers and large government fertiliser subsidies that incentivise fertilizer overuse are fully preventable (Wimalawansa and Wimalawansa, 2014). Ultimately those are persisting for long time in the environment and causes environmental pollution.

div.head: Effect of agrochemicals on soil
div.p: The overuse of synthetic fertilizers and pesticides impacted as decline in organic matter, the soil's micro biota and microbial enzymes (Kalia and Gosal, 2011).However, the usage of herbicides impacts on the rhizobium-legume symbiosis (Drew et al�, 2007) and a decrease in the population of soil fungus has caused due to application of fungicide (Kalia and Gosal, 2011). Besides, the agrochemicals may also kill beneficial soil micro-organisms, increase nitrate level in soil, alter the soil pH, reduced soil quality by increasing residual effect and toxicity.

div.head: a) Impact of agrochemicals on soil microorganism
div.p: The soil micro flora plays a crucial role in the process of carbamate organic matter decomposition. The impact of pesticides on soil micro flora growth, activity, and enzyme levels may lead to negative consequences for soil fertility and overall soil health. The study conducted by Ghoshal and Hati (2019) studied that the utilization of Rynaxypyr, cartap hydrochloride, fipronil, and chlorpyrifos did not have any discernible adverse impacts on the population of collembolan. However, the application of carbofuran and phorate resulted in a reduction of 27.65% and 13.47%, respectively, in the rice-maize cropping system. Soil contamination or soil pollution is caused by the presence of xenobiotic (human-made) chemicals or other alteration in the natural soil environment. Industrial activity, agricultural chemicals or improper disposal of waste are on the hit-chart responsible for land degradation (Pangos et al�, 2013).

div.head: b) Impact of agrochemicals on soil microbial metabolism
div.p: Agrochemicals also interfere with microbial metabolism and change soil enzyme activity. Agrochemicals in soil are biodegraded in part by soil enzymes. Soil enzymatic activity is a significant bio indicator of soil health and fertility due to its function in maintaining soil equilibrium (Mandal et al�, 2020 andVirág D et al�, 2007). Herbicides, atrazine, and metolachlor, in particular, have a negative impact on the invertase and dehydrogenase enzymes' abilities to function. Fungicides including benomyl, mancozeb, thiram, and tridemorph block the action of phosphatase, urease, and dehydrogenase. Agrochemicals, particularly pesticides meant to kill certain target soil microorganisms, also cause nontarget microorganisms to multiply quickly and thrive.

div.head: Effects of agrochemicals on water
div.p: Agrochemicals pass into the water bodies by different processes such as runoff, drift and leaching via the soil or they may be applied directly into surface water to control some aquatic pests or mosquitoes. Insecticides and herbicides can affect fish and invertebrates and alter the species composition of aquatic commodities by mixing in surface water through spray-drift or run-off. About 41% of all amphibian species are threatened (IUCN 2020).

According to IUCN, 2009, one-third of 6000 amphibian species worldwide are threatened. Besides habitat loss, overexploitation, or introduced species, amphibians are affected by the pollution of surface waters with fertilizers and pesticides (Praween and Jan, 2020). Due to impact of agrochemical water becomes unfit for consumption, it promotes the growth of algae and it leads to eutrophication due to excess use of chemicals fertilizers. Aquatic animals are exposed to agrochemical or pesticides in three ways namely, breathing (uptake via gills during breathing), dermally (direct absorption via skin), and orally (entry via drinking contaminated water). It can affect aquatic forms by decreasing dissolved oxygen in the water and can trigger physiological and behavioural changes in fish populations. A study in India revealed the presence of Organochlorine pesticides in 58% of drinking water samples which were taken from various hand pumps and wells around Bhopal.

div.head: Effects of agrochemicals on Air
div.p: The quality measurement of air is based on the amount of pollutants in our atmosphere, including indoor and outdoor air. Agrochemicals particularly, pesticides in agriculture and urban settings have the potential to contaminate our air, affecting human, animal and plant health. Some pesticide ingredients stay in the atmosphere for short time period, while others can last longer. Agrochemical released into the air can settle to the ground, be broken down by sun light and water in the atmosphere, diffuse with air and altering their composition. Residues and particles of agrochemicals can lead to air pollution. Pesticides drift may be happens when air carries the agrochemicals particle from one place to another place and more amount of spray evaporation may be happen due to low relative humidity and higher temperatures. It can cause respiratory illness on surrounding organisms' health due to inhalation of polluted air by the use of agrochemicals.

div.head: Impact of agrochemicals on non-target flora
div.p: Herbicides have an impact on the variety of flora (Gove et al�, 2007). The use of herbicides has a greater impact on young plants. When plants are exposed to the highest concentrations of herbicides, their reproductive systems are impacted (Boutin et al�, 2014). Beginning with the second year of exposure, the spray of agrochemicals drift impacts the balance of species. Additionally, this disrupts the diversity of plant species. Fungicides and insecticides have a negative impact on non-target flora. Plant photosynthesis is impacted by the application of fungicides (Petit et al�, 2008). The metabolism of both carbon and nitrogen is also impacted (Saladin et al�, 2003). A decrease in floral visits and a change in the community of floral visitors both have an impact on pollination effectiveness (Cahill et al�, 2008). According to Jennings et al� (2012), pesticides also have an impact on plant development and germination.

div.head: Impact of agrochemicals on vertebrates
div.p: Organophosphates and carbamates impair vertebrates' ability to regulate body temperature, consume nutrients, and forage for food, reproduce, and other metabolic processes (Story and Cox, 2001). In addition to affecting the thyroid function in rodents, birds, amphibians, and fish (Brucker-Davis, 1998), insecticides, herbicides, and fungicides also have an impact on the immune system (Galloway and Handy, 2003). According to Sanchez-Bayo ( 2012), terrestrial vertebrates are less vulnerable to pesticides made from plant or fungal toxins than are birds and mammals.

div.head: Effect of agrochemicals on snails and slugs
div.p: Snails and slugs exhibit the capability to accumulate insecticides, specifically organophosphates and carbamates, within their anatomical structures. The present study revealed that the organisms under examination contained elevated levels of diazinon, phorate, and carbofuran, which can be attributed to their water solubility. The usage of insecticides might not pose any detrimental effects on the intended target, but it is deemed harmful towards the predatory avian species that subsist on the snails and slugs. These avian creatures are at severe risk of perishing as a result of the utilization of insecticides. Agrochemicals Chemical pesticides Plant growth regulators Fertilizers Soil conditioner and Chemicals used in animal husbandry.

div.head: Association between agrochemicals and climate change
div.p: A consequence of climate change is that agrochemical usage has grown as a result of an increase in pest and disease outbreaks brought on by warmer temperatures. Climate issues are also brought on by the production and application of agrochemicals to combat pests and illnesses. Fuel from fossil fuels must be burned in order to produce nitrogen-based fertilisers, which is an energy-intensive process. Chains of chemical reactions produce nitrous oxide, or N 2 O, a greenhouse gas, after farmers apply these synthetic fertilisers to crops. Compared to carbon dioxide and methane, N 2 O has a 265-times-greater potential to cause global warming. (https://www.bloombergquint.com/onweb/ synthetic-fertilizer-ammonium-nitrate-makes-climate-change-worse).

div.head: Management strategies of agrochemicals to minimise their effect
div.p: It's crucial to choose safe technologies for the use of agrochemical. The danger effect of agrochemicals can be minimized by through the use of safe working systems. The management strategies includes:
• Users of agrochemicals should keep in mind to ask for advice when necessary.

• Selection of pesticides based on its lowest toxicity to humans, mammals, fish, birds and other invertebrates. • Use less toxic agrochemical with higher soil absorption, lower water solubility, shorter half-life, most persistent and have greater chance of offsite movement via runoff. • There may be several varieties of spray equipment on the market, but the greatest ones are frequently those that are ideal for safety. • Agrochemical should have, low runoff potential e.g. liquid applications rather than granular formulations of pesticides. • It should science based holistic environmental management approaches aimed at reducing the possibility of pesticides and other chemicals and sediments of moving off treated areas into receiving water bodies. • Use of recommended doses of agrochemical Control the rate, method timing and type of chemicals being applied.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Agrochemicals are regarded as a potent tool or "magic bullet" in underdeveloped nations for increasing agricultural output and significantly raising key public health indicators. However, it has been shown that agrochemicals pose significant risks. The ecology is negatively impacted by pesticide use. The employment of biological techniques must be an option to using agrochemicals. The growth of the plants is impacted by agrochemicals. The use of agrochemicals results in the death of insects, pests, nematodes, weeds, etc., but they are also environmentally unfriendly and polluting. It is necessary to thoroughly investigate their molecular level mechanism.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The toxicant must be able to be applied effectively in order to come into touch with the pest or leaf and to leave a uniform and permanent deposit on the plant surface. Pesticide in chemically pure form is known as technical grade pesticides. These cannot be applied as such due to high toxicity and complex physical properties. They are converted into a ready to-use form known as formulation. Formulation converts the technical grade pesticide into a product which can be suitably diluted & applied by practical methods. Which have homogeneous distribution over large area, convenient to handle and more attractive to the pest. Formulation can be defined as "a physical mixture of pesticide with inert ingredients which can be suitably stored, transported and applied by practical means to achieve effective, safe and economic control of pests" or it is a substance which processed through formulation in order to increase its storage, handling, application, efficacy, safety for the user and the environment, and financial success. Grewal (2005) defined as "formulation is the process of creating a product from an active component by adding various active (functional) and non-active (inert) ingredients". or the formulants are defined as inert diluents, they are usually not required to be tested for chronic toxicity (R. Mesnage et al�, 2013Mesnage et al�, & 2019)). It is the last physical state in which pesticide is offered for sale. The mode of application is generally constrained by the formulation, which describes what and how other ingredients are combined with the active component. There are several "formulas" for pesticides and majority of technical pesticides are created by combining one or more active ingredients (a.i.), which kill pests with inert substances, diluents, preservatives, adjuvants, etc. prior to use in order to create a product that is efficient, simple to handle and apply, has a good shelf life, and is free of unfavourable side effects. The qualities of the inert component, coupled with the properties of the active ingredient, define the signal word that is shown on the product label. Some inert substances offer health concerns to pesticide handlers or applicators. Due to the inert ingredients present in the formulation, pesticide products with the same active ingredient are marketed in the form of granules, dusts, solutions, wettable powder, flowable suspension concentrate, emulsifiable concentrate, spray able oils, aerosols etc. (Muntz et al�, 2016).

A formulation is categorised as based on their physical condition in the container at the time, solids or liquids buy time. More than one active component may be included in a formulation, and many of them must be further diluted with a suitable carrier (such as water) before usage. Several alternative formulations of the same pesticide are frequently offered for sale and it is grouped in two major categories: The pesticide formulations are also grouped on the basis of their physical appearance in the market as solid, liquid and other formulations:

div.head: Emulsifiable granules (EG):
div.p: It is a granular formulation, which may contain water-insoluble formulants, to be applied as an oil-in-water emulsion of the active constituent(s) after disintegration in water.

div.head: Emulsifiable powders (EP):
div.p: It is a powder formulation, which may contain water-insoluble formulants, to be applied as an oil-in-water emulsion of the active constituent(s) after dispersion in water.

div.head: Water soluble gel (GW):
div.p: It is a gelatinized formulation to be applied as an aqueous solution.
6. Water Soluble Granules (SG): It is a granule formulation to be applied as a true solution of the active ingredient after dissolution in water, but which may contain insoluble inert ingredients.

div.head: Water soluble powder (SP):
div.p: A powder preparation to be applied as a true solution of the active substance after solution in water but which may contain insoluble. These mixtures resemble wettable granules in appearance. But when combined with water, soluble powders easily dissolve and create a real solution. There is no need for extra agitation after a thorough mixing. Soluble powders include an active component in concentrations ranging from 15% to 95%.

div.head: Water soluble powder for seed treatment (SS):
div.p: It is a powder formulation to be dissolved in water before application to the seed. 9. Tablet Formulation (Main components: effervescent tablet includes active ingredients, effervescent disintegrants, thinners, adhesives, surfactants, lubricants, glidants and others.

It is characterized by low or no water solubility, a complementary delivery system containing an organic acid, an inorganic base, a dispersant, a disintegrant, and a wetting agent an contain about 20% to 75% of a pesticide.

a) Water soluble tablets (ST): It is a tablet formulation to be used individually, to form a solution of the active ingredient after disintegration in water. The formulation may contain water-insoluble formulants.
b) Water-dispersible tablets (WT): It is a tablet formulation to be used individually, to form a dispersion of the active constituent after disintegration in water.

10. Water dispersible granules (WDG): These are granule-sized, dustfree particles made from compressed WP formulations. The granules disintegrate into a fine powder when they are stirred in water. These products contain wetting agent and dispersing agent similar to those used in wettable powders, but the dispersing agent is usually at higher concentration. Water dispersible granules are usually more expensive than older types of formulations (dusts, wettable powders) but their safety and greater convenience regarding application make them still desirable for many users (Knowles, 2008).

11. Wettable powder (WP): (Ingredient: pesticide, diluents/ carrier, wetting and dispersing agents) These are the pesticide formulation consisting of the active ingredient in a finely ground state combined with wetting agents and sometimes bulking agents (www.crodacropcare.com). Wettable powder are free-flowing powders that contain wetting and dispersion agents, carriers, and a majority of technical material between 25 and 75 percent. When dissolved in water, WPs create suspension.
12. Water-dispersible powder for slurry seed treatment (WS): It is a powder formulation to be dispersed at high concentration in water before application as slurry to the seed.

13. Dust (D): (Ingredients: Pesticide, Carrier/Diluents): It is a pesticide formulation in which, the toxicant is diluted either by mixing with or by impregnation on a suitable finely divided carrier. The finished product may contain 0.1-25 per cent of the toxicant. The particle size of dusts ranges between 1 and 40. In general, the toxicity of an insecticide increases as the particle size decreases.

14. Baits (B): (Composition: Pesticide, Diluents, food attractant): These are ready-to-use formulations (RTUs) that combine an active component with food or another alluring material. Pests are either drawn to the bait or it is placed in an area where they will locate it.

div.head: Liquid formulation A. Simple solution
div.p: 1. Solutions for seed treatment (LS): It is a clear to opalescent liquid to be applied to the seed either directly or as a solution of the active constituent after dilution in water. The liquid may contain water-insoluble formulants.
2. Oil-miscible liquids (OL): It is a homogeneous liquid formulation to be applied as a homogeneous liquid after dilution in an organic liquid.
3. Soluble concentrates (SL): It a clear to opalescent liquid to be applied as a solution of the active constituent after dilution in water. The liquid may contain water-insoluble formulants.

4. Ultra-low-volume (ULV) liquids (UV): It is a homogeneous liquid ready for use through ultra-low-volume (ULV) sprayer. These products are designed to be sprayed without dilution, and thus must be applied very carefully with specialized equipment. In addition to the active ingredient and a solvent system in which the active is very soluble, UL products often contain surface-active agents and drift control substances.

div.head: B. Solutions for dispersion
div.p: 1. Dispersible concentrates (DC): It is a liquid homogeneous formulation to be applied as a solid dispersion after dilution in water.

div.head: Emulsifiable concentrate (EC):
div.p: (Ingredient: Pesticide, Solvents, Emulsifiers, Stabilizers)The formulation consists of the toxin, the toxin's solvent, and an emulsifier. It is a transparent solution that, when diluted with water to spray strength, forms an emulsion of the oil-in-water kind.
Spraying a solvent causes it to swiftly evaporate, leaving a toxicant deposit where water also evaporates.

div.head: C. Emulsions 1. Emulsions for seed treatment (ES):
div.p: This formulation is a stable emulsion for application to the seed, either directly or after dilution.

2. Emulsions, oil-in-water (EW): (Composition: Active ingredient, emulsifier, antifreezing agent anti-foaming agent and preservative and water); This formulation is physically stabilised by specifically identified polymeric surfactants incorporated at an appropriate level. The emulsion has already been established in the formulation and is only diluted further in the spray mixture. They can be considered as a safer and more environmentally friendly alternative to emulsifiable concentrates. In an EW the continuous phase is water (as opposed to an organic solvent for ECs) which offers the benefit of lower phytotoxicity, no flashpoint concern, ease of handling, and a lower environmental impact.

3. Microemulsion (ME) (Composition: Active ingredient, Min. solvent Emulsifier, Co-surfactant water); Microemulsion is water-based formulations with a very small emulsified droplet size; this makes the formulation transparent. It is thermodynamically stable over a wide temperature range due to this very fine droplet size, usually between 0.01 and 0.05 μm.

div.head: D. Suspensions 1. Aqueous capsule suspensions (CS):
div.p: This formulation is designation for a stable suspension of micro-encapsulated active ingredient in an aqueous continuous phase, which is normally intended for dilution with water before use.

div.head: Flowable concentrates for seed treatment (FS):
div.p: It is a modification of suspension concentrates with supplemental additives for adhesion to the seed surface and colourants as safety markers to indicate that a seed has been treated with a product. It can be to the seed, either directly or after dilution and now the most popular type of seed treatment because they are concentrated formulations and are safer to apply as they are water based. 3. Oil-based suspension concentrates (OD): It is stable suspension of active constituent(s) in water-immiscible fluid, which may contain other, dissolved active constituent(s), intended for dilution with water before use.

It is containing high melting a.i. with low solvent solubility and dispersed in oil as fine particles. 4. Aqueous suspension concentrates (SC): (Composition: Active ingredient, surfactants, thickener, antifreezing agent, antifoaming); The active component is included in this liquid formulation as a solvent that is compatible with water. During spraying, the solvent is combined with water and dissolves, leaving the active component intact. A surfactant is used to provide wetting power. They are created using a wet grinding technique, and their range of particle sizes is 1 to 10 m. Small particles are kept from re-aggregating during the grinding process by inert substances that are adsorbed onto particle surfaces. Due to the active ingredient's increased access to plant tissues due to the smaller particle size, these substances frequently display improved bio efficacy when used. They have several benefits since they are water-based, including ease of pouring and measuring, operator and environmental safety, and affordability. Consequently, they are growing in popularity as a formulation type (Woods, 2003;Knowles, 2005).

div.head: E. Dual character liquid formulation
div.p: 1. Aqueous suspo-emulsions (SE): (Composition: Insoluble solid (a.i.) as dispersed particle, insoluble liquid (a.i.), as oil droplets, surfactants, water as bulk phase); It is a fluid, heterogeneous formulation consisting of a stable dispersion of active constituent(s) in the form of solid particles and fine globules in a continuous water phase. The formulation is intended for dilution into water prior to spray application.

div.head: F. Multi character liquid formulation 1. Mixed formulations of CS and SC (ZC):
div.p: It is a mixed formulation of capsule suspensions and suspension concentrates. This formulation consists a stable aqueous suspension of microcapsules of one technical material combined with a fine particle of second technical material in an aqueous phase together with suitable formulants. In microcapsules, the active ingredient is inside discrete, inert, polymeric microcapsules to provide slow release second active ingredient is free to provide immediate effect. This formulation is intended for dilution into water prior to spray application and it is easy to handle and measure, dust free, non-flammable and offer good miscibility with water. It is working as broad spectrum pesticide formulation. 2. Mixed formulations of CS and EW (ZW): It is a mixed formulation of capsule suspensions and emulsions, oil-in-water. The formulation consists of an emulsion of fine droplets of technical material in the form of the combination of a suspension of microcapsules in an aqueous phase together with suitable formulants. In the case of microcapsules, the active ingredient is present inside discrete, inert, polymeric microcapsules. After gentle agitation the material must appear homogeneous and be suitable for dilution in water worked and provide a broad-spectrum pest control.

div.head: Mixed formulations of CS and SE (ZE):
div.p: It is a mixed formulation of capsule suspensions and suspo-emulsions. The formulation consists of an emulsion of fine droplets of technical material and a suspension of fine particles of technical material in an aqueous phase, together with suitable formulants. After gentle agitation the material must appear homogeneous and be suitable for dilution in water.

div.head: G. Formulations prepared as devices
div.p: 1. Mosquito coils (MC): It a coil that burns without producing a flame and releases the active constituent into the local atmosphere as a vapour. 2. Vaporising mats (MV): It is a mat, which made from pulp, or other suitable inert materials, and impregnated with an active constituent.
The mat is intended for use in a heating unit designed to produce slow volatilisation of the active constituent.

div.head: Liquid vaporisers (LV):
div.p: It is liquid formulation in a cartridge, designed to fit a suitable heating unit, from which the formulation passes up a heated wick and evaporates into the local atmosphere.

div.head: H. Other formulations 1. Aerosol dispensers (AE):
div.p: It consist of a liquid formulation in a pressurised, non-refillable aerosol dispenser, containing propellant(s), synergist(s) and other formulants, as required, intended for release of the active ingredient into the air in the form of an aerosol.

div.head: Controlled release formulation (CR) (Composition: Active ingredient
div.p: Polymer, Surfactant) Controlled-release formulations are absorbed more slowly than conventional tablets and produce more stable serum levels during the day and at night, even when given twice daily as compared with conventional tablets given three times daily. Peak serum concentrations are usually attained within 4-8 h with plain tablets. 3. Neem based water dispersible granules: It is a neem-based insecticide formulation with 0.1-3 mm diameter particles which are free flowing and dust free with a homogenous dispersion in water. 4. Nanoemulsions of botanicals: Many botanical based nanoemulsions viz., castor oil nanoemulsion, cashew nut shell liquid, neem nanoemulsion + biodiesel waste as co-solvent and neem oil nanoemulsion + botanical synergist are also available.

Nanopesticides: These are nanostructures with two to three dimensions between 1 to 200 nm, used to carry agrochemical ingredients (AcI) and synthesized by using carbon nanotubes and graphene-based nanoparticles. In formulation development and drug delivery, the term 'nanoparticles' refers to a variety of particles used to deliver drug products or proteins to their target site. These include liposomes, solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs) micelles and polymeric nanoparticles. it increases the solubility of water-insoluble pesticides, attacks only on target pests, and protects the premature degradation of pesticide. The common nanocarrier types include polymers (b1-b4) such as chitosan, cellulose and polyethylene existing in the forms of nanocapsules (b1), nanospheres (b2), nano(hydro)gels (b3) and nanomicelles (b4), clay NMs (for example, silica, montmorillonite and kaolinite; b5), nanocomposites (b6), carbon nanotubes (CNTs; b7) etc. (Rajna et al�, 2019).

div.head: Purposes of pesticidal formulation
div.p: Formulations improves the properties of a chemical for handling, storage, application and may substantially influence effectiveness and safety (Knowles, 1998).

The following purposes of pesticidal formulations are mentioned below: a) To make improve handling and application of pesticide easy b) To maximize biological efficacy c) To improve defects in pesticides d) To make pesticides safer for workers and users e) To reduce harmful effects and impact on non-target organism and the environments f) To give various order to pesticides in order to broaden their spectrum of activity

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Pesticide development, usage, disposal, and storage continue to be issues. Despite the fact that contemporary agrochemical businesses are dedicated to creating secure formulations to prevent persistent and bio accumulative.

Given the inherent properties of chemicals, it is difficult to anticipate and prevent all potential detrimental effects of pesticides on human health and entire ecosystems. Even contemporary pesticides with purportedly short halflives can linger in soil for up to many years in some circumstances, posing a risk to soil invertebrates. Alternative approaches (such as the creation of bio pesticides) might reduce harmful qualities and hasten the emergence of resistance in the quest of safety. To decrease the amount of dangerous ingredients in pesticide products and innovative technology are actively being developed. For instance, Nano carriers lessen pesticide losses and delay release, due to improved affinity to the target pest species, and increase the precision of delivery of active chemicals. Government regulations are a crucial part in ensuring the safety of agricultural goods for consumers. The testing of formulants' safety for non-target species based on thorough and trustworthy data on their effects, the necessary examination of the long-term impacts of adjuvants, and improving the predictive capacity of formulants.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Pesticide application plays a major role in effectively stabilizing crop yield by reducing losses to crop pests in agriculture (Cooper and Dobson 2007).

Proper technique of application of pesticide and the equipment used for applying pesticide are important to the success of pest control operations. The mains purpose of pesticide application technique is to cover the target species and safety to the non-target organisms and the environment. The complete knowledge of pest is essential for correct time of application. All pesticides are poisonous substances and they can cause harm to all living things. Chemicals are widely used for controlling disease, insects and weeds in the crops (Mayur et� al 2023). They are able to save a crop from pest attack only when applied in time. They need to be applied on plants and soil in the form of spray, dust or mist. The important methods of applying pesticides are dusting and spraying. However, spraying is the most common method now because of certain disadvantages associated with dusting like: i) The loss of chemical due to drift and consequent pollution risk.

ii) Less efficient deposition of dust particles on plant surface resulting in decreased efficiency in pest control. iii) Tendency of the chemicals in dust formulation to separate itself from the carrier, if its density is widely different from that of the latter, resulting in loss of chemicals. Dusting will be an useful method of application of chemicals in areas like the vast stretches of unirrigated crop tracts with limited water supply, in hilly terrains and also in case of control of pests occurring in crop just before harvest like ear head bugs and caterpillars. Certain disadvantages with dusting over spraying. They are : i) Requirement of loss labour for the operation, ii) Coverage of a larger extent of crop per day than with spraying and iii) Light weight of dusting application with less risk of corrosion. Appliances that are used for applying dry dust formulation of pesticides are called dusters. All dusters consist essentially of a hopper which usually contains an agitator, an adjustable orifice or metering mechanism and delivery tubes. A rotary fan or a bellow provides the conveying air. There are two types of dusters. i) Manually operated dusters 1. Bellows duster 2. Rotary dusters 3. Plunger dusters ii) Power operated dusters 1. Knapsack dusters 2. High pressure trolley/ Barrow mounted 3. Tractor mounted/trailed duster Spraying and sprayers: Sprayers are essential tools for the application of pesticides and herbicides in modern agriculture (Aktar et al�, 2009). The general principles involved in the spray fluid may be a solution, an emulsion or a suspension of the toxicant. Its liquid phase is usually water or loss frequently, a light oil. However, water is not a good carrier for pesticide because of the hydrophilic nature of the insect cuticle, insolubility of same and breakdown by hydrolysis of certain other insecticides in water and of its evaporation from small droplets thus making the particles light and hence drift. To obtain an effective control of the pest, the toxicant has to be well distributed and to meet this requirement the spray fluid is broken down to fine droplets.in different types of sprays the droplets vary from 30-400 microns in size. The also varies greatly at different distance from the point of emission from the sprayer. Spraying is classified on the basis of the droplet size of the spray as On the basis of volume of the spray fluid per unit area, spraying is classified as 1. high volume spraying (or) full cover spraying: here droplet size is larger.

Spray fluid requirement is 500-1000 lit/ha in case of field crops. For orchard crops (1500-2000). 2. low volume spraying: the low volume sprays are 8-25 times more concentrated than high volume spraying. Droplet size is 70-150µ.

3. ultralow volume spray: air craft or ultralow volume sprayer with special nozzles require 0.5-5.6 lit/ha with droplet size of 20-70 µ. 4. Aerial spraying: Aerial air craft has been employed for application of agricultural and public health pesticides. It is used for spraying, dusting and application of baits. However, spray formulation is more suitable than dusts because of wind speed should not be more than 5 Kmph.it has to be done at low height and in the early hours of the days to ensure uniform deposition of dust particles.

div.head: Parts of a typical sprayer
div.p: A sprayer consists of important parts are i) Tank: a built -in tank should have been made of a non-corrosive metal or thick polyethylene and its capacity may range from less than one litres as in a pneumatic hand sprayer to 10 to 25 litres in a knapsack sprayer or mist blower and up to 200 litres in large sprayers. ii) Pump: it is necessary for location energy required for atomization of spray fluid.

Types of pumps i) Air pump (pneumatic): The force created by pump acts over spray fluid and the pump does not act directly over spray fluid. They do not pump liquid directly. This type of air pump is commonly used in compression sprayers. ii) Positive displacement pump: This type of pump is used in plunger, rotary and centrifugal pump sprayers and in this, the pump takes a definite volume of liquid from inlet and transfer it without any escape to outlet. iii) Agitator: To disperse the pesticide uniformly specially in dusters and the spraying where wettable powder is used for spraying. if the spray fluid is a solution of the toxicant it does not settle and hence does not require to be agitated. iv) Filters: Filters are provided between pump and spray lance and within lance. The purpose is to protect the pump from abraxion, and to protect blocking of nozzles. v) Valves: which control the direction of flow of spray fluid. vi) Hose: for conduction of the spray fluid from the sprayer to the lance and, in the case of sprayer with no built -in tank, from the container to the sprayer, hose pipe is used. It is attached to sprayer one and end spray lance and within the lance. Plastic and nylon hoses are much in use because they are light and cheap.

vii) Spray lance: the nozzle of a sprayer is usually attached to spray lance which has 120 0 C bend to be useful for spraying under surface of leaf. viii) Spray cut off -devices: To shut off the flow of liquid usually there is a device in the lance.it may be a stop -cock or a trigger mechanism with provision for adjusting the release of fluid either as a cone or as a jet. ix) Spray boom: spray burn carrying more than one nozzle is known as spray booms. x) Nozzle: Nozzle is an important component in a sprayer.it breaks the liquid into droplets and spray outside on the surface. Nozzles performs four types of function (Bayat and Yarpuz-Bozdogan 2005).
• Atomizes liquid into droplet.
• liquid into droplet.
• Disperses the droplets in a specific pattern.
• Meters liquid at a certain flow rate.

• Provides hydraulic momentum. Selecting a spray nozzle • The Correct selection and use of spray nozzle is the most important part of pesticide application. • The nozzle determines the amount of spray that is generated over a given area, the uniformity of the spray produced, the coverage obtained and the amount of drift that occurs. • Nozzle tips are usually available in brass, stainless steel, and engineering plastic. Steel tips are most resistant to corrosion and abrasion. • Hollow cone/ hydraulic nozzles: is used for spraying insecticides and fungicides to field crops where complete coverage of the leaf surface is important. At pressures of 40 -8 psi hollow cone nozzles give excellent spray coverage to the undersides of reduces penetration. • Flat fan nozzles or fish tail nozzles: used for spraying herbicides. the best operation pressure for flat fan nozzle is 15 -30 psi.

• Flood jet nozzles: Generally, the spray generated by the flood jet is not as uniform as the flat fan type. These are ideal for high application rates and speeds, because they produce a wide -angle, flat fan pattern. These nozzles are also called as deflector nozzle or impact nozzle type. Nozzles are used for herbicide spraying and low pressure 15-25 psi.

div.head: Components of a nozzle
div.p: • Nozzle body: it is the main part which enclose all other components of a nozzle. • Swirl plate: it is metal disc with two tangential holes which imparts a swirl or rotation to the liquid passing through it. 2. Knapsack sprayers: they are adopted for spraying large quantities of liquids, otherwise they are similar to pneumatic hand sprayers in the working principle.A typical pneumatic knapsack sprayer comprises a tank for holding the sprays liquid as 'well as compressed air, a vertical air pump with a handle, filling hole with a strainer, spray lance with nozzle and release and shut off device (Sinha et al�, 2018).

ii) Manually operated mist blower: The common air atomizer, often known as a flit gun, is a compact, convenient device. It works on the principle of air blast breaking up the spray droplets and carrying them to the target. It consists of a simple compression cylinder made of tin, with an air pump. These appliances are useful for spot treatment in household spraying against bedbugs, mosquitos and house flies and for treating individual plants.

iii) Manually operated hydraulic sprayers 1. Hand syringe or garden syringe: it is a single acting pump working on the principle of a bicycle pump. it consists of a cylinder and a plunger or piston. spray fluid has to be contained in a separate tank. The spray is made of large droplets and is just like drenching. It is useful for small scale spraying in kitchen garden and pot plants. 2. Bucket sprayer or stirrup pump: The bucket sprayer is designed to pump the spray fluid directly from, the open container, usually a bucket.

The hydraulic pump will be put inside the bucked and held properly with the help of foot rest. As the plunger is pulled up, the fluid enters through the suction ball valve assembly and when the plunger is pressed down, the suction valve closes and the fluid enters the pressure chamber through a ball valve assembly. As the plunger is continuously worked, pressure is built in the pressure chamber and the delivery. A pressure of 4 kg / cm2 is developed in most of the models. 3. Knapsack sprayers: the sprayer is used for spraying low crops, vegetable, nursery, shrubs and trees up to 2.5 m high. The capacity of the contains is 15 litres. The pump has to be operated continuously for maintenance of necessary pressure (3-5 kg/sq. cm). 4. Rocker sprayer: rocking sprayer has a pump assembly, fixed on a wooden platform with an operating lever, a valve assembly with two ball valves, a pressure chamber, suction hose with strainer, and delivery hose with spray lance. The operation is continued till the entire suction pipe, ball valve assembly, delivery hose and a portion of pressure vessel is fitted with spray fluid and the pump operator finds it difficult to push the piston forward, due to the downward pressure developed by the entrapped compressed air in the pressure vessel. Thereafter, the trigger cut off valve will be opened to allow the spray fluid to rush through the nozzle and get atomized. Usually 14 to 18 kg/cm 2 pressure can be built in the pressure chamber and hence can be conveniently used for free spray. 2. Controlled droplet application sprayer (CDA): these sprayers apply the correct size and uniform droplets on a given target area so that optimum use is made of spray volume and dosage. It is a logical extension of the ULV concept. The most promising method of controlling droplet size by varying their rotation speed. Uniform droplet density can be obtained by using as little as 500mL/ha of the formulation with a droplet size of 4µ or 1-8 L/ha droplets. The advantages of CDA are it improves spraying efficiency, eliminated waste and protect environment.

div.head: Maintenance and Cleaning of Pesticide Appliance
