div.p: • Avoid bending of the hoses to prevent the pressure reduction and damage to them (Yenisoy-Karakas S 2006). • Read the instruction manual before using the appliance • Avoid over heating of the engine • Use the recommended fuel-oil mixture for running the engine • Use of recommended lubricant

div.head: For dusters
div.p: • Blow out all the residual dust at the end of the operation.
• Gear box and gear teeth should be greased occasionally.

div.head: For sprayers
div.p: Pump out the residual liquid after each operation and then operate the pump with clean water to rinse it and pump out the water again.

• Lubricate the cup leather washer to keep it soft because a dry and wrinkled washer would not produce any suction force. • Never plunge the piston rashly; this may damage both the cup leather washer and the piston • Machine should be never be blown with mouth nor cleaned near the source of drinking water • Operator should never smoke or eat during operation and cleaning of an appliance. • Cleaning pesticide application equipment is necessary and worthwhile.

The risk from not cleaning your spray equipment is reason enough to take the time to do it right. Even a trace of leftover chemical sprayed on a different crop can cause severe damage. • After each application it is crucial that the sprayer is cleaned to prevent residues from inside the tank contamination the next pesticide used and causing phytotoxicity on the crop.

• Phytotoxicity: the application of pesticides or insecticides on plants is intended to control the pest without causing adverse or harmful effects to plants. It is common to see some adverse insecticides in field which is called phytotoxicity.

There are two types 1. Permanent phytotoxicity: leads to the death of the effected plant part or whole plant. 2. temporary phytotoxicity: which allows the plant to recover after showing phytotoxicity.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Crop protection practice are most important activities during crop production. Application of pesticides has been increasing in the recent past with advances in technology and changes in farming practices. This chapter will be helpful for the users in selection of suitable sprayer for precise spray as per the need of application. This will not only reduce the cost by application of correct quantity of chemical in appropriate manner but also helpful in the environment by reducing the soil, water and air pollution.

Horizontal transmission Horizontal transmission is carried by external agents such as insect vectors, human activities, pruning, agriculture tools, and other direct or external contamination. In this pathway of transmission, viruses are directly or indirectly transmitted among individuals of the same generation. In direct horizontal transmission, plant viruses are transmitted by a direct route such as airborne infection and food-borne infection, whereas in indirect transmission, plant viruses are transmitted through intermediate biological vectors such as aphids, whiteflies, and leafhoppers, which acquire and transmit virus from diseased plant to healthy plants.

div.head: Vertical transmission
div.p: Vertical transmission occurs when a virus's goes in to the progeny from the infected parent's plant either through asexual propagation such as cuttings, grafting, and budding etc. or in sexual reproduction via infected seeds. In this pathway, viruses are passed vertically from mother to offspring via egg, either on the surface of the egg, called "transovum transmission," or within the egg, called "transovarian transmission" (Clayton and Tompkins, 1994;Ewald, 1994).

div.head: Insect transmission
div.p: • 400 spp. have been reported to transmit plant viruses.
• 94% of these belong to Phylum Arthropod in this 90 % are insects • 70% of insects belong to Homoptera in which Aphididae are the most important group.
• Beetles, thrips, mites etc. are also important vectors.

div.head: Insect vector for plant viruses Most plant viruses depend in insect vectors
div.p: for their survival, transmission and spread. Specific virus. Insect vectors interactions have evolved, in a mutualistic symbiosis at the detriment of the plant. The principal families of insect vectors which cause the most damage to agricultural crops through the spread of plant virus are in the order Hemiptera, and include the aphids, leafhoppers, delphacid planthoppers and whiteflies etc.

div.head: Mechanism of Transmission
div.p: 1. Non-persistent or stylet borne transmission: The virus binds in the stylet during feeding and is released when the insect secrets saliva on a new feeding place, mostly aphids. 2. Semi-persistent transmission: Viruses are retained in the foreguts or salivary glands. Such as whitefly. 3. Circulative, non-propagative: The virus circulates up through the food canal and cross the midgut and hindgut reaching the haemocoel. The virus

div.head: Management Destruction of volunteer plants and alternate hosts:
div.p: The volunteer plants and several weed hosts are known to serve as a reservoir for viruses and these plant species become primary sources of infection. Russian thistle is an overwintering host, both for virus, Sugar beet curly top virus, and its vector, sugar beet leafhopper Circulifer tenellus. There are many viruses vectored by aphids, whiteflies and leafhoppers that have reservoir hosts suc has Physalis sp� (Rugose mosaic virus in potato), Hibiscus tetraphyllus (Yellow vein mosaic virus in okra) and Datura spp�/ Cantharanthus sp� respectively. The uprooting of cotton sticks is a practice used to eliminate the sprouting problems of cotton in many areas in the world. In Sudan, there is a regulation pertaining to the destruction of reservoir hosts of Cotton leaf curl virus with a view to eliminate the source of inoculum. There moval of alternate reservoir hosts of Wheat streak mosaic virus is also being practiced to tackle this problem of virus disease in the wheat crop at many places. Peach yellows and Peach rosette viruses are being kept under check with the destruction of alternate hosts. In South India Santhyamanglem, the practice of destruction of diseased trees of sandal wood is being followed in order to reduce the further spread of Spike disease of sandal wood. The practice of destruction of weeds from banana orchards is followed to get rid of Banana bunchy tops virus in South India.
Rouging of diseased plants/ parts of the plant: It is common practice to delay the spread of plant viruses.

To follow this practice, it is advisable to uses lightly higher seed rate at sowing. This practice is common in cotton in North India and it is being followed on a large sale to mitigate the problem of leaf curl disease in cotton.

The rouging of diseased plants in cassava (Cassava mosaic virus), green gram (Yellow mosaic virus), citrus (Citrus tristeza), cardamom (Katte disease) and banana (Bunchytops of papaya virus) is being practiced regularly to mitigate the disease severity. The surgical approach is sometimes helpful in reducing the disease. There moval of bunchy tops of papaya and decapitating of trees prevented the Empoasca papayae vector from spreading the bunchy top virus.
In addition to papaya virus, surgery also proved useful in preventing the Witches broom in Willow.

div.head: Modification of harvesting dates: A specific technique has been developed
div.p: in India that can be emulated in other situations throughout the globe to tackle aphid vectors of plant viruses. In earlier production of virus-free seed of potato, the potato seed production programme in India was mainly confined to the hill regions because of absence of aphid vector population during the period of growth of potato. With scientific progress, the arrival of aphids was analyzed in the plains of the state of Punjab. Now the seed production programme is taken up in plains of Punjab through seed plot technique instead of in the hills. In this technique, the potato seed production is done during the period of absence of aerial vectors by advancing the planting of potato and cutting of halms in relation to the appearance of aphid vectors in Punjab plains. The crop sowing is done in the last week of September and halms are cut before 20th December (when the aphid vector begins to appear). Once the halms are cut, the aphid vector is unable to pick up the virus from the stems. The harvesting can therefore be taken up at the convenience of the farmer. The potato seed produced via this technique is free from almost all viruses. Based on the data of aphid arrival in plains for thirty years, the appearance of aphid vector is expected by 20th of December or later.

Planting time/ planting density: A higher planting density in cotton is being recommended to manage the Cotton leaf curl virus-CLCuV in North, India which is a limiting factor in the successful cultivation of cotton. Also, leaf curl resistant hybrid Fateh has been developed to check this malady in cotton. The closer planting of tomato is quite useful in checking the incidence of TbLCuV transmissible by whiteflies. Similarly, Cucumber mosaic virus-CMV in cucurbits (Aphis gossypii) and Rosette virus in groundnut (Aphis craccivora) transmissible through aphids are managed with dense planting. Tillage-Minimum tillage has been found to be better than conventional tillage in reducing the incidence of Tomato spotted wilt virus as well as population of thrip vector, Frankliniella occidentalis. The thrips were unable to detect the crop due to no tillage and the presence of crop debris of the previous crop.

Intercropping/mixedcrops: To cover the risk of adversity of nature, two or more crops are planted and mixed with the main crop. This practice is quite common in minimizing the incidence of non-persistent plant viruses. The cucumber, being the preferred host of Bemisia tabaci and non-host of Tomato yellow leaf curl virus, is planted about one month earlier so that whitefly should remain confined to cucumber only where the se whiteflies are controlled through the use of pesticides. The mixed crops (3 rows of maize/ sorghum/pearl millet) around/between the main crop (blackgram/greengram) are another important method to keep the main crop free from yellow mosaic virus diseases. Mixed cropping of carrot and onion in England (Uvah and Coaker, 1984) are instrumental in reducing the population of thrips to the tune of 80% and 20%, respectively.

div.head: Use of trap crops
div.p: • The use of yellow sticky traps management of aphid and whitefly.
• The reflection of these mulches is instrumental in the killing of whiteflies due to heating effect.
• Use of light traps.

div.head: Use of fertilizers:
div.p: The fertility is known to influence the population of insect vectors. The reduction in the dose from 402 kg/ha of nitrogen in onion crop is instrumental in reducing the population of thrips (Buckland et al�, 2013). The application of phosphorus (P2) reduces the population of whitefly Bemisia tabaci in cotton as the leaves become thickened and adult whiteflies do not prefer such a heavily fertilized crop (Butter et al�, 1992b). Chemical: Chemicals are applied in order to manage the colonizing vectors in a crop, but rarely the invading population of immigrant vectors from outside the primary crop. The goal of managing the colonizing population of vectors is achieved and the desired control is obtained. Despite the availability of different chemicals, insecticides are not a priority tactic in managing nonpersistent plant viruses. The non-persistent and semi-persistent viruses require a short inoculation period. Under these circumstances, the use of chemicals increases the incidence of viruses. The pesticide application enhances movement of vectors and increases the intensity of probing which in turn increases the incidence of stylet-borne viruses. These chemicals also kill the natural enemies of vectors. As a result, the vector population increases beyond the limit and causes extensive damage due to the large population, by way of the spread of viruses. However, the use of insecticides proved better against circulative/propagative viruses. The chemicals used to manage vectors include chemicals of plant origin, repellents, use of baits, mineral oils, behavior manipulating chemicals, antifeedants and insecticides. Of these groups of pesticides, repellents and baits appear to be more promising. However, these have not been exploited to the desired level. Furthermore, insecticide use in combination with other tactics can be still more fruitful.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: The plant pathogens are dependent of various agencies for their spread and survival. Among the various plant pathogen transmitting through the vectors, insect vectors are prominent ones. ETL level can't be decided in the insect vector. One individual vector can be transfers many pathogens in plant.The management of insect vectors with the use of various tactics should be used to reduce the severity and to delay appearance of disease.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: In agricultural and horticultural ecosystems greater extent of losses caused by pests includes insect-pests, plant diseases, and weeds and have directly affected the production. Out of these, a group of animals other than the insects also causes the considerable yield losses to agricultural and horticultural crops and commonly known as vertebrate pests such as birds, rodents and mammals.

In recent times mammals such as blue bull (Nilgai), wild boars, rodents, porcupine and monkeys has gaining the status of pests and in certain cases a huge damage is being encountered (Rao, et al�, 2015). Among mammals, blue bull and wild boar both has become a serious problem in most of the Indian states for farmers in major agricultural and horticultural crops and resulting into immeasurable damage. Of the both, the wild boar (Sus scrofa L.) also known as the "wild swine" is one of the utmost broadly spread ungulates and large mammals' pest in the biosphere owing to its great multiplicative speed, adaptableness, and resourceful nourishing (Ballari and Barrios, 2014) and has turned out to be a consistent threat to agriculturalists in case of most important plants causing into massive losses (Muhammad Sarwar, 2019). Destruction of natural habitats by escalating rate of deforestation can be basic reason to abrupt raise in wild boar populations. Deforestation also resulted in the decline the population of natural predators such as Tigers, Panthers, Wild dogs, Wolf, and Jackal etc. thereby indirectly contributing to the phenomenal raise in the wild boar populations. (Khokhar and Rizvi, 1998). Over exploitation of forest resources by the mankind forced wild boars out of their natural habitat and compelled them to depend on cultivated agricultural crops like Rice, Maize, Sorghum, Pulses, Oil seeds and horticultural crops (Fruits and Vegetables).
The damage caused by wild boar is more alarming than their actual feeding in the crop and more apparent in agricultural fields with close proximity adjoining forests areas (Rao et al�, 2015).

To develop an effective management strategy for the management of wild boar, the basic needs to know about its origin, distribution, habitat, feeding behaviour and reproduction patterns. The wild boar Sus scrofa is a social species with a strong post weaning association between mothers and daughters lasting several years with some fluctuations throughout the year (Kaminski et al�, 2005). They inhabit a diverse array of habitats from boreal taigas to deserts. The wild boar even occupies alpine zones in mountainous regions, that occurs up to 1,900 m (6,200 ft) in the Carpathians, 2,600 m (8,500 ft) in the Caucasus and up to 3,600-4,000 m (11,800-13,100 ft) in the mountains of Central Asia and Kazakhstan (Heptner et al�, 1988). The wild boars require a vast range of habitats with heavily brushed areas providing shelter from predators, water for drinking and bathing purposes and irregular snowfall. Being widely distributed in India, wild boars prefer grasslands, scanty bush jungles, forests and agricultural ecosystems. They generally move in groups and the group size varies between 15-35 and home range between males and female. The wild boar is omnivorous in nature, whose diversity in choice of food is comparable to that of humans. They feed on Rhizomes, roots, tubers and bulbs, nuts, berries and seeds, leaves, bark, twigs and shoots, along with garbage.

div.head: Scientific or
div.p: Beside it they also feed on earthworms, insects, mollusks, fish, rodents, insectivores, bird eggs, lizards, snakes, frogs and carrion in warm periods.

Wild boar causes a lot of damages to almost all the agricultural field crops throughout the world. About 400 plants species have been recorded as the wild boar's diet, of these, 40 species are crop plant. The wild boar injury is further upsetting than their real nourishing in the crop (Chauhan, et al�,1993). Wild boar able to identify the host plants by their smell detection sensory organs and more pronounced feeding has done in dawn and dusk periods. By using the crops for both food and shelter, the wild boar caused serious injury to the crops. In many of the states, the wild boar (S� scrofa) is frequently linked with significant crop losses.

div.head: Reproduction in Wild Boar
div.p: Wild boars are prolific breeders will breed year-round, but births peak in November to January in most of the areas. The reproductive period in wild boar is seasonal and mostly correlated with availability of food and other climatic factors. Prior mating, the males develop their subcutaneous armour in preparation for confronting rivals and travel a long distance for searching a sounder of sows, eating little on the way. The males sexually mature in 15 to 24 months but they mate with female at five years of age, when reached their full size. However, females have mate at 18 months of age. Females have an estrous cycle of 21 days and generally receptive for 2-3 days. The gestation period for first-time breeders, it lasts 114-130 days, while it lasts 133-140 days in older sow and a sow will give birth to anywhere from 5 to 12 little piglets of 600-1,000 grams body weight in March and May (Heptner et al�,1988). She can have 2 litters a year and mostly seen after the rains. The newly borne piglets fades three months after their birth, with adult colouration being attained at eight months. The lactation period of new is about 2.5-3.5 months, the piglets begin displaying adult feeding behaviour after 2-3 weeks. The permanent dentition is completed in 1-2 years. The male wild boars leave their sounder after 8-15 months, while females either remain with their mothers or establish new territories nearby. Wild boars are potentially dangerous because of their size and their tusk but they don't attack unless provoked. The maximum lifespan of boar in the wild is 10-14 years with captivity have lived for 20 years (Baskin and Danell, 2003). Crop Raiding Behaviour of Wild Boar Wild boar may destroy to agricultural and horticultural crops by crushing or eating of plants. They harm to crops by injury to plant roots, and form holes and grooves that harm farm equipment and endanger operations. Wild boar injury is much higher in the fields, that are in neighboring vicinity of forests ranges. They harshly influence trees and timber resources in many ways like disturbance of planted seedlings, girdling and rubbing of matured trees, chewing and rooting of sideway roots of fully-fledged trees, and tusking or scent marking with the tusk glands that injuries to tree bark (Sarwar, 2019). According to Chauhan, 2011; about 927 human casualties were recorded during 1990-2010 in 11 states by wild pigs with 4.2% death and 95.8% injury cases. Among the causality's cases maximum (81.2%) were recorded as male followed by 18.8% in female causalities. Among all the observed cases, the maximum cases were occurred under forest areas (77.9%) followed by 18.3% cases under cropped fields and 3.8% cases in the vicinity of village.

div.head: Integrated Management of Wild Boar
div.p: Wild boar can be managed through small-scale exclusion, trapping, and/or shooting. While recreational hunting is often a preferred method, it is not effective at controlling wild boar population growth. In Indian conditions, wild boars have been listed in Schedule III of Wild Life Protection Act 1972, hence killing methods are not applicable in its management because of any method resulting into death of the animal is directly punishable. Under such situation use of strategic and logical integrated management practices are effectively minizine the crop injury caused by wild boar and also farmer -animal convict in agricultural ecosystem.

div.head: Physical barrier
div.p: This particular character is exploited for developing certain types of physical barriers to save the crops from this noxious animal.

div.head: Wire fencing
div.p: • Fencing the field with 3 rows (1 st row fitted 12 inch above the ground) of barbed wire forming a physical barrier to many of the stray animals as well wild boars entering into the cropped area. • Fenced the field with barbed wire on farm periphery with installing cement poles at 10' (3.05 m) interval and tie 7 parallel rows of barbed wire one above other and 2 rows diagonally crossing each other at center between two adjoining poles to restrict boar (Sus scrofa) entering into crop field.

The parallel rows of barbed wires, starting from 6" (15.24 cm) above ground, lower 4 rows 8" (20.32 cm) apart and upper 3 rows 12" (30.48 cm) apart. • Fencing of field by fixing Razor blades with iron wire at regular interval around the cropped area as border by forming circular rings may cause serious injury to wild boar, when they try to enter the field.
• Chain link meshes of 3 feet height can be fixed around the crop by maintaining a distance of 1ft away from the cropped area.

div.head: Plastic fencing
div.p: Polypropylene fencing is a less expensive and easier to install and repair than the other type of fencings and it is widely acceptable and meet various regulations. It is a short-term (up to 10 years) fencing method and comparatively less effective in wild animals' endemic areas.

div.head: Solar fencing
div.p: Solar fencing is a modernized and unconventional fencing method and it is one of the most effective and efficient options of providing security. It is not only doing the guarantee property protection, but also uses renewable of solar energy for its functioning. The solar fencing works like an electric fence with 12-volt (DC) electricity, which delivers a brief yet fierce shock when any animal including wild boar or human come in contact with the fence. The shock of solar fencing is provide a deterrent effect, that ensuring to no loss of the life.

div.head: Electric fences
div.p: The electric fencing is acting as a barrier against the wild animals, cattle and human to entering in the fenced area. It generates an electric shock to deter any animals or people from crossing a boundary. The voltage of the shock may have effects ranging from discomfort up to the death. The electric fencing is also useful for controlling the movements of wild boar from raiding crops. This is a long-term fencing and very effective against the wild animals to protection protect the crops. The costs of electric fencing is variable and depending on specific type and size of an area.

div.head: Trenching
div.p: Wild Boar starts work with hand trenching and pitting in the field. If the farmers can make the trenches of (depth of 8ft with bottom width 3.25ft and 7ft upper width) around the cropped area then the wild boar will fall into the trench, when they try to cross the trench and the forest department personnel will easily rescue it (Kumaran, 2015).

div.head: Bio fencing
div.p: Bio-fencings is the lines of trees or shrubs planted around the field boundaries that provide protection to crop against wild animals or cattle. It also acts as windbreaks, enrich the soil, bee forage, shading to crop and control the dust. These are less expensive and more useful than the wooden, barbed wire or stone masonry fencing. It is ecofriendly in nature giving additional income to the farmer through their produce. Various species have been tested to discover their suitability for use as bio-fencing plants ex. thorny species have been widely used.

• Use of four rows of Safflower as border crop around ground nut found to be most promising in preventing the damage by wild boar. At the first instance wild boar will fail to locate the crop or if it is locating, the thorns of safflower cause injury when they trying to enter into the crop specially in groundnut.

• Planting of 4-5 rows of castor crop around the maize field with close spacing helps the protection against wild boar. Because of the wild boars being capable of identifying maize crop only through smell cannot do sowing to the strong odour emitted by the castor successfully masking the odour emitted by the maize crop. Castor can be used as border crop also in pulses and oil seeds.

• Planting of thorny bushes and xerophytes viz., Cacti sp., Euphorbia caducifolia, E� meriifolia, Opuntia elatior, O� dillenii, Ziziphus mauritiana and Agave americana, Caesalpini acriatata on around the crop helps to protect the crop from wild boars due to their thorny nature.

• Planting of Karanda around the crop field planting of karanda (Carrissa carandus) around the crop as bio-fence does prevent effectively the entry of wild boars into the cropped area owing the thorny nature.

• The grey wolf is the ferocious predator of wild boar throughout its range and a single wolf can able to kill upto 50 to 80 wild boars of differing ages group in a single year. Human are the main predator of wild boars. Wild boars also be destructive to farmland and natural ecosystems causing humans to implement removal process. Mostly the young wild boars are targeted by predatory coyotes and bobcats, while juveniles and adults may fall prey to American black bears and cougars like larger predators (Chapman and Trani, 2007); Dholes, leopards and tigers also prey on boars.

div.head: Trappings
div.p: The maximum effectual means for eliminating wild boars is trapping for the reason that it is a constant action needing far a reduced amount of time and work than other approaches, for instance, hunting. A successful boar trapping pivots upon more than a few following important machineries We captured wild boar in big cage traps of 5 x 2 x 2 m by video-observation and manual (electromagnetic) release.

div.head: Using of Dogs
div.p: Wild boars hunting through dogs has grown into a very widespread method of sport hunting. This technique of hunting is controversial, it helps as a prized device while demanding to eliminate boars in areas where extreme gunfire and catching stress have compelled boars to develop further nightly in their activity pattern.

div.head: Local methods
div.p: • Spraying of dung solution of local pigs around the crop by 1ft width at seven days interval confuse wild boars with a false assumption of entering into the territory of local pigs or other animals, there by their movement will be prevented to avoid territorial conflict. • Use of human hair in the movement route of the wild boar gets sucked through nostrils causing severe respiratory irritation and disturbed to loses its track by making distress calls, which will ward off others wild pigs are entering into the cropped area. • Arrangement of colourful used sarees around the crop can make wild boars to falsely assume human presence in the area thereby they avoid to enter into cropped areas. • Burning of dried dung cakes with local pig dung helps in sensitizing wild boar about the un existing presence of local pigs and the wild boars avoid to movement into such areas. • Using the trained dogs on a community basis in endemic areas scared away the approaching wild boars to attacks. This is also effective and sustainable. • Using the fire crackers, sounds through local drums or empty tins, making bonfires and shouting on community basis help to scared away the wild boar's form cropped area.

div.head: Bioacoustics
div.p: The bio-acoustic technique uses only the sounds of predators, distress and alarm calls of target and closely related species of the target animals. The calls are broadcast in a field by suing an electronic platform with sound drives. Wild Boar reppeler is an impressive and state of the art device that uses different type of sounds at regular intervals to keep wild boars away from the cropped area. This device consists of 1 or 2 loudspeakers, an electric control module with cable, battery terminal clips for power supply and a fixation-plate. That produces fixed volume of 110 dB, and 4-5 acres area at 42 dB ambient noise level and upto 19 acres at 37 dB ambient noise level. This device is perfect for the farmers for protecting agricultural area. A newly designed Duet sonic technology allows the Ultra-Duet Pest repeller, that emitting ultrasonic waves by the help of two different sound systems simultaneously and able to deter and keep wild boar and other stray animals away from house, garden and cropped area.

div.head: Chemical methods for wild boar management
div.p: Wild boar management by killing through chemical is easy practice but cannot be practiced due to listing of wild boars in schedule III of Wild Life Protection Act 1972. The following are the some of such chemical methods that are worked out to manage the wild boars truly under the limits of Wild Life Protection Act.

• Arrange the granular insecticides with sand the crop at 60-100cm height and 3 mts by tied in perforated polythene bags helps to prevent the crops from wild boar attack due to slow release of the smell by wind currents. Strong smell of insecticide may also help in the masking of natural smell of the crop, there by confusing the wild boars in locating the crop. • Wild boar using smell of the crop as criteria for identification, hence the spraying of egg solution (20 ml/l of water) either on the border row of the crop or on the wet soil around the crop is capable of successfully making the natural odour of the crop and thereby reducing the wild boar damage. Under haviour crop damage again repeat it at 10 days interval. • Arranged the 3 rows of niwar around the crop after socked in kerosene oil for 2hrs at 1feet row to row distance with the help of wooden poles. The wild boar could not identify the crop due to dominating smell of the kerosene and it is generally effective for 10-15 days. • Arrange the 3 rows of coconut ropes soaked in the mixture of sulphur + pig oil around the crop at 1 feet row to row distance and 10 days interval. It generates the typical smell there by repelling wild boars, thus they do not enter into the cropped area. in between. • Use of poison baits with non-lethal doses of monofloro acetate (0.025%)

or warfarin (0.025%) cause stomach irritation in wild boars after consumption. However, pre-baiting with 100 gms of wheat flour mixed in onion and garlic paste should be done continuously for a about 3-4 days by keeping 10-15 baits on field bunds, which makes the wild boar to feed.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Entomophagy, the consumption of insects, is rooted in human evolutionary history (Fontaneto, 2011). Throughout Africa, Europe, Asia, and Latin America, insects have held a vital position in the timeline of human nutrition. There exist more than 1900 documented species of insects that serve as human diets at globally, with notable groups encompassing grasshoppers (Orthoptera), caterpillars (Lepidoptera), grubs (Coleoptera), wringed termites (Isoptera), some hymenopteran insects, cicadas (Hemiptera), and a diverse array of aquatic insects. It is intriguing to observe that insects are regularly consumed by over two billion people, and in specific regions, they make up a substantial portion of the animal proteins consumed. (Van Huis, 2013). With its wide adoption and favorable comparison to conventional livestock production in terms of nutritional value and environmental impact, the consumption of insects (entomophagy) has the potential to play a significant role in addressing under nutrition within the increasing global population (Van Huis et al�, 2013).

div.head: Diversity of edible insect species in the world
div.p: Insects have thrived for a remarkable span of time, with a history that dates back at least 400 million years, positioning them as one of the earliest known land-dwelling creatures. More than 390 million years ago, insects diverged as a part of one of the largest subphyla within the arthropod family, undergoing a rapid evolution and radiation that surpasses the pace of any other group (Gaunt and Miles, 2002). In terms of population size, insects hold the title for being the largest animal group on our planet, comprising up to 80% of the animal kingdom. Due to their nutritional significance and abundant availability, over two billion individuals regularly partake in the consumption of edible insects. (Premalatha, 2011).

Various authors have documented the inclusion of over 1900 species of edible insects in the diets of 300 ethnic groups across 113 countries worldwide (Mac Evilly, 2000) (Table 1). In Africa alone, Van Huis et al. (2013) reported that 246 species of edible insects have been documented in 27 countries.

Ramos-Elorduy conducted a study two years later, which highlighted Africa as a significant hotspot for the biodiversity of edible insects. The study recorded 524 species from 34 African countries (Ramos-Elorduy, 2005). The majority of these species belong to the orders Orthoptera, Lepidoptera, Coleoptera, Hymenoptera, and Isoptera. Figure 1 provides a checklist of the most commonly consumed edible insect species worldwide, along with their corresponding orders and locations.

div.head: Why eat insects?
div.p: The issue of hunger and malnutrition presents a significant challenge as the global population continues to expand. To ensure food security, it is crucial for the world's food supply to grow at a rate that matches or exceeds population growth. Consequently, there is an ongoing pursuit of new food sources, including the exploration and cultivation of locally specific ethnic food options (Van Huis et al., 2013).

In various regions around the world, particularly in Africa and Latin America, the availability of food resources is becoming increasingly scarce, leading to higher costs of food imports. Therefore, it is crucial to identify and develop local food resources. Researchers have directed their focus towards insects as a potential solution to not only address the challenges posed by rapid population growth but also to other pressing issues. Insects are abundant, possess significant biomass, and offer high-quality protein. Moreover, the longstanding cultural practices in many diverse communities in Africa and Latin America, where insects are consumed in live, roasted, and fried forms, contribute to their appeal as a nutritious protein source that is easily digestible (Conconi, 1974;Wand et al., 2004). Additionally, insects are rich sources of essential nutrients such as fat, vitamins, and minerals, particularly iron and zinc (Oliveira et al., 1976). The multifaceted benefits of insects as food further strengthen their selection as a viable option.

div.head: Edible insects as food for humans and feed for animals
div.p: It is estimated that around 80% of all species are accounted for by insects, which are recognized as the most abundant and diverse multicellular organisms on our planet (Scaraffia et al., 2012). They play a vital role in pollinating numerous crops, thereby contributing to their growth and reproduction. Moreover, insects provide various agricultural and human health benefits beyond their pollination services (Dzerefos et al., 2013). There are more than 1900 known species of insects that are incorporated into human diets, with over 2 billion people regularly consuming them (De Foliart, 1989). In certain regions, insect consumption contributes significantly to the intake of animal protein (De Foliart, 1989). Insects continue to hold significant importance as a preferred and vital food source, providing essential protein, fat, minerals, and vitamins, particularly in developing countries and among diverse cultures worldwide (Durst et al., 2010). Comparable nutritional value to meat and fish has been discovered in certain edible insects, while others exhibit even higher proportions of protein, fat, and energy (De Foliart, 1992)

div.head: Importance of edible insects as medicine
div.p: The scientific validation of traditional knowledge in bioprospecting has brought greater recognition and importance to edible insects. These insects have long been significant dietary components and remedies for illnesses in various regions worldwide (Srivastava and Gupta, 2009). Traditional healers have successfully utilized insects as medicinal treatments for a range of diseases in both humans and animals. These include conditions such as fever, scabies, epilepsy, severe headaches, bronchitis, hemorrhage, and even dog bites. Insects are also employed in wound healing, gangrene prevention, and stimulating milk production in lactating women, among other applications (Tango, 1981). Modern hospitals are increasingly adopting these traditional insect-based treatments. Furthermore, researchers have exploited the defensive chemicals produced by edible insects to develop antibacterial and anticancer drugs. As an illustration, pierisin, a protein extracted from the pupa of the cabbage butterfly, demonstrates cytotoxic effects on human gastric cancer.

Likewise, cecropin has exhibited cytotoxic properties against lymphoma and leukemia cells in mammals (Srivastava and Gupta, 2009). Despite their high nutritional, economic, and medicinal value, edible insects are often overlooked.
The acknowledgment and utilization of the benefits these creatures offer to the environment and human health are essential for scientists.

div.head: Market value of edible insects
div.p: Rural communities of Asia, Africa and South America, there are a deep understanding of the nutritional value and palatability of consuming insects, making them highly sought after. Insects such as crickets, grasshoppers, and locusts are regarded as seasonal delicacies, while giant water beetles find their way into salads, showcasing their culinary appeal (Srivastava and Gupta, 2009). These communities recognize that insects not only provide a valuable source of protein but also supply essential minerals and vitamins, making them a valuable dietary component and enjoyable snacks.

The immense popularity of edible insects has resulted in numerous species becoming highly sought-after commodities in local markets throughout Africa and tropical and semi-tropical regions worldwide (DeFlorita, 2002). In various African regions, such as Zimbabwe, Nigeria, South Africa, Ivory Coast, and Zambia, many families generate a respectable income by selling insects (Balinga et al., 2004). Collected primarily from bushes and farmland by women and children, these insects are processed before being either consumed or sold in local markets, school areas, and town areas. Furthermore, certain insects undergo processing and are exported to local and international shops and restaurants (Chavunduka, 1975). The Capitalization of edible insects presents substantial economic prospects for numerous households in Africa.

However, challenges such as poorly understood and poorly organized market chains greatly limit the growth of agribusiness in the region. Documenting the market constraints encountered by farmers in their efforts to diversify production within the edible insect industry is essential.

div.head: Edible insects as source of nutrient
div.p: The importance of insects in human nutrition should not be underestimated (Sirithon, 2008). There is ample evidence to affirm that insects serve as an exceptionally nutritious and healthful food source, supplying essential nutrients like fats, carbohydrates, vitamins, proteins, amino acids, fibers, and minerals that are vital for both humans and animals. (Vn Huis A, 2013). However, the nutritional composition of edible insects can vary considerably between and within species, influenced by factors such as the insect's metamorphic stage, habitat, diet, as well as the methods of preparation and processing prior to consumption (Rumpold and Schluter, 2013). While several studies have explored the nutritional composition specific edible insects in countries such as India, the USA, Mexico, and Thailand, there is a scarcity of comprehensive nutritional data on edible insects from around the world (De Foliart, 1992). Insufficient nutritional information on the majority of edible insects can undermine consumer confidence and impede the integration of insect consumption with other food sources. Hence, the objective of this study is to compile comprehensive nutritional data on globally consumed edible insects.

div.head: Dietary energy content of edible insects
div.p: As the global population continues to grow, over 3.7 billion individuals are currently affected by malnutrition, primarily caused by insufficient protein and energy intake (Olaf and Michael, 2005). Additionally, the availability of new agricultural land is limited, making it challenging to produce an adequate food supply for the growing population. Consequently, a greater number of people are relying on animal protein, which requires substantial resources to produce. Livestock production is particularly costly due to the high energy input compared to the energy output (Mlcek et al., 2014). Moreover, livestock compete with humans for nutrients and energy resources. The utilization of insects as a protein source has the potential to contribute to insect conservation by promoting habitat protection (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Edible insects are rich in polyunsaturated fat, which serves as a substantial energy source for sustaining life. The energy content of edible insects varies across species and regions, and it has been observed to be notably higher compared to that of livestock and vegetables (Krause and Mahan, 2003).

Therefore, edible insects have the potential to efficiently provide the necessary energy for vital functions and the survival of organisms. According to Myers et al., (2008) A vital role is played by insects as they utilize diverse organic sources in nature, recycle organic waste, and provide nutrients to both farm animals and humans.

div.head: Protein and amino acid composition of edible insects
div.p: Proteins, consisting of amino acids, are essential organic compounds that serve as the building blocks of life. They can be categorized as either essential or nonessential. Protein plays a fundamental role in the activities of all organisms and is involved in the formation of vital substances such as enzymes, hormones, and hemoglobin. Proteins, being the most prevalent biological macromolecules, are found in every cell and across diverse cellular structures. They demonstrate remarkable diversity, encompassing thousands of distinct types that span from small peptides to large polymers with molecular weights reaching the millions. This vast variety can even be found within a single cell. Moreover, proteins exhibit an extensive array of biological functions and serve as the primary outcomes of genetic information pathways. They function as molecular instruments for the expression of genetic information. Hence, ensuring a reliable protein source is of utmost importance to provide energy for both humans and animals.

Insects have the potential to serve as an energy-efficient protein source for humans (Table 2), whether consumed directly or used as food supplements for livestock (De Foliart, 1989). In numerous local communities globally, especially those in the Amazon region, insects contribute approximately 8-70% of their dietary protein intake (Paoletti and Dufour, 2005). Regarding protein content, the order Lepidoptera exhibits the highest level of crude protein, followed by Coleoptera, while the order Hymenoptera contains the lowest protein content.

The significant protein content highlights the nutritional significance of edible insects as a valuable dietary component for both humans and animals, offering a potential alternative to expensive animal protein sources that are often lacking in the diets of rural populations in developing countries (Banjo et al., 2006). Xiaoming et al. (2010) conducted an assessment of protein content in 100 species from various insect orders. The study revealed that the protein content was notably high, ranging from 13% to 77% of the dry matter. Additionally, significant variations in protein content were observed both between and within the different insect orders.

Research has indicated that when compared on a mass basis, edible insects contain a higher protein content than other animal and plant-based foods, such as beef, chicken, fish, soybeans, and maize (Teffo et al., 2007). The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) conducted a comparison of protein content among insects, reptiles, cattle, and fish (Table 3) (FAO, 2004). It was indicated by the findings that higher protein contents were exhibited by the chosen insect species compared to fish and mammals. This significant finding strongly supports the notion that insects are a valuable protein source, highlighting the importance of promoting their consumption. Substantial amounts of amino acids, notably tryptophan and lysine, have been consistently found in edible insects through various studiesSignificant benefits can be offered by supplementing lysine-deficient staple cereals through the incorporation of these insect species into diets. (Elemo et al�, 2011). Additionally, many scientific studies have revealed that the insect species Tenebrio molitor contain a significant quantity of essential and nonessential amino acids compared to beef (Table 4).

div.head: Composition of Fat and carbohydrate in edible insects
div.p: Fats and carbohydrates are crucial nutrients in the human body, serving as the most important energy source, dropping the need for protein consumption, and aiding in detoxification processes (Alamu et al., 2013). A crucial role is played by edible insects as a valuable carbohydrate source as well. Sting bugs and cicadas are containing carbohydrate 6.71% and 15.98% respectively (Raksakantong et al., 2010). Their carbohydrates are primarily derived from chitin, a macromolecular compound that holds high nutritional and health value. Chitin has been shown to effectively reduce serum cholesterol levels (Burton and Zaccone, 2007). Furthermore, recent reports indicate that edible insects contain substantial amounts of polysaccharides, which have the potential to enhance immune functioning in the human body (Sun et al., 2007). Fat, as the most energy-dense macronutrient in food, is composed of triglycerides and three fatty acid molecules forming its backbone. Fatty acids can be categorized as saturated, unsaturated, or essential. Research from dietary interventions and epidemiological studies has emphasized the significant role of fatty acid intake in human health. Lowering the consumption of dietary saturated fatty acids has been associated with a decrease in factor VII coagulant activity, which is considered a risk factor for cardiovascular disease (Lefevre et al., 2004). Scientific literature further indicates that the consumption of polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) offers various health benefits for humans. These benefits include the reduction of glucose tolerance, thereby decreasing the risk of diabetes and blood pressure (Sirtori and Galli, 2002), prevention of insulin resistance (Mori et al., 1997), inhibition of thromboxane A2 formation, thus reducing the tendency for blood clotting (Nieuwenhuys and Hornstra, 1998), & lowering blood pressure (Asaf et al., 2002). Womeni et al. (2009) revealed that content and composition of fat extracted from various insect species (Table 5).

Their findings revealed that the oils extracted from insects are abundant in polyunsaturated fatty acids, often including the essential fatty acids linoleic acid and α-linolenic acid. These two essential fatty acids are widely acknowledged for their nutritional significance, particularly in promoting the healthy development of children and infants (Michaelsen et al., 2009).

div.head: Mineral composition of edible insects
div.p: Minerals have significant roles in the metabolic and physiological functions of living organisms. Certain minerals such as iron, zinc, copper, and manganese serve as antioxidants and enzyme cofactors, thereby strengthening the immune system (Talwar et al., 1989). Similarly, minerals like magnesium, zinc, and selenium help prevent conditions such as cardiomyopathy, muscle degeneration, growth retardation, impaired spermatogenesis, immunological dysfunction, and bleeding disorders (Chaturvedi et al., 2004). Iron deficiency poses a major concern in the diets of women, especially pregnant women, particularly in developing regions, particularly in Africa (Orr, 1986). Magnesium, on the other hand, is involved in over 300 biochemical reactions within the body. It aids in maintaining normal muscle and nerve function, regulates the heart rhythm, and helps regulate blood sugar levels (Saris et al., 2000). The analysis of elemental composition in edible insects has revealed their richness in essential nutrients such as potassium and sodium (cricket nymph), calcium (adult cricket), copper (mealworm), iron (axayacatl), zinc (cricket), manganese (cricket), and phosphorus. This indicates that edible insects have the potential to provide the necessary nutritional elements required for optimal human body functions. By including edible insects in the diet along with other nutrient-rich foods and animals, the overall nutritional profile can be enhanced. This approach ensures a diverse intake of essential minerals. Table 6 presents the mineral content of specific edible insects found in Nigeria.

div.head: Vitamin composition present in edible insects
div.p: Vitamins, essential organic compounds, have a crucial role in human metabolism. Unlike other substances, the human body cannot synthesize vitamins internally, making it necessary to consistently obtain them from dietary sources (Alamu, 2013). Ascorbic acid, also known as vitamin C, functions as an antioxidant and reducing agent, while components of coenzymes make up the vitamin B group. Vitamin K is necessary for normal blood clotting, and vitamin A is crucial for maintaining proper vision. Numerous studies have demonstrated that edible insects contain significant amounts of vitamins (Ekpo, 2005). Edible insects, with their significant vitamin content, show great promise as food supplements for malnourished individuals and animals. Table 7 presents the vitamin contents of various insect orders, all of which contain noteworthy levels of vitamins A, B 2 , and C. Opportunities for insect rearing in the environment (ecological opportunities)

Insects provide a multitude of ecological services that are vital for the survival of humanity. The utilization of insects as a protein source can contribute to insect conservation through habitat protection (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Insects play a crucial role as they feed on organic matter in nature, efficiently utilizing all available organic sources. They also play a significant role in recycling organic waste and providing nutrients for farm animals (Myers et al., 2008, Diener et al., 2009). Many insect species are essential for improving soil fertility. They aid in the breakdown of waste products, making them suitable for consumption by fungi and bacteria. As a result of this decomposition process, minerals and nutrients are released, making them readily available in the soil for plants to absorb. This, in turn, improves soil fertility. For example, animal carcasses are consumed by fly maggots and beetle larvae, and the decomposition of manure is significantly contributed to by dung beetles, with around 400 known species. Consequently, insects can serve as efficient bio-transformers, converting abundant and cost-effective organic waste into animal biomass rich in proteins, suitable for animal nutrition (FAO, 2009).

div.head: Beneficial roles of insects in human welfare
div.p: In addition to being sources of food, edible insects offer a wide range of valuable products to humans. Numerous insect species possess significant commercial and pharmaceutical value. For instance, bees are known for producing substantial quantities of honey, while silkworms yield vast amounts of silk, both of which can be sold in local and international markets (FAO, 2009). Silkworms alone produce over 90,000 tons of silk (Youn-woo, 1999). Scale insects of the Hemiptera order produce carmine, a red dye used in coloring foods, textiles, and pharmaceuticals. Potential has been shown in medical applications for repairing arteries using resilin, a rubber-like protein that enables insects to jump, owing to its elastic properties. (Elvin et al., 2005). Moreover, other products derived from edible insects, like honey bees wich produced honey, propolis, royal jelly, and venom, have been used in the treatment of traumatic and infected wounds and burns (Van Huis A., 2003).
Additionally, insect products have found applications in engineering methods for the production of biomaterials (Lewis, 1992).

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Meeting global food demands in a sustainable manner presents one of the greatest challenges for humanity, and it has garnered significant attention in recent years (West et al., 2014). It is widely acknowledged that food security is significantly bolstered by agriculture, which enhances the availability of economical food and uplifts the lives of the underprivileged. Within the framework of a sustainable diet, the integration of insects as a source of food and feed assumes a crucial role in ensuring food security and enhancing the well-being of individuals in Africa. They are rich in protein and amino acids, including essential amino acids that are essential for maintaining human health. Additionally, they offer a valuable source of unsaturated fatty acids, minerals, vitamins, and carbohydrates, providing exceptional nutritional value. Moreover, their significance extends to medical, commercial, and ecological aspects. Given these remarkable attributes, edible insects warrant thorough consideration as a means to address the pressing global challenge of human nutrition.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Bees belonging to the Apis genus can be found in a wide range of climates worldwide. Among them, the Apis mellifera Linnaeus species is distributed across diverse environments such as sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Central Asia. This species exhibits remarkable adaptability, thriving in various habitats like the oases of the African desert, the Alps, the edges of the tundra, and the misty regions of the United Kingdom. Additionally, other honey bee species within the Apis genus are predominantly distributed in Asia, specifically in tropical Southeast Asia.Honeybees are good biological indicators. Honey bees (Apis mellifera L.) are insects of great importance for humanity. Humans have taken advantage from them from ancient times, both directly -through beekeeping− and indirectly -through farming, so their importance goes beyond ecology, being also economic. Honey bees and honey bee health have become a major topic recently due to the important role honey bees play in pollination and food production.

Although it is often suggested that mankind will not survive for long once honeybees are gone, this is rather exaggerating the role of honey bees for human nutrition since primary food production, and especially our staple foods, is independent of animal (insects, birds, bats) pollination. Our staple foods (e.g. wheat, corn, rice, potatoes) are wind-or passively self-pollinated or are vegetatively propagated, meaning that their production does not increase with animal pollinators.90% commercial pollination is performed by managed honey bees (Apis mellifera L.).

• These affected larvae undergo a transformation into a brown hue, undergoing putrefaction and emitting an unpleasant odor reminiscent of fish glue. Over the course of about a month, they desiccate into a firm, adherent scale. • The typical period for an infected larva to display symptoms of the disease is approximately 12.5 days post-hatching, with nearly all afflicted larvae manifesting visible discoloration within a range of 10 to 15 days posthatching. • The caps that cover these diseased larvae rapidly become damp and assume a dark coloration. This causes the caps to depress inward, prompting adult bees to initiate their removal. The process begins with the formation of small openings, eventually culminating in the complete exposure of the cell. • At the stage where the caps have sunken, if a matchstick is inserted into the remains of the larva and then withdrawn, it extracts the brown, partially liquid remnants in a stringy thread-like manner.

div.head: Management
div.p: • Thoroughly sanitize previously used equipment.
• Introduce queens from colonies with tolerance to re-queen the hives.
• Provide colonies with supplementary food during periods without nectar availability. • Position colonies in an appropriate habitat.
• Conduct an early and precise diagnosis of diseases.

• Utilize the shaking technique as an efficient approach to manage American foulbrood (AFB). This method entails relocating adult bees to a hive devoid of drawn combs and eliminating the brood combs within the infected colony. Subsequently, the transferred bees consume the contaminated honey present in their honey stomachs, all the while constructing new combs to replace the ones discarded.

div.head: Chalk brood
div.p: Chalkbrood is an ailment that impacts the offspring of Apis mellifera L. bees. This fungal infection is widespread globally, with a predominant presence in the temperate areas of the northern hemisphere. It is induced by the fungus Ascosphaeraapis. This condition impacts all types of honeybee brood, infecting them when they consume food tainted with spores of the Ascosphaeraapis fungus.

initially documented this ailment, and it has subsequently been detected across the globe. The most commonly noted species is Aspergillus flavus, succeeded by Asp. fumigatus; however, other species like Asp. niger are also capable of inducing honey bee mortality. Aspergillus can infiltrate the host through ingestion of spores, traversing the gut, or via the outer covering, known as the cuticle. Consequently, adults, as well as larvae and pupae, are susceptible to infection.Furthermore, the majority of Aspergillus varieties produce aflatoxins, which have been proposed as the principal cause of demise among honey bees affected by stonebrood.The indications commonly identified within a colony afflicted by stonebrood bear resemblances to chalkbrood symptoms and encompass irregular sealing of the brood cells. Infected brood, often referred to as "mummies," become discernible within the combs. These mummies undergo a hardening process, adopting a resemblance to petite stones, as opposed to the spongy texture characterizing chalkbrood mummies.

div.head: Nosema disease
div.p: Nosema ceranae is a type of microsporidian parasite that has been identified as infecting both the Asian honey bee, Apis cerana, and the European honey bee, Apis mellifera. Among the microsporidian parasites documented within honey bees, only two have been recognized so far, namely Nosema apis Zander, 1909 andNosema ceranae Fries et al�, 1996. Nosema apis was originally identified within the European honey bee (Apis mellifera L) and stands as one of the initial microsporidia to be documented (Zander, 1909). To address this ailment, strategies such as securing sufficient winter supplies, practicing hygienic feeding and watering methods, and routinely disinfecting hives using substances like Formidol and the antibiotic fumagillin have been proposed for disease management.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: The significance of honey bees to humanity is emphasized, both economically and ecologically, especially in their role as pollinators. Honey bees (Apis mellifera) play a crucial role in commercial pollination, contributing to around 90% of the process. The health of honey bees and the control of diseases like European foulbrood, American foulbrood, chalk brood, stone brood, and Nosema disease involve distinct symptom identification and specific management approaches for each ailment. Safeguarding honey bee populations and managing their diseases is not just a matter of ecological conservation; it is essential for our food security, economic stability, and overall environmental health. The survival and well-being of honey bees are intricately linked to our own well-being, making their protection a global priority.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The Greek term "Sericos" is the source of the English word "sericulture". The words "Seri" and "Cos" in this word both refer to culture. Sericulture is the art of rearing the silkworms for the purpose of producing silk. An elastic protein of insect origin, silk is synthesized by the silk gland of the silkworm. Fibrion and sericin, two various types of proteins, constitute the fiber known as silk. Silk is most frequently produced by chins. Following China, India is the second-largest producer of silk. Furthermore, it is the world's largest consumers of silk. The major silk producing state in India is Karnataka. Tasar, Muga, Mulberry, Oak tasar, and Eri silk are all produced in India. One other commercial fabric of animal origin outside wool is silk, which is a gift from nature to humans. Being an eco-friendly, biodegradable, and self-sustaining substance, silk has unique significance in the present day. The promotion of sericulture can have both major financial advantages and an advantageous effect on ecosystem development.

India is the world's fifth-largest producer of silk and practices sericulture, which has been dubbed an employment-oriented industry. All of the industry's segments, including mulberry cultivation, silkworm seed production, silkworm rearing, reeling and weaving of silk, and collection of byproducts and its processing, generate substantial employment and, as consequently, provide a source of income for rural and tribal people. Mulberry sericulture has been a traditional occupation in Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, A.P. and Kashmir; Tasar one, in M.P., Chota Nagpur Division and Orissa; Muga one, in Assam, Nagaland, Tripura; and Eri one, in Assam's and West Bengal. Due to this peculiar nature, the Indian planners have identified sericulture as one of the best-suited occupations for ideal growth and development of rural India.

Sericulture has been strongly promoted in both traditional and nontraditional areas by central and state level government silk departments. With the start of major development activities, it is projected that sericultural activities will begin to pick up speed in the whole country, permitting for the gradually combine of employment opportunities. This may help to alleviate the acute unemployment trouble in rural India and, in turn, to some extent slow down rural-to-urban migration. Sericulture is an agro-based cottage industry that includes interdependent rural, semi-urban, and urban-based activities; thus, in contrast to other agro-based professions, the contribution of women in sericulture industry is dominating, which will be helpful for improving the status of women in family enterprises. In the light of women welfare through Sericulture industry, the Central Silk Board, a statutory organization, carried out a project called "Women Welfare through Sericulture Industry" in the year 2012.

div.head: History
div.p: An ancient Chinese couple named Lot zu and K wang T are credited with developing silk from silkworms. When a silk cocoon fell into the woman's cup of tea while she was seated beneath the tree, the hot beverage helped the long thread of silk unravel. She discovered a little larva when the silk ran out. It dawned on her that silk comes from a caterpillar larva. This spread when she told everyone about it. Around 2850-2750 B.C. in China, sericulture first appeared. By mistake, a Chinese woman named Xi-Ling created the mysterious silkworm cocoon. The Sericulture Project (Source: www.indiaagronet.com). It is known with certainty where silkworms come from. It is generally believed that the origin is in the Chinese Himalayan area. Additionally, there are viewpoints that attribute the origin of the silkworm to the north-eastern Indian Himalaya. C14-Isotope-Test results provide proof.

Sericulture extension was encouraged at the highest governmental level, and sericulture reached out to small farmers and their education. In a short period of time, silk manufacturing has risen throughout about half of the old imperial China. Between the years 200 B.C. and 300 A.D., Chinese immigrants brought the knowledge of silkworm rearing and mulberry farming to Korea and Japan, With the captures, conquests, and population emigration of Persians and Arabs, the empire continue its expansion. Later in the 9th centuries, the Moors introduced sericulture to Sicily and Spain. In the 15th century, sericulture extended from Spain to northern Italy, which was home to the majority of Europe's major producer-and trading centers. The sericulture afterwards spread to England, Germany, Switzerland, and the Netherlands. Breeding efforts in the USA fell behind those in other nations. During this period, there is labor diversity between the manufacture of cocoons and commercial fabric processing that is focused on commerce. As a result, the primary producing nations are still China, India, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan, Kirghistan, and recently also Brazil, whereas the main processing nations are the USA, Italy, and France (Jahnke et al�, 2001).
Moriculture: Moriculture refers to the cultivation of mulberry plants. There are more than 20 species of mulberries, of which four are widely used:

• Morus alba -White mulberry is a medium-sized, deciduous tree that grows to a height of 25 to 35 meters. A heavy, spreading crown that is frequently larger than the tree's height is present. The white mulberry tree can grow in a pyramidal style or tend to sag. It has a straight, cylindrical, buttressfree bole that may be up to 1.8 meters in diameter. The bark is vertically fissured, dark greyish-brown in color, and exudes a latex which can be both white or yellowish. The leaves are very different in form, alternating, petiolate, cordate at the base, and light green in colour. They can be simple or complex (3-5 lobed), dentate, palmately veined, coriaceous, and caducous, even on the same tree. Axillary and pendulous inflorescence.

The blooms are single, inconspicuous, and greenish in color, resembling, White mulberry is chiefly used to rear silkworm for silk production. Its foliage can be used as a source of fodder for livestock. The leaves and stems can be cooked as a vegetable. The fruits are edible and can be eaten raw or dried and used as a raisin substitutes.

• Morus indica -Similar to other members of the mulberry family, Morus indica is sometimes referred to as a tiny tree or a shrub, seldom growing taller than 10-15 meters (33-49 feet). The plant has little, silky hairs called as on its branches while it is young, but as it ages, these hairs fall off. Light gray-brown describes the branches. The leaves are linked to the tree by petioles and range in size from 4 to 12.5 centimeters (1+12 to 5 inches) in length and 2.5 to 7.5 centimeters (1-3 inches) in width.

• Morus Serrata -The Himalayan Mulberry is a large, 20-meter-tall deciduous tree. Up to 2-3 m in circumference, with reddish or grey-brown, scaly bark on older trunks and smooth on younger branches, and delicate shoots velvet-hairy. A 2-5 cm long, velvet-haired leaf stalk carries the individual leaves. The leaf blade is oblong to broadly ovate, 5-15 cm long, 3-10 cm wide, 3(-5) nerved from the heart-shaped base, often 3-lobed, edges coarsely serrated bi saw toothed, tip or apices of lobes taperingwith-a-tail, veins hairy underneath; stipules linear-lance shaped, 1.5-2.5 cm long, membranous. Different trees have male and female catkins. Male catkins are 2.5-5 cm length and hairy. Sepals on male flowers are ellipticoblong and about 1.5 mm long. They are blunt, hairy, and have filaments that are flattened at the base. • Morus latifolia -Morus latifolia is a medium-sized tree and a very big shrub with a somewhat asymmetrical 8 m tall and broad crown. Strongly bent and twisted branches on which the leaves are joined in a zigzag pattern produce the most striking characteristic. The extremely broad, ovate to heart-shaped, glossy green leaves range in size from 15 cm to 22 cm. The borders have a pointed tip, are hairy on both sides, and are not lobed or dissected. In autumn, the leaf becomes yellow before dropping. "Spirata" only bears female flowers, and no fruits have ever been seen. Frost may be hard on saplings. Plant ideally in a bright, warm, and protected location. Mulberry cultivation occurs the most frequently by stem cuttings, root grafts, and seeds. From a mature stem, cuttings which are 22 to 23 cm long, 3 to 4 buds thick and long are produced. These may be planted directly in the soil or first in nurseries before being transplanted. Routine pruning is done after the plants have developed and has two benefits: it promotes growth and encourages the sprouting of new branches. Harvesting of leaves for feeding larva is done in three ways:

• Leaf picking • Branch cutting • Top shoot harvesting Plucking leaves involves plucking each leaf by hand. In the branch-cutting technique, the entire branch and its leaves are chopped and provided to the larvae of the third instar. The tops of the shoots are cut off during top shoot harvesting, and the 4th and 5th instars get them. The agronomic procedures used to cultivate mulberry trees, such as irrigation, fertilizer use, etc., have an impact on the leaf output and quality. Under ideal circumstances, it is thought that each acre of leaves may yield between 20,000 and 25,000 kg annually. In Japan, 600-650 kg of leaves are said to be needed for spring raising and 500-550 kg for fall rearing to produce one box of 20,000 eggs. The amount of leaves needed in India is around 350-400 for 20,000 eggs. The number of generations known as "Crops" in the scientific sense of sericulture, when the two instars reach the height of their development and feeding With respect to the moth's race or strain, the generation varies.
• Univoltine-Only one generation in a year.
• Bivoltine-Two generation in a year.

• Multivoltine-If more than two or more generation in a year. Multivoltine are usually the locally occurring races at same place e�g� "Mysore" race producing greenish -yellow cocoons in Karnataka and "Nistari" race producing deep yellow cocoon in West Bengal.

div.head: Tasar Silkworm
div.p: Tasar silk is generated by the silkworm Antheraea mylitta which mainly thrive on the food plant.
• Sal (Shorea robusta)
• Ber (Zizypus jujuba)
• Asan (Termenalia arjuna)
• Arjun (Termenelia tomentosa)

Tasar silkworms are entirely wild and can only be feed outdoors by being attached to food plants, therefore cultivation of the latter may be avoided. The male are smaller and brick-colored, while the female is larger yellowish color and has an obvious eye spot on its wing. Though less glossy than mulberry silk, it has a unique feel and allure. Along with Maharashtra, West Bengal, and Andhra Pradesh, Tasar silk is mostly produced in the states of Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, and Orissa. Many tribal communities in India rely heavily on their tasar tradition.

div.head: Oak Tasar Silkworm
div.p: The silkworm, Antheraea pernyi J., produces a superior kind of tasar. These originate in south China and are now more thoroughly spread across subtropical and tropical Asia. They consume natural food plants of oak, which are abundant in the sub-Himalayan region of India, which includes the states of Manipur, Himachal Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Assam, Meghalaya, and Jammu & Kashmir, unlike the domestic silk moth, which is completely dependent on human care. The majority of oak tasar produced globally comes from China.

div.head: Eri Silkworms
div.p: The male of Eri silkworm is smaller than the female, and both sexes have wooly white abdomens. Adults are brown in color or have black and green wings with white crescent patterns. Males emerge earlier than females when it comes to adult moths, which happen between morning and midday. The first mating takes place two hours after emergence and continues for until dusk. The males can then be utilized for two to three further matings before being separated. One male is used for mating with the seed crop. These are multivoltine and undergo 5-6 generations every year.

Eri silkworms have a varying longevity of 44 days in the summer and 85 days in the winter. The farmed silkworm Philosamia ricini, which mostly consumes castor plants, produces eri silk. Eri culture is a domestic activity done mostly for the protein-rich pupae that the indigenous people eat as a delicacy. These indigenous people utilize the silk creatively to create chaddars (wraps) for personal usage. This culture is mostly prevalent in Assam and the north-eastern states of India. It may also be found in West Bengal, Orissa, and Bihar.

div.head: Muga Silkworms
div.p: Muga is an Assamese term that means brown or amber. Male moths have dark brown and copper-colored wings and bodies, while females have yellowish to brown wings and bodies. Two pairs of wings have eye spots. In addition to color, the male moth may be identified from the female by its somewhat smaller size, thin abdomen, bushy antennae, and sharply pointed forewing tips. The male moth is actively flying during mating with the female. The semi-domesticated multivoltine silkworm Antheraea assamensis spawns nonfeeding eggs after copulation, which are non-feeding and expire in 7-12 days. Eggs: About 300 eggs are laid at a time by the female mulberry silkworm. The eggs have viscous secretions on them that help them adhere to the leaves and papers. The eggs are initially a creamy golden color, changing to a brown hue as they become older and get closer to hatching. The female moth lays eggs and then perishes since she doesn't consume anything afterward. To enable long-term storage, the eggs are kept in a cold environment. Larvae emerge from the eggs at a temperature of 18 to 25 degrees Celsius after two weeks.

div.head: Larvae
div.p: • The tiny black-colored larvae emerge from the hatched eggs.
• Three to four days after the eggs hatch, the larvae reach their first instar.

• After the first instar, the mouth parts are dark black in color and the larval body is pale black. • The first instar larvae after two to three days; the second instar larvae after three to four days; and the third instar larvae after four to five days. • The larvae's body color changes after the second moult, turning completely creamy white. • The fourth instar larvae appear 3 to 4 days after the third moult and 3 to 4 days after the fourth moult. • Larvae reach full maturity after their fourth moult, at which point their bodies take on a pale yellowish coloration and turn translucent.

div.head: Pupa
div.p: Female pupa
• The female pupa's body is larger than the male pupa's.
• A vertical line can be seen on the ventral side of the eighth abdiminal segment. • The female mulberry moth's ninth abdominal segment is spotless.
• Male and female cocoons weigh different amounts.

div.head: Male pupa
div.p: • Male moths lack a vertical line on the ninth abdominal segment and have a thinner body than female pupae.
• On the ninth abdominal segment of the male moth, there is a tiny speck.

div.head: Adult
div.p: • The mouth parts of both the male and female, which are primarily sucking in nature, are not functioning and neither one can fly. • They have short, plumose-style antennas and are cremish white in color.
• In contrast to the male, the female has a bigger stomach.
• In contrast to the female moth, the male has much larger antennae.

• In order to attract females for mating, male moths are more active and flap their wings quickly. Within 24 hours of mating, the male moth dies, while the female lays a large number of eggs before also passing away. • After then, a new stage of the silkworm life cycle starts. What is silk ? natural protein fiber known as silk, which comes in several forms that may be woven into textiles, is known as the "Queen of Textiles." Fibroin makes up the majority of the protein fiber that makes up silk, which is produced by specific insect larvae to create cocoons.

div.head: Properties A) Physical property
div.p: The triangular cross section and rounded corners of the 5-10µm wide Bombyx mori silk fibers are distinctive. Due to a 59-mer repeat sequence of amino acids with slight changes, the fibroin-heavy chain is primarily made up of beta-sheets. Silk has a natural sheen due to the fibrils' flat surfaces' numerous angles of light reflection. Primary filaments (brain), which are bound together by sericin proteins that serve as glue, are spontaneously extruded from two silkworm glands to form silkworm fibers. In contrast to many synthetic fibers, silk has a delicate, smooth texture that is not slippery. Even though silk is one of the strongest natural fibers, getting it wet can reduce its strength by up to 20%. 11% moisture recovery is a respectable amount. Its elasticity ranges from moderate to poor; even a tiny amount of stretching causes it to remain stretched. If exposed to too much sunshine, it may get weakened. Additionally, especially if kept dirty, insects could assault it. Due to its weak electrical conductivity, silk is prone to static cling. Silk feels cool to the touch because of its high infrared emissivity. Silk should either be washed before use in clothing fabrication or dry cleaned since unwashed silk chiffon may shrink up to 8% as a result of a relaxation of the fiber macro structure. Chiffon might still shrink by up to 4% when dry cleaner. Rarely will moderate steaming with a press cloth be able to undo this shrinkage. Both shrinkage caused by molecular-level deformation and progressive shrinkage are essentially nonexistent.

The molecular structure of silk, both natural and synthetic, is likely what causes it to exhibit piezoelectric capabilities in proteins. The denier, a measurement of fiber linear density, was established using silkworm silk as the reference material. The linear density of silkworm silk is consequently around 1 den, or 1.1 dtex.
Comparison of silk fibers Linear density (dtex) Diameter (μm)
Coeff. variation
Moth: Bombyx mori 1.17 12.9 24.8% Spider: Argiope aurantia 0.14 3.57 14.8%

Clothing Due to its absorbency, silk is a comfortable material to wear in warm weather and when exercising. Due to its limited conductivity, warm air is kept close to the skin in cold conditions. garments items such shirts, ties, blouses, formal dresses, high-fashion garments, lining, lingerie, pajamas, robes, dress suits, sun dresses, and Eastern folk costumes are frequently made from it.
