Silk is a great material for utilitarian clothing since it resists numerous biting insects, including horseflies and mosquitoes that would typically pierce clothing.

Silk is frequently used to make a variety of fabrics, including charmeuse, habutai, chiffon, taffeta, crêpe de chine, dupioni, noil, tussah, and shantung. Furniture-: Many uses in furnishing call for silk because of its alluring sheen and drape. In combination with another fiber, it is used for carpets, bedding, wall hangings, upholstery, wall coverings, and window decorations. Industry-: Silk is used extensively in industry and commerce, including in parachutes, bicycle tires, comforter filling, and sacks for artillery gunpowder. Medicine-: Silk can be used as non-absorbable surgical sutures since the exterior sericin layer has been removed through a particular manufacturing procedure. Due to this procedure, specialty silk undergarments, including those used to treat dermatitis, have lately been developed. Silk has been given new applications and manufacturing methods for everything from disposable cups to medicine delivery devices and holograms.

div.head: Rearing
div.p: Rearing house-: Since humidity and temperature are particularly sensitive environmental factors, the room or structure where the silkworms are raised must fulfill the requirements. Avoid wetness, air stagnation from exposure to direct sunshine, and maintain optimum humidity and ventilation to obtain the optimal raising house.

div.head: Rearing Equipment
div.p: Rearing stands-These are the frame stands that have silkworm raising trays on them. Wood was used to make these.
• Ant wells: Because ants pose a severe threat to silkworms, the rearing stand's leg is kept submerged in water combined with pesticides on a regular basis.

div.head: Pre cocoon processing
div.p: • Brushing: The process of transferring the silkworm to rearing trays is called brushing. Suitable time for brushing is about 10.00 am. Eggs at the blue egg stage are kept in black boxes on the days prior to hatching. • Preparation of feed bed and feeding: After brushing, the bed is prepared by collecting the worms and the mulberry leaves together by using a feather. The bed is spread uniformly using chopsticks. The first feeding is given after two hours of brushing. • Bed Cleaning: Periodical removal of left over leaves and worms' excreta may be undertaken and is referred to as bed cleaning. It is necessary for proper growth and proper hygiene. • Spacing: Provision of adequate space is of great importance for vigorous growth of silkworms. • Moulting: A caterpillar's moult is a very important life event. Feeding ceases when the larva settles in for molting. After the last feed, it is advised to scatter some lime powder to stop early ecdysing larvae from starting to feed and preserve uniform growth throughout. The worms are more prone to muscardien infection during the moulting process, although this might be avoided by dusting them with ceresan lime. • Mounting: Mounting is the process of transferring adult 5th instar larvae to mountages. When a larva reaches full maturity, it loses its color, its size, stops feeding, and begins looking for a spot to attach itself so that it can spin a cocoon and pupate. They are taken by the hand and mounted. • Harvesting of cocoon: Cocoons are manually collected. The cocoons are sorted after harvesting. Inside the cocoon, the larva goes through a transformation process to become a pupa. Pupal skin is brittle and prone to rupture in its early stages. Therefore, early harvest may cause pupa damage, which may harm the silk thread. The developing moth may break threads after a late harvest.

div.head: Post cocoon processing
div.p: • Stifling: Stifling describes the method used to destroy pupae inside the cocoon. It is accomplished by exposing the cocoon to hot water, steam, dry heat, sunlight, or fumigation. • Reeling: The process of removing the threads from killed cocoon is called reeling. The cocoons are cooked first in hot water at 95-97 0 C for 10-15 minutes to soften the adhesion of silk threads among themselves, loosening of the threads to separate freely, and to facilitate the unbinding of silk threads

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Lac, a resinous exudation from female scale insects, has been utilized in India since the Vedic period. Its earliest mention can be traced back to the Atharva Veda, where the insect is referred to as 'Laksha,' and its characteristics and behavior are described. In the renowned Indian epic 'Mahabharata,' there is also a mention of a 'Laksha Griha,' a house made of lac, skillfully constructed by the 'Kauravas' with the help of their architect 'Purocha,' with the malicious intent of setting the 'Pandavas,' their formidable enemies, on fire. The use of lac was well-known to the Greeks and Romans since ancient times. The use of lac was well-known to the Greeks and Romans since ancient times. In Asia, the cultivation of lac insects has a rich history, with indications that it dates back as far as 4000 years in China, where its cultivation coincided with the growth of the silk industry. They hardened resin, produced by small lac insects belonging to a bug family, is used in various applications. To obtain 1 kg of lac resin, approximately 300,000 insects lose their lives. From these lac insects, resin, lac dye, and lac wax are derived. Lac cultivation serves as a significant income source for the livelihoods of forest and sub-forest dwellers residing in regions such as Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, West Bengal, Maharashtra, Odisha, and certain parts of Uttar Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, and the NEH (North-Eastern Hill) region. Over time, the uses of these products have evolved and changed. Promotion of lac and its culture can help in eco-system development as well as reasonably high economic returns. It is a source of livelihood of tribal and poor inhabiting forest and sub-forest areas.

div.head: Lac Insect Taxonomy
div.p: Kerr provided the initial scientific description of the lac insect in 1782, which was published in the Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London (vol. 71,. The Indian lac insect, Kerria lacca (Kerr) (order: Hemiptera, suborder: Homoptera, family: Lacciferidae), possesses piercing and sucking mouthparts through which it extracts plant sap (Colton, 1984). It feeds on over 400 plant species (Ramani et al�, 2008). The commonly chosen host trees for lac cultivation include Palash (Butea monosperma), Ber (Zyziphus mauritiana), Kusum (Schleichera oleosa), and Flemingia semialata. Ramani and Sharma (2010) reported K� lacca on Acacia tortilis and Calliandra surinamensis. According to a survey, lac cultivation contributes approximately 28 percent of the total agricultural income on average (Jaiswal et al�, 2006).

div.head: Distribution
div.p: Lac insects flourish and feed on specific species of tropical trees, leading to its distribution across South-East Asian countries. Currently, lac production takes place in India, Myanmar, Thailand, Malaya, Laos, and the Yuan province of China. Among these, India and Thailand are the primary regions involved in lac production, with India holding a dominant position. Lac cultivation was introduced to Thailand from India.The states of Bihar, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Eastern Maharashtra, and northern Odisha collectively account for over 90% of the Indian lac production. Additionally, there are pockets of lac cultivation found in Andhra Pradesh, Punjab, Rajasthan, Mysore, Gujarat, as well as in the Mirzapur and Sonebhadra districts of Uttar Pradesh.

div.head: Life Cycle:
div.p: The lac insect is a tiny crawling scale insect that pierces its suctorial proboscis into plant tissue to extract juices, grow, and produce resinous lac from its body. This lac ultimately forms a protective covering around the insect in a structure referred to as a "cell," offering defense against predators.

div.head: Life cycle of the lac insect
div.p: • Life spans approximately six months and includes several stages such as egg, nymph instars, pupa, and adult. • The insects reproduce using an ovoviviparous method, where the female lays 200-500 eggs that are already fully developed and ready to hatch upon laying. • Incubation period is a few hours, giving rise to crimson-red first instar nymphs known as "crawlers," which measure about 0.6 x 0.25 mm. • The emergence of these nymphs is referred to as "swarming" and can last for about five weeks. During this period, the nymphs crawl on branches until they find soft, succulent twigs to settle on. • Nymphs congregate in groups of 200-300 insects per square inch, where both male and female nymphs feed on tree sap by inserting their suctorial proboscis into the plant tissue.

• After settling for a day or so, the nymphs begin to secrete resin from glands distributed under their cuticle, except around their mouthparts, breathing spiracles, and anus. • This resin is semi-solid and hardens upon exposure to air, forming a protective cover around the nymphs. Inside these cells, the nymphs molt three times before proceeding to the adult stage. • After the initial molt, both male and female nymphs undergo degeneration, losing their appendages and eyes. While still inside their cells, the nymphs shed their exoskeleton twice more, reaching maturity as adults. Both male and female larvae reach sexual maturity in approximately eight weeks.

div.head: Adult Lac insect (Male)
div.p: Only the male lac insect undergoes a complete metamorphosis, transforming into another form; it loses its proboscis and develops antennae, legs, and a single pair of wings. The male lac insect is red in color, measures 1.2 to 1.5 mm in length It resides in a brood cell, somewhat slipper-like, equipped with a round trap door (operculum) from which it emerges. The adult male is winged and walks over females to fertilize them.

div.head: Adult Lac insect (Female)
div.p: The female brood cell is larger measuring 4-5 mm in length and globular (pear) in shape, firmly attached to the twig. The female retains her mouthparts but fails to develop any wings, eyes, or appendages. As it develops, it becomes a relatively immobile organism with little resemblance to an insect. Essentially, the females primarily function as egg-producing organisms. As the female lac insect grows, her body size increases to accommodate the growing number of eggs she carries. During this period, lac resin is secreted at an accelerated rate, forming a continuous layer that merges into one cohesive body. After a span of fourteen weeks, the female reduces in size, creating space for both light to pass into the cell and the eggs to reside. Around this time, two yellow spots emerge at the rear end of the cell, which gradually enlarge and turn orange. At this stage, the female has oviposited a substantial quantity of eggs within a space known as the 'Ovisac.' The ovisac appears orange due to the presence of a crimson fluid similar to cochineal, called lac dye, which indicates that the eggs will hatch in about a week. Upon hatching, the larvae emerge, and the entire process begins anew.

Once the life cycle completes and the next generation is about to emerge, the resin-encrusted branches are harvested. These branches are carefully scraped, dried, and processed to create various lac products. To initiate the next cycle a portion of the brood lac from the previous crop is retained for the purpose of inoculating new trees environment for the lac insect's growth and development. Once the host plants are selected, they are cultivated and maintained to ensure a healthy and abundant supply of food for the lac insects. Cultivation of host plant for the lac production in two ways:

1. The plant grows in natural way and the lac-culturist is only protected and care for proper growth of plant. 2. On the other way, a particular piece of land piece of land is taken for purpose and systemic plantation of host plant should be made there. Proper care of plant is required which include • Regular visit to the field • Proper Ploughing and Hoeing • Timely irrigation facilities should be provided • Suitable application of artificial manure • Protecting the plants from cattle and human beings Lac Brood inoculation: In this step, the lac brood, which consists of young lac insects, is introduced to the selected host plants. The brood is usually collected from mature lac colonies and carefully placed on the branches or twigs of the host plants. The young insects then attach themselves to the host plants and start feeding on their sap. This process of introducing the lac brood to the host plants is known as "brood inoculation. Composition of lac with various constituents and their respective percentages as follows:

• Lac resin: 68% • Lac wax: 6% • Lac dye: 1-2% • Other components: 25% Processing of lac: It's occurs after a certain period, typically spanning several months, during which the lac insects complete their life cycle, leading to the formation of the resinous substance known as "lac" around their bodies. This lac is harvested from the host plants either in the form of stick lac or by scraping it from the branches. Subsequently, the collected lac undergoes a series of processing steps to eliminate impurities and unwanted materials. The processing methods may involve procedures such as heating, filtration, and shaping, depending on the intended use of the lac. Through this process, different forms of lac, such as shellac or seedlac, can be obtained. Stick lac: It is also referred to as raw lac or scraped lac, is obtained by carefully removing the encrustation of lac from the twigs of the host plant through scraping. atoideabactrae, Trichogramma brasiliensis, T� pretiosum, and T� chilonis, have been employed for managing lac predators.

• The use of systemic insecticides that are safe for lac nymphs and effective against predators can aid in pest control. Combining these various management components holds the potential to reduce pest populations significantly, ultimately leading to an increase in lac crop yield.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: It is essential for us to embrace and acknowledge the profound beauty and importance of lac culture. By extending our support to the skilled artisans and communities engaged in this craft, we can truly appreciate its significance. Moreover, we must recognize its invaluable contributions to sustainable development, the promotion of cultural diversity, and the preservation of our collective human heritage. By actively promoting and cherishing lac culture, we ensure that this ancient art form continues to serve as an everlasting wellspring of inspiration, creativity, and cultural pride for generations that follow. Together, let us safeguard and celebrate this timeless tradition, ensuring its continued prosperity and legacy in the ever-changing tapestry of human civilization.

(A) Sterilised insect method (SIT) in picture form (B)CRISPR/Cas9 targeting of female reproductive wellness(C)Ovary targeting signals are injected into CRISPR/Cas9 components to provide permanent genome modification in the germline. Source: https://doi.org/10.3390/ agriculture12111896 Recently, a theory on the application of gene editing methods for plant pest management emerged. With the use of CRISPR/Cas9 technology, Zuoet al.(2017) altered Spodoptera exigua, producing a mutant insect with a high level of resistance to the insecticides chlorantraniliprole, cyantraniliprole, and fubendiamide. Helicoverpa armigera was modified using CRISPR/ Cas9 to alter the cadherin gene in order to show how the gene contributes to the development of resistance against Bt toxin. Similar to other significant agricultural pests, the beetroot armyworm (Spodoptera exigua) is generally managed using chemical pesticides. Resistance to the pesticide Spinosad has recently been identified in S. exigua field populations.Zuo et al. (2020) effectively created a homozygous strain (Se6-KO) with a 1760-bp deletion inside Se6 in S� exigua, resulting in a premature termination of Se6 via the CRISPR/Cas9 genome-editing method. They convincingly confirmed the functional involvement of Se6 in spinosyn pesticide resistance and offer an example of genome editing being used to validate an objective premature truncation linked with resistance.Moon et al. (2022) have examined and explored the application of CRISPR-Cas technology for genome editing in for a long-time insect pest management.

figure.head: Fig. 1 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1. Classification of pesticidal formulations (Source: http://apvma.gov.au/node/10901) Solid formulation 1. Granules (G): (Ingredient: Pesticide, carriers, (river sand/marble hips), Coating agent) Formulations that are ready to use are granules. Either the granules' outside is coated with the active component, or it is absorbed by tiny clay, tale, or other carrier particles. The amount of 2. Powders for dry seed treatment (DS): This type of formulation look like a powder for application in the dry state, directly to the seed.

• Nozzle disc: this component breaks the fluid in to fluid into fine droplet. It is a flat disc with an orifice at the centre. When the spray solution reaches the disc from the swirl plate the disc builds up further pressure on the fluid and when the fluid passes out of the orifice it breaks in to fine droplets. • Strainer: a small circular plastic ring with nylon wire mesh to filter any dust particle coming the chemical solution. • Spacer: there are two number of runner/plastic ring placed in between nozzle plate and swirl plate and between swirl plate and strainer for effective travel of the solution (nuyttens et al�, 2007). Types of sprayers: the spraying machines are manually operated or power operated. based on energy employed to atomise and eject the spray fluid (Pankaj et al�, 2018). Sprayers are classified into 4 types • Hydraulic energy sprayer • Gaseous energy sprayer • Centrifugal energy sprayer • Kinetic energy sprayer. In sprayer working with hydraulic pressure, pressure is developed by the direct action on the spray fluid. This pressure force the liquid through the nozzle. i) Manually operated pneumatic sprayers 1. Hand sprayer (kitchen sprayer): the container for the spray fluid also acts as the pressure chamber. An air pump attached to the chamber project inside. The inner end of the discharge pipe runs down to the bottom of the container and its outlet terminates in a nozzle. It is commonly used for spraying kitchen gardens, in glass houses and indoor against household insects.

Foot sprayer: This is modified version of rocker sprayer. The pump is fixed in a vertical position with necessary braces. The plunger moves up and down when operated by the pedal. A ball valve is provided in the plunger assembly itself to allow the fluid to cross the plunger and getting pressurized in the pressure vessel. During the upward motion of the piston fluid is sucked in and pressurized into the pressure vessel and during downward movement, the sucked fluid crosses the plungers and enter the pump. The pressure developed is about 17-21 kg/cm 2 . Power operated sprayers 1. Electrodyne sprayer (electrostatic sprays): the functioning of the electrodyne sprayer is based on a system which atomizes and propels charged droplets of chemical sprays by electrical forces set up between a nozzle with a positive high voltage charge. Since the charged droplets are readily deposited on a plant, the total deposit on the foliage can be bottle and fed by gravity to the nozzle, where it picks up charged droplets (positive charges) generated by batteries. The liquid leaves the nozzle in a number of uniform ligaments which break up into electrically charged droplets. The positively charged droplets move along curved electrical field line towards and around the plants, covering the visible as well as hidden surfaces. The size of the droplets ranges from 20 and 200µ. a special feature of the type of sprayer is the formulation used in it come in a special bottle fitted with particular nozzle required.it has nozzle (prepacked pesticide ＋ inbuilt nozzle). the insecticide used is cypermethrin.

Rearing trays: Rearing trays are made of wood and occasionally plastic, and their typical size is 150 x 90 cm. • Paraffin paper: To maintain humidity in the rearing trays, thick craft paper sheets coated in paraffin wax are needed. • Foam rubber strip: To maintain humidity, pieces of 2.5x2.5cm foam rubber that have been soaked in water are placed all around the silkworm raising beds. • Chopsticks: Chopsticks are bamboo rods with a tapered end that are used to pick up tender leaf stages while maintaining hygienic handling and avoiding injury. • Feathers: Feathers are used to prevent harm to newly hatched worms by brushing them together. • Leaf chamber: This chamber is used to store mulberry leaves intended for animal feed. The chamber is completely wrapped in gunny sacks cloths that are maintained wet throughout the summer and dry days. • Chopping board, knife, and matting: Since mulberry leaves are providedto the worms in a chopped form in the early stages of the worms, a wood chopping board is utilized to easily collect the chopped leaves. • Clearing net: These nets, which are made of cotton and nylon with mesh sizes appropriate for the various instars, are used to change the rearing beds so that any leftover leaf fragments and trash are filtered out without disturbing the animals.

figure.figDesc: The virus passes past epithelial cells and combines with receptors in the insect's stomach. After reaching the hemocoel, infection spreads to critical tissues and organs, especially fat bodies. Within 5 to 14 days, severe infections cause the host to pass away. Insects infected with baculoviruses have a white appearance as a result of a serious infection of the fat body, which causes the integument (exoskeleton) to gradually shrink until it ruptures, releasing billions of occlusion bodies (OBs) that help inocula multiply in the field. Four nuclear polyhedrosis virus isolates from the beetroot armyworm Spodoptera exigua H. (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae) have been the subject of studies. The isolates (SeNPVSP1 and SeNPVSP2) were from the USA, Thailand, and Spain. The modest restriction fragment length polymorphism in the viral genomes indicated related but different genotypes (variants). As a constraint fragment length polymorphism marker, the Bg lII fragment of each isolate was employed. The genome of SeNPVs is 134 kbp in size. The mobility patterns of the occluded virion polypeptide and polyhedrin are very similar between the four SeNPV isolates.
figure.note: The insect's activity slows down, it stops eating, and its growth stops as the first obvious signs. Every EPV family has a different method of pathogenesis and reproduction, but infection is virtually always spread through ingestion. After being incorporated by Staphylococcus aureus V8, the polyhedrin from SeNPVUS was determined to be distinctive. SeNPV showed the lowest LD50 in the second instar S� exigua larvae bioassays, with just 1.5 polyhedra per second instar larva. With cotton pests including S� exigua and P� gossypiella (Lepidoptera: Gelechiidae), as well as the Heliothis or Helicoverpa complex, EPVs have demonstrated efficacy. Commercial baculovirus biopesticides are thought to present a low risk to both people and wildlife because they typically have a limited host range and are typically confined to the order and family of the host of origin. Only in vivo can baculoviruses be created in large quantities, but they are commercially viable for more advanced hosts like Lepidoptera.

figure.head: Manage plants near fields to control certain pests:
figure.figDesc: One method, known as perimeter trap cropping, works best when plants such as snap beans or cowpeas are cultivated to keep stink bugs and Mexican bean beetles away from soybeans. Plants that are highly appealing to target pests are planted around a cash crop, completely surrounding it without gaps, in perimeter trap farming. Pesticide treatments may be drastically reduced by enticing pests away from the cash crop via perimeter trap cropping. By restricting pesticide application in field edges or eliminating it entirely, we can preserve the predators. Beneficial insects are drawn to certain plants, so if you're attempting to manage a certain pest, pick blooming plants that will attract the suitable beneficial insect(s). The size and form of the blooms determine which insects may reach the pollen and nectar of the flowers. The most beneficial blooms, including parasitic wasps, are little and somewhat open. Plants of the aster, carrot, and buckwheat families are particularly beneficial. Use weeds to attract beneficials: While weeds are sometimes the best blooming plant for attracting beneficials, this practise complicates maintenance. Although some weeds promote insect pests, harbour plant diseases, or compete with cash crops, others provide critical resources to many beneficial insects and contribute to agroecosystem biodiversity. Unquestionably, weeds may stress crops, yet there is strong evidence that farmers can increase beneficial insect populations by modifying weed species and weed control practises. Many farmers are emphasising weed management over weed control as they get a better understanding of the intricate interactions between crops, weeds, pests, and predators. In reaction to insect or mite attacks, crops may either directly or indirectly emit poisonous compounds, relying on other parties for aid. Volatile chemicals attract predators, and plants under assault produce different molecules than plants that are not under attack. Different types of the same plant or sections of the same plant might produce varying amounts and proportions of volatile chemicals. Leaves that are not injured generate and emit volatiles, which increases the signalling capacity of damaged plants. As an example, when a beet armyworm chews on a cotton plant, the plant releases a specific chemical signal blend into the air, which
figure.note: Manage the vegetation in field margins: With proper preparation, you may transform your field margins into predator reservoirs. These areas can be key overwintering grounds for crop pest predators. They can also give pollen, nectar, and other nutrients to predators. Create predator corridors: By seeding varied blooming plants into strips every 165 to 330 feet (50-100 m) throughout the field, you may supply predators with highways of habitat. Beneficiaries can circulate and disperse into field centres by using these corridors.Pick the proper plants: Improve plant pest defences:A healthy plant is the first line of defence against insect pests. Plants that are healthy are better equipped to endure the onslaught of insect pests and can respond by mobilising inbred defence systems. Improve soil and provide the greatest possible growth circumstances, including appropriate (but not excessive) water and nutrients, to boost natural defences. Plants have developed a variety of insect defences, including both continuous and triggered responses. Around the clock, continuous defences are maintained, including poisonous compounds that prevent feeding and barrier construction. Pest assaults cause induced responses, allowing plants to utilise resources more flexibly. Plant defence mechanisms that are both effective and long-lasting combine continuous and triggered responses. attracts female parasitic wasps. However, inappropriate nitrogen levels can alter the chemical signal's ratio, rendering it unnoticeable. Plant breeding: while overwhelmingly advantageous in the near term, can have unintended repercussions that derail plant geneticists' best-laid intentions.

figure.figDesc: The majority of insect pathogen formulations that are commercially available are frequently utilised. Biological pesticides derived from Bacillus thuringiensis function by paralysing the gut of insects. The active element in these products is a bacterial protein, and many do not include living bacterial spores, preventing disease transmission. Beneficial nematodes are live predators that are deployed in large numbers to attack their hosts and release symbiotic microorganisms. They feed on bacteria and insect tissue, mate, and reproduce, producing new juvenile nematodes after one to two weeks. Because nematodes are very resistant to desiccation, UV radiation, and harsh temperatures, they are effective against insects in soil or protected habitats. Adequate moisture and temperatures ranging from 53 to 86º Fahrenheit are required for success. Despite the high costs of bulk raising, storage, and shipping, artificial diet research continues. Skunks, who are classified as Grade A nuisances, eat berries, leaves, and grasses but are not drawn to beekeepers because to their thick fur. Owls, who are super-carnivores, assist farmers in reducing rodent infestations and protecting crops. Lacewings, syrphid fly larvae, praying mantids, parasitic wasps, and predatory mites are among the other insects. These insects perform an important role in keeping the environment healthy and pests at bay.

row.cell: Natural predators can benefit from environmentally friendly agricultural
practises by providing habitat, food, and shelter. Predators require a variety

figure.note: ConclusionPredators include birds, amphibians, reptiles, fish, and mammals, among others, that devour insects. Because of their shorter life cycles and limited prey species distribution, they are more typically utilised in biological control. Predators including lady beetles, ground beetles, rove beetles, flower bugs, lacewings, and hover flies survive by catching and consuming other creatures. They are frequently bigger and stronger than their prey and play an important role in pest management in fruit production systems. Farmers and gardeners rely on ladybirds, spiders, and coyotes to manage pests. Ladybirds eat crop-damaging aphids, mites, scales, mealy bugs, thrips, and white flies. Spiders manage bug and crop-eating pests, but coyotes are excellent predators that keep rodent populations in check. of resources, but simple efforts can assist in providing habitat for a variety of species. Invertebrates that prey on humans seek refuge in field edges, buffer strips, and neighbouring semi-natural environments. Hedgerows and rough

figure.figDesc: Biological control offers a long-term, sustainable solution for weed management, reducing the reliance on chemical pesticides and their potential negative impacts on the environment and human health Biodiversity Conservation: The use of predatory insects for weed control can contribute to the conservation of natural biodiversity by promoting the presence of beneficial insect species in agricultural landscapes. Resistance Management: Unlike chemical pesticides, which pests can develop resistance to over time, biological control agents can adapt to changing conditions, making them a valuable tool for managing resistant weed populations. Integration with Other Methods: Biological control can be integrated with other pest management practices, such as cultural and mechanical control methods, to create a comprehensive and effective weed management strategy.
figure.note: Cost-Effectiveness: Once established, natural predators can reproduce and persist, reducing the need for repeated applications, potentially making it a cost-effective approach in the long run. Public Perception: Biological control is generally viewed more favorably by the public, as it aligns with the principles of sustainable agriculture and reduced chemical use.

figure.figDesc: Resistance to Predators: Some weed species can evolve defenses against predatory insects, reducing the efficacy of biological control over time. Public Awareness and Education: Farmers and other stakeholders may require education and training to adopt biological control methods effectively. Risk of Displacement: If not managed carefully, introduced predators might displace native species, affecting local ecosystems.

Except Antarctica and many oceanic islands this species occurs purely wild or narrowly modified feral form on all continents. It is found throughout the plain and broad-leaved forest regions of the Palaearctic from western Europe to the east while soviet far East, extending southwards as far North Africa. The Mediterranean basin and middle east, through India, Indo-China, Japan, Taiwan and Greater Sudan Islands of South East Asia. It is common in the whole of India except Jammu and Kashmir, upper Himalayas and desert, Sri Lanka, Burma and Tenasserim, Syria and part of the Malya Peninsula.

figure.figDesc: . The demand for alternative protein sources has gained considerable importance due to fast urbanization in developing nations and shifting global food preferences. Particular significance is held by Coleopterans, Hymenopterans, Isopterans, Lepidopterans, Odonata, and Orthopterans among the various insect orders consumed worldwide, and they are highly regarded.(Fasoranti and Ajiboye, 1993) (Figure2). Notable examples within these orders include locusts, termites, worms, grasshoppers, caterpillars, palm weevils, and beetle grubs, among others. Despite being commonly regarded as pests that adversely affect humans, plants, and animal well-being, insects play a crucial role in mitigating food insecurity and providing ecosystem services, including pollination, waste degradation, and biological control. Additionally, insects play a crucial role as a primary food source for a wide variety of animal species. According toVan Huis et al. (2013) food and feed security are upheld by importance of insects.

div.head: History
div.p: • The fossil evidence also indicates the presence of plant diseases 250 million years ago. • The diseases caused by fungi and nematodes were discovered by Monocern in 1735. • Johan Needham (1743) identified wheat gall caused by a nematode. • Adolf Mayer (1886), demonstrated the transmission of Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV). • EF Smith (1890) described bacterial wilt in cucurbits.

• The thrips (Thysanoptera) as vectors of plant viruses were identified the scientific studies made by Samuel and his team. • Karl Maramorosch (1952) demonstrated the multiplication of Aster yellows virus (AYV) in leafhopper vector. • The mite Aceriatulipae has been identified as a vector of Wheat streak mosaic virus by JT Slykhuis in 1955. • The term "viroid" (viruses without protein coat) was coined by TO Diener during 1971. • Single stranded Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) was identified in 1982.

div.head: Mode of transmission the transmission of plant pathogens takes place through two different mechanisms under natural conditions (vertical transmission and horizontal transmission).
div.p: particles cross accessory salivary glandsand return to the plant via the saliva canal, Some spp. of aphids and leaf hoppers.

div.head: Persistent propagative transmission:
div.p: The virus circulates in the host as described above, but actually infects insect cells and replicates in the vector, mostly leaf hoppers. Honey bees are susceptible to a variety of diseases which are follows:

div.head: European foulbrood (EFB)
div.p: EFB is a bacterial brood disease that affects honey bee colonies, caused by the bacterium Melissococcus plutonius. They affect the brood (larvae and pupae) of honey bee colonies.Here are the typical symptoms of European foulbrood disease in honey bees:

• Infected brood cells appear sunken and may have a color ranging from yellow to brown. The larvae inside the cells may be discolored and have a foul-smelling odor. • The capping of infected brood cells may become perforated due to the decay of the underlying larvae. • Infected brood frames may show a spotty brood pattern with healthy and infected cells intermixed. Management • Regularly inspect the hives for signs of infection. Look for symptoms such as sunken and perforated cappings, discolored larvae, and foul odor coming from the brood cells. • If you find an infected hive, isolate it from other colonies to prevent the spread of the disease. Move it to a separate location or at least several hundred meters away from healthy colonies. • Encourage hygienic behavior in bees.

• Oxytetracycline and tylosin are commonly used as antibiotics for control European foul brood. • Sterilize hive tools and equipment between inspections to prevent the transmission of bacteria between colonies. American foulbrood (AFB) AFB is a highly destructive bacterial infection that primarily targets the brood, which includes both larvae and pupae, within honey bee colonies. American foulbrood is the result of an infection caused by Bacillus larvae, which is a bacterium characterized by its rod-shaped morphology measuring approximately 2.5-5 μm in length and 0.5-0.8 μm in width. This bacterium is capable of movement through peritrichous flagella and exhibits a Grampositive staining pattern. Let's explore the characteristic signs of American foulbrood disease observed in honey bee larvae:

• American foulbrood is a condition that affects larvae, typically leading to their demise after they have completed their cocoon formation and lie sprawled on their backs, oriented with their heads towards the cap of the cell. Although most commonly observed in propupae, some pupae are also subject to fatality.

div.head: Symptoms
div.p: • Colony health issues become evident through the presence of lifeless brood remains, referred to as mummies, located in front of the hive entrance or on the ground below it. • Inspecting the combs reveals perforated cappings, with both mummified broods and empty cells, which points to the diligent removal of deceased brood through the hygienic behavior of worker bees. • Infected larvae typically succumb within the initial 48 hours after capping, during their pupal phase. • Shortly following their demise, they undergo expansion and become enveloped by the fungus's white mycelia. • In the subsequent phase of the disease, certain pupae maintain a pale hue reminiscent of chalk (hence the term "chalkbrood"). Others undergo a transformation from brown to gray and, ultimately, black. This discoloration occurs due to the emergence of fruiting bodies, which can vary in size and color.

div.head: Management
div.p: • As an initial approach to managing the issue, it is advisable to replace aged honeycombs or those displaying signs of A. apis infection in order to eradicate spores from the hive. The introduction of fresh wax into the combs offers the benefit of diminishing spore presence while also stimulating the queen to lay eggs, thereby fostering an increase in the bee population. • A suggested practice involves an annual replacement of one third of the hive's combs, alongside the meticulous cleansing and sanitization of tools like chisels, gloves, and fumigators subsequent to handling affected hives. Hives exhibiting indications of any disease should be managed in a final phase, reducing the potential for healthy colonies to encounter contamination through the beekeeper's equipment. • Numerous natural compounds have been evaluated for their antifungal attributes. Essential oils containing citral, geraniol, and citronellal have exhibited notable inhibitory effects on fungal growth in laboratory conditions. In Canada, field studies have been conducted on a wideranging antimicrobial substance, lysozyme, showing promising outcomes in the context of chalkbrood control within bee colonies.

div.head: Stone Brood
div.p: Stonebrood is an exceptionally uncommon malady affecting honey bee broods, provoked by various fungi belonging to the Aspergillus genus. Massen (1906) significance that are produced by several species of silkworms, which eat a variety of food plants other from mulberry silks.

All of these commercial types of silk are only produced in India. 1. Mulberry silkworm 2. Tasar silkworm 3. Eri silkworm 4. Oak tasar silkworm 5. Muga silkworm Mulberry Silkworm-This species provides the majority of the world's commercial silk production, and Mulberry silk is frequently mentioned when discussing silk in general. Bombyx mori L., a kind of silkworm, consumes the leaves of the mulberry plant over time to produce mulberry silk. Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, West Bengal, Tamil Nadu, and Jammu and Kashmir are the primary mulberry silk producing states in India, producing collectively 92% of the nation's mulberry raw silk.

The wings of an adult moth are weak and incapable of supporting flight. They are roughly 2.5 cm length, creamy white in color, and completely unmarked. Two to three days after emergence are the adult life span. Emergence occurs followed quickly by mating.

India is the rightful owner of this golden-yellow silk, which is also the state's of Assam's pride. The Muga culture is unique to the state of Assam and is ingrained in that state's heritage and culture. Sarees, mekhakas, and chaddars, among other items, are made from the high-quality muga silk.

div.head: Stages and Production
div.p: • The silk moth lays eggs, and after they hatch,
• The larvae eat mulberry leaves.

• The larvae of the silk moth eat mulberry leaves once the eggs have hatched and are ready to hatch. They are 10,000 times heavier than when they were eggs by the time the silkworms are roughly 25 days old. • At this point, they are prepared to spin a silk cocoon.
• Two glands in the head of the silkworm make silk, which is subsequently pushed out of the body through spinneret-shaped apertures. • Upon coming into contact with the air, the silk condensed.

• In around two or three days, the silkworm spins about one mile of filament before entirely enclosing itself in a cocoon. • The amount of usable silk in each cocoon is little due to quality restrictions.

Since 1 kilogram of silk requires the production of 5500 silkworms. • The silk is obtain from the undamaged cocoon by brushing the cocoon to fined the out side end of the filaments. • The silk filaments are then wound on a reel.

div.head: Sericulture opportunities
div.p: Sericulture is a cottage industry that contributes significantly to the creation of jobs and the reduction of poverty. It is one of the most lucrative activities in the rural economy. Indigenous technology is easily accessible and reasonably priced. Returns that are timely and consistent. Handwoven silk is in high demand and is very well-liked. Possibility of developing a huge production unit and organized sector. Silk is weaved into clothing and sarees. The world's largest silk consumer is India.

div.head: Infrastructure of Sericulture in India Union Government Central Silk Board (CSB)
div.p: The Central Agency for the Development of Sericulture and Silk Industry is a part of the Ministry of Textile of the Government of India. The CSB's primary activities include maintaining a four-tiered seed production network, assisting the provincial government in implementing a field development program, promoting silk on a national and worldwide scale, and cultivating and putting policy into practice to promote the structured development of the sector. 300 units and 4000 businesses across the nation carry out CSB's activities, while 800 scientists and 2500 technical employees are assigned to specialist areas across a variety of industry specialties. In order to advance the sericulture and silk industries, CSB invests roughly $60 million USD annually. Provincial Government: All the provincial Government has separate departments dealing with sericulture and silk industry. Their major responsibilities are; implement field development programme, Extension, Marketing, Seed production and incentive investments among the stakeholders. The investment by Governments to the industry is at level of US $ 50 Million per annum. Private Sector: The development of the industry is greatly aided by the involvement of the private sector, which also includes non-governmental organizations. The export, post-yarn, and seed production sectors all exhibit private investment.

div.head: Host Plants
div.p: Lac insects flourish on specific plant species, where they feed on the plant sap, continuously growing and simultaneously secreting lac resin from their bodies. These particular plants are referred to as host plants. Despite being natural pests to these host plants, lac insects hold a unique position as they are not considered harmful. Several reasons account for this special status:
• They produce a valuable product,
• The host plants are not economically significant, and • The damage caused by the insects to the host plants is temporary and can be recovered from.
The mentioned varieties of host plants are recognized as lac host plants, with the following being prevalent in India:
• Butea monosperma (Palas)
• Zizyphus spp� (Ber)
• Schleichera oleosa (Kusum)
• Acacia catechu (Khair)
• Acacia arabica (Babul)
• Acacia auriculiformis (Akashmani)
• Zizyphus xylopyrus (Khatber)
• Shorea talura (commonly known as Sal, grown in Mysore)

• Cajanus cajan (also called Pigeon-pea or Arhar) Among these host plants, Palas, Kusum, Ber, and Khair are of significant importance, while the others have regional significance and are considered less crucial. It is worth noting that the quality of lac directly depends on both the host plant and the strain of lac insects used. Among the different varieties, Kusumi lac stands out as superior in terms of industrial parameters, commanding a higher price in the market. Consequently, the Ber tree is gaining momentum as a potential host for producing Kusumi lac.

div.head: Various Strains of Lac
div.p: In India, the Lac insect is known to have two distinct strains Kusumi and Rangeeni. The names of the crops are derived from the Hindi months in which they are harvested.

div.head: Kusumi Strain
div.p: • The Kusumi strain is cultivated on Kusum or other host plants using Kusumi brood • Kusumi strain, the two crops are as follows:

div.head: Rangeeni Strain
div.p: • The Rangeeni strain thrives on host plants other than Kusum.
• Rangeeni strain yields two crops.

• The lac from the Rangeeni crops is harvested while it is still immature. Among the Rangeeni crops, Aghani and Baisakhi are the main contributors, accounting for about 90% of lac production, while the remaining 10% is contributed by the Kusumi crops. However, the Kusumi crop lac is considered to produce a superior resin due to its lighter color and fetches a better price in the market.

div.head: Rearing of Lac insects
div.p: The cultivation of lac involves three essential steps: Host plant selection and cultivation: The first step is to select suitable host plants for lac cultivation. Lac insects thrive on specific trees like kusum, ber, and palas. These trees are chosen based on their ability to provide a favorable

div.head: Shellac:
div.p: The shellac is the finished product of lac orange to pale yellow in color used across the world. Seed lac is processed into shellac by three methods: Handmade country process, Heat process and Solvent process. It is utilized as a fruit coating for citrus fruits and apples and serves as parting and glazing agents for sweet treats like marzipan, chocolate, etc. Bleached shellac finds applications in various industries:
• Paint Industry: It is used as a primer for plastic parts and plastic films.

• Aluminum Industry: It serves as a primer for aluminum and aluminum foils. • Pharmaceutical Companies: Bleached shellac is utilized as a coating for pills, tablets, and gel caps. • Confectionery: It is employed in the coating of confections, chewing gums, marzipan chocolate, jelly, coffee beans, and more. By diligently following three key steps -host plant selection and cultivation, lac brood inoculation, and meticulous processing of lac -successful lac cultivation is ensured, ultimately resulting in the production of highly valuable lac resin.

div.head: Natural Enemies of Lac Insect and Their Management
div.p: Harmful parasitoids like Aprostocetus (Tetrastichus) purpureus, Tachardiaephagus tachardiae, and Parechthrodryinus clavicornis have been identified. In addition, 20 predators have been recorded, including significant ones like Eublemma amabilis (white moth), Pseudohypato papulverea (black moth), and Chrysopa sp� (green lacewing). These predators alone contribute to 35-40% of lac crop losses. During different seasons, specific pests like Chrysopa lacciperda and C� madestes pose a substantial threat to lac insects.

div.head: Management
div.p: • Various cultural control techniques such as early inoculation (within 10-15 days), trap cropping, and intercropping with plants like okra and cotton can be employed. • Mechanically, synthetic net container bags with sixty mesh can be used for inoculating brood lac, effectively trapping nymphs while preventing adult predator moths from escaping. • The application of the biopesticide like Bacillus thuringiensis Berliner in the field is an effective method to control Pseudohypatopa pulverea and Eublemma amabilis. • Additionally, ant predators like Solenopsis geminata and Camponotus compressus can be utilized to target the pupae and larvae of these predators.
